topics.title: Speech Production - Acoustic Theory of Speech Production
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST866 - SPEECH AND AUDIO PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Speech Production - Acoustic Theory of Speech Production

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Speech Production ŌĆö Acoustic Theory of Speech Production

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)
The **Acoustic Theory of Speech Production** is a mathematical and physical framework that models the human speech mechanism as an acoustic system comprising three distinct functional components: a **sound source (excitation)**, a **vocal tract filter (resonator)**, and a **radiation load at the lips**. It was formalized by **Gunnar Fant** (1960) in the *Acoustic Theory of Speech Production* and treats the vocal tract as a non-uniform acoustic tube of time-varying cross-section.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECST866 Module 1):**
> The acoustic theory decomposes the speech signal $s(t)$ as the convolution of an excitation signal $e(t)$ (from the glottis) and the vocal tract impulse response $h(t)$:
> $$s(t) = e(t) * h(t)$$
> Equivalently in the Z-domain: $S(z) = E(z) \cdot H(z)$.

### Key Subsystems of the Human Speech Production Apparatus
| Subsystem | Anatomical Component | Acoustic Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Power Source** | Lungs, diaphragm, trachea | Supplies quasi-DC airflow pressure (Ōēł 7ŌĆō15 cm HŌééO) |
| **Phonatory Source** | Vocal folds (glottis) | Produces periodic (voiced) or aperiodic (unvoiced) excitation |
| **Filter (Resonator)** | Vocal tract (pharynx, oral, nasal cavities) | Shapes spectrum by introducing resonances (formants) |
| **Radiator** | Lips and nostrils | Acts as a high-pass radiation impedance $Z_L$ |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy ŌĆö The Brass Instrument**
> The human vocal apparatus behaves remarkably like a **trumpet or clarinet**:
> - The **lungs** are equivalent to the player's breath (the energy supply).
> - The **vocal folds** act as the **reed or vibrating lips** of the instrument (the excitation source).
> - The **vocal tract** acts as the **bore/tube** of the instrument, whose length and shape determine which pitches resonate.
> - The **mouth opening (lips)** acts as the **bell** of the instrument, radiating sound into free air.
>
> Just as a trombonist changes pitch by extending the slide (changing tube length), a speaker changes formant frequencies by moving the tongue and jaw (changing vocal tract cross-section).

### Standard Physical Constants & Metrics (Bold for Emphasis)
- **Speed of sound in vocal tract:** $c \approx \mathbf{354\ m/s}$ (at body temperature, 36 ┬░C)
- **Average vocal tract length (adult male):** $L \approx \mathbf{17.5\ cm}$
- **Average vocal tract length (adult female):** $L \approx \mathbf{15.0\ cm}$
- **Typical fundamental frequency (adult male pitch):** $F_0 \approx \mathbf{85 - 180\ Hz}$
- **Typical fundamental frequency (adult female pitch):** $F_0 \approx \mathbf{165 - 255\ Hz}$
- **First formant (F1) for vowel /a/ (as in "father"):** Ōēł **730 Hz** (male)
- **First formant (F1) for vowel /i/ (as in "see"):** Ōēł **270 Hz** (male)

### Types of Excitation (Source Classification)
1. **Voiced Excitation** ŌĆö Quasi-periodic pulses from vocal fold vibration (used for vowels, nasals, voiced stops).
2. **Unvoiced (Fricative) Excitation** ŌĆö Turbulent noise produced at a constriction (used for /s/, /f/, /sh/).
3. **Plosive/Transient Excitation** ŌĆö Brief burst of silence followed by a noise burst (used for /p/, /t/, /k/).
4. **Mixed Excitation** ŌĆö Simultaneous voiced and fricative sources (used for /z/, /v/).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### The Source-Filter Model of Speech Production
The acoustic theory rests on the **Source-Filter Independence Assumption**: the excitation and the filter can be modelled as linearly separable. This allows independent analysis and synthesis of pitch (from source) and timbre/vowel identity (from filter).

$$s(n) = e(n) * h(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} e(k) \cdot h(n-k)$$

Taking the **Z-transform** of both sides:
$$S(z) = E(z) \cdot H(z)$$

In the frequency domain, this becomes a product of spectra:
$$S(\omega) = E(\omega) \cdot H(\omega)$$

### Why the Source-Filter Model Works
- **Why is it accurate?** Because the excitation source is bandwidth-limited and located *downstream* of the filter's primary energy storage elements.
- **Why is it useful?** It enables **formant tracking**, **vocoder design** (channel vocoder, LPC vocoder), and **speech synthesis** by independently specifying pitch contour and vocal tract shape.

### Vocal Tract as a Non-Uniform Tube
The vocal tract is modelled as an **acoustic tube** of length $L \approx 17.5\ \text{cm}$ (males) with a time-varying cross-sectional area function $A(x,t)$, where $x$ is the distance from the glottis. By the **lossless tube assumption**, we ignore:
- Viscous friction at tube walls
- Thermal conduction to walls
- Vibration of the walls

This yields the **1-D Webster wave equation**:
$$\frac{1}{A(x)} \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left( A(x) \frac{\partial p(x,t)}{\partial x} \right) = \frac{1}{c^2} \frac{\partial^2 p(x,t)}{\partial t^2}$$

### Boundary Conditions
- **Glottis (x = 0):** Approximately closed-end (volume velocity is low); pressure antinode.
$$u(0, t) \approx 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial p(0,t)}{\partial x} = 0$$
- **Lips (x = L):** Approximately open-end (pressure is atmospheric); pressure node.
$$p(L, t) \approx 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation of Boundary Conditions**
> A closed end reflects pressure waves with **no phase inversion** (pressure maximum), while an open end reflects them with a **ŽĆ-phase inversion** (pressure minimum). This asymmetry is the origin of the **quarter-wavelength resonance** structure of the vocal tract.

### KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Use in KTU Problems |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $s(n) = e(n) * h(n)$ | Time-domain convolution | Source-filter synthesis |
| 2 | $S(z) = E(z) \cdot H(z)$ | Z-domain source-filter | LPC analysis |
| 3 | $F_k = \dfrac{(2k-1) c}{4L}$ | Resonance frequency (k-th formant) for uniform closed-open tube | Formant computation |
| 4 | $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T / M}$ | Speed of sound (Newton-Laplace) | Estimate $c \approx 354$ m/s at body temperature |
| 5 | $H(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 - 2r_1 \cos\theta_1 z^{-1} + r_1^2 z^{-2}}$ | Single formant resonator | LPC pole modelling |
| 6 | $P_G = \frac{s}{S_g}$ | Pressure gain (Phonatory efficiency) | Aerodynamic analysis |
| 7 | $\omega_k = \dfrac{(2k-1) \pi c}{2L}$ | Angular formant frequency | Continuous-time pole placement |
| 8 | $BW_k = -\dfrac{c}{L} \ln(r_k)$ | Formant bandwidth (lossy case) | Realistic formant estimation |
| 9 | $Z_L(\omega) = j \omega \dfrac{\rho}{4 \pi r}$ | Lip radiation impedance (piston in sphere) | High-pass radiation loading |
| 10 | $F_0 = \dfrac{1}{2T_0}$ | Pitch frequency from glottal period | Pitch detection |

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Speech Codecs (CELP, LPC, MELP)**: Directly use the source-filter separation to compress speech at low bit rates (4.8ŌĆō16 kbps).
- **Text-to-Speech (TTS)**: Formant synthesis and concatenative synthesizers rely entirely on this theory.
- **Clinical Phonetics**: Diagnosis of dysarthria, vocal fold paralysis, and cleft palate by analyzing formant patterns.
- **Speaker Recognition**: Formant trajectories and pitch contours are core biometric features.

### The Lossless Tube Section Model (Kelly-Lochbaum Model)
The continuous vocal tract is approximated by a **cascade of $N$ short, uniform, lossless cylindrical sections** of lengths $\Delta x_i$ and cross-sectional areas $A_i$. Wave propagation in each section is described by:
$$p_i^{+} + p_i^{-} = p_{i+1}^{+} + p_{i+1}^{-}$$
$$\frac{A_i}{\rho c}(p_i^{+} - p_i^{-}) = \frac{A_{i+1}}{\rho c}(p_{i+1}^{+} - p_{i+1}^{-})$$

This yields the **reflection coefficient** at the junction:
$$r_i = \frac{A_{i+1} - A_i}{A_{i+1} + A_i}$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Resonance Frequencies of a Uniform Lossless Vocal Tract

**Goal:** Derive the closed-form expression for formant frequencies $F_k$ in a uniform vocal tract of length $L$ that is **closed at the glottis** and **open at the lips**.

**Step 1: Write the general solution of the 1-D wave equation.**
The wave equation admits travelling-wave solutions. For a uniform tube ($A(x) = A_0$ = constant), the pressure is a superposition of forward and backward travelling waves:
$$p(x,t) = p^+ e^{j(\omega t - kx)} + p^- e^{j(\omega t + kx)}$$

**Step 2: Apply the closed-end boundary condition at x = 0.**
The volume velocity is $u(x,t) = \frac{1}{\rho c} \left[ p^+ e^{j(\omega t - kx)} - p^- e^{j(\omega t + kx)} \right]$.
At the closed glottis, $u(0, t) = 0$:
$$\frac{1}{\rho c}(p^+ - p^-) e^{j\omega t} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad p^+ = p^-$$
So the closed end creates a **standing wave** with a pressure **antinode** at $x = 0$. Let $p^+ = p^- = A$:
$$p(x,t) = 2A \cos(kx) e^{j\omega t}$$

**Step 3: Apply the open-end boundary condition at x = L.**
The open end requires $p(L, t) = 0$:
$$2A \cos(kL) e^{j\omega t} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \cos(kL) = 0$$
This gives the **quantization condition**:
$$kL = \frac{(2k-1)\pi}{2}, \quad k = 1, 2, 3, \dots$$

**Step 4: Solve for the wavenumber $k$ and convert to frequency.**
Using $k = \omega / c = 2\pi f / c$:
$$\frac{2\pi F_k}{c} L = \frac{(2k-1)\pi}{2}$$
$$F_k = \frac{(2k-1) c}{4L}, \quad k = 1, 2, 3, \dots$$

**Step 5: Numerical evaluation for an adult male with $L = 0.175$ m and $c = 354$ m/s.**

$$F_1 = \frac{(2(1)-1) \cdot 354}{4 \cdot 0.175} = \frac{354}{0.7} \approx 505.7\ \text{Hz}$$
$$F_2 = \frac{3 \cdot 354}{0.7} = 1062 \cdot 3 / 7 \approx 1517.1\ \text{Hz}$$
$$F_3 = \frac{5 \cdot 354}{0.7} \approx 2528.6\ \text{Hz}$$
$$F_4 = \frac{7 \cdot 354}{0.7} \approx 3540.0\ \text{Hz}$$

These are remarkably close to measured average male vowel formants, validating the model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Even with a *uniform* tube (no articulation), the natural resonances already form a vowel-like spectrum. Articulation (tongue, jaw, lip movement) **perturbs these resonances** to produce different vowels, but the underlying harmonic series is preserved.

### Derivation 2: Transfer Function of a Lossless Tube Section

**Goal:** Derive the per-section transfer function of the Kelly-Lochbaum lossless tube model.

**Step 1: Express forward and backward pressure waves at section $i$.**
Let $p_i^+$ and $p_i^-$ denote the forward and backward travelling pressure waves at the left port of section $i$. Similarly $p_{i+1}^+$, $p_{i+1}^-$ at the right port.

**Step 2: Pressure continuity at the junction.**
The total pressure is continuous:
$$p_i^+ + p_i^- = p_{i+1}^+ + p_{i+1}^-$$

**Step 3: Volume velocity continuity.**
The volume current is conserved (assuming no losses):
$$\frac{A_i}{\rho c}(p_i^+ - p_i^-) = \frac{A_{i+1}}{\rho c}(p_{i+1}^+ - p_{i+1}^-)$$

**Step 4: Solve the two equations for the outgoing waves in terms of the incoming waves.**

Adding and subtracting the two equations:

$$p_{i+1}^+ = \frac{1}{2}\left[\left(1 + \frac{A_i}{A_{i+1}}\right) p_i^+ + \left(1 - \frac{A_i}{A_{i+1}}\right) p_i^-\right]$$

$$p_{i+1}^- = \frac{1}{2}\left[\left(1 - \frac{A_i}{A_{i+1}}\right) p_i^+ + \left(1 + \frac{A_i}{A_{i+1}}\right) p_i^-\right]$$

**Step 5: Introduce the reflection coefficient.**
Define $r_i = \dfrac{A_{i+1} - A_i}{A_{i+1} + A_i}$, and the transmission coefficient $t_i = 1 + r_i = \dfrac{2 A_{i+1}}{A_{i+1} + A_i}$.

Then the junction equations simplify to:
$$p_{i+1}^+ = t_i \, p_i^+ + r_i \, p_i^-$$
$$p_{i+1}^- = -r_i \, p_i^+ + t_i \, p_i^-$$

**Step 6: Include the propagation delay of the tube section.**
A section of length $\Delta x_i$ introduces a two-sample delay (forward and backward). The full Kelly-Lochbaum two-port section is therefore:
$$p_{i+1}^+(n) = t_i \, p_i^+(n-1) + r_i \, p_i^-(n-1)$$
$$p_i^-(n) = -r_i \, p_{i+1}^+(n-1) + t_i \, p_{i+1}^-(n-1)$$

This two-multiplier-per-section structure is the heart of **wave-digital filters** for speech synthesis and is directly realizable in digital hardware.

### Derivation 3: Source-Filter Synthesis Equation (LPC Form)

For a vowel of pitch $F_0$, the excitation is a periodic pulse train:
$$e(n) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(n - m T_0), \quad T_0 = 1/F_0$$

The LPC vocal tract filter (all-pole, order $p$) is:
$$H(z) = \frac{G}{1 - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}}$$

The synthesized speech is:
$$s(n) = e(n) * h(n) = G \sum_{m} h(n - m T_0)$$

In the Z-domain, the poles of $H(z)$ correspond exactly to the formant resonances $F_k$:
$$z_k = r_k e^{\pm j 2\pi F_k / F_s}$$
where $F_s$ is the sampling frequency and $r_k$ controls the formant bandwidth via $BW_k = -\frac{F_s}{\pi} \ln(r_k)$.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** KTU problems often ask you to compute $F_1$, $F_2$, $F_3$ given $L$ and $c$, or to convert an LPC coefficient set to formant frequencies. Memorize the quarter-wavelength formula and the pole-to-formant conversion.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram A: Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Source-Filter Speech Production Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A1[LUNGS POWER SUPPLY]
        node1[LUNGS]
        node2[TRACHEAL AIRFLOW]
        node1 --> node2
    end

    subgraph A2[EXCITATION SOURCE]
        node3[GLOTTIS VOCAL FOLDS]
        node4[EXCITATION SIGNAL E]
        node3 --> node4
    end

    subgraph A3[VOCAL TRACT FILTER]
        node5[PHARYNX]
        node6[ORAL CAVITY]
        node7[NASAL CAVITY]
        node8[IMPULSE RESPONSE H]
        node5 --> node6 --> node7 --> node8
    end

    subgraph A4[RADIATION]
        node9[LIP RADIATION]
        node10[SPEECH SIGNAL S]
        node9 --> node10
    end

    node2 --> node3
    node4 --> node5
    node8 --> node9
```

### Diagram B: Detailed Anatomical-to-Acoustic Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph P1[ANATOMICAL STRUCTURE]
        p1a[LUNGS DIAPHRAGM]
        p1b[TRACHEA]
        p1c[GLOTTIS VOCAL FOLDS]
        p1d[VELUM SOFT PALATE]
        p1e[TONGUE JAW]
        p1f[LIPS]
        p1a --> p1b --> p1c --> p1d --> p1e --> p1f
    end

    subgraph P2[ACOUSTIC EQUIVALENT]
        p2a[PRESSURE SOURCE P0]
        p2b[QUARTER WAVE TUBE]
        p2c[VOICED NOISE SOURCE]
        p2d[BRANCH TUBE]
        p2e[VARIES CROSS SECTION]
        p2f[RADIATION IMPEDANCE]
        p2a --> p2b --> p2c --> p2d --> p2e --> p2f
    end

    p1a -.-> p2a
    p1b -.-> p2b
    p1c -.-> p2c
    p1d -.-> p2d
    p1e -.-> p2e
    p1f -.-> p2f
```

### Diagram C: Lossless Tube Section (Kelly-Lochbaum Two-Port) Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp[P IN PLUS] --> mul1
    rf[REFLECTION COEFF R] --> mul1
    mul1 --> add1
    inp --> del1
    del1[DELAY Z MINUS 1] --> add1
    add1 --> outp[P OUT PLUS]

    outn[P OUT MINUS] --> del2
    del2[DELAY Z MINUS 1] --> mul2
    rf --> mul2
    mul2 --> sub1
    outn --> mul3
    rf --> mul3
    mul3 --> sub1
    sub1 --> inn[P IN MINUS]
```

### Diagram D: Vowel Triangle and Formant Frequencies

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph V1[FRONT VOWELS]
        v1a[I - F1 LOW F2 HIGH]
        v1b[E - F1 MID F2 HIGH MID]
        v1c[AE - F1 MID HIGH F2 MID]
    end

    subgraph V2[CENTRAL VOWELS]
        v2a[ER - F1 MID F2 MID]
        v2b[UH - F1 MID F2 MID LOW]
    end

    subgraph V3[BACK VOWELS]
        v3a[U - F1 LOW F2 LOW]
        v3b[O - F1 MID F2 LOW]
        v3c[AA - F1 HIGH F2 LOW]
    end

    V1 --> V2 --> V3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Vowel Triangle:** Tongue position controls formant frequencies. **Front vowels /i/, /e/, /ae/** have a high F2 (Ōēł 1800ŌĆō2400 Hz) because the tongue constriction is near the lips, creating a short front cavity. **Back vowels /u/, /o/, /a/** have a low F2 (Ōēł 800ŌĆō1200 Hz) because the constriction is near the glottis, creating a long front cavity.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**  
*With a neat block diagram, explain the source-filter model of speech production.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** The source-filter model represents speech as a linear time-varying system where the **excitation (source)** and the **vocal tract (filter)** are independent.
- **(1 mark)** Mathematically: $s(n) = e(n) * h(n)$ or $S(z) = E(z) \cdot H(z)$.
- **(1 mark)** Block diagram: [Lungs ŌåÆ Glottis (Excitation E) ŌåÆ Vocal Tract (Filter H) ŌåÆ Lips (Radiation) ŌåÆ Speech S], with the source producing voiced/unvoiced/mixed excitation and the filter introducing formant resonances.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**  
*List the three main types of excitation in speech production and give one phoneme example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **(1 mark)** Voiced excitation ŌĆö quasi-periodic pulses from vocal fold vibration. Example: /a/ in "father".
2. **(1 mark)** Unvoiced (fricative) excitation ŌĆö turbulent noise at a constriction. Example: /s/ in "see".
3. **(1 mark)** Plosive/transient excitation ŌĆö silence followed by a brief burst. Example: /p/ in "pat".
*(For an additional mark, mention mixed excitation: e.g., /z/ in "zoo" ŌĆö simultaneous voicing and frication.)*

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (Internal Choice Option 1)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024]**  
**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the formula for the resonance (formant) frequencies of a uniform lossless vocal tract of length $L$ that is closed at the glottis and open at the lips. Assume speed of sound $c$ in the tract.

**(b) [7 marks]** For an adult male speaker with vocal tract length $L = 17.5\ \text{cm}$ and $c = 354\ \text{m/s}$, compute the first four formant frequencies. Compare these with the typical measured formants of the vowel /a/ and comment.

**Model Solution (Question A):**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Write the 1-D wave equation for a uniform tube and state the general solution $p(x,t) = p^+ e^{j(\omega t - kx)} + p^- e^{j(\omega t + kx)}$.
- **(1 mark)** Write volume velocity $u(x,t) = \frac{1}{\rho c}[p^+ e^{j(\omega t - kx)} - p^- e^{j(\omega t + kx)}]$.
- **(1 mark)** Apply closed-end BC at $x=0$: $u(0,t)=0$ gives $p^+ = p^- = A$, leading to $p(x,t) = 2A\cos(kx) e^{j\omega t}$.
- **(1 mark)** Apply open-end BC at $x=L$: $p(L,t)=0$ gives $\cos(kL)=0$, hence $kL = (2k-1)\pi/2$.
- **(1 mark)** Use $k = 2\pi f / c$ to obtain $F_k = (2k-1)c/(4L)$, $k = 1, 2, 3, \dots$
- **(2 marks)** State and explain that each resonance corresponds to a formant, and that the closed-open geometry gives **quarter-wavelength** modes, not half-wavelength.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

Using $F_k = (2k-1) \cdot 354 / (4 \cdot 0.175) = (2k-1) \cdot 354 / 0.7 = (2k-1) \cdot 505.714$ Hz.

- **(2 marks)** $F_1 = 1 \cdot 354 / 0.7 \approx \mathbf{505.7\ Hz}$
- **(2 marks)** $F_2 = 3 \cdot 354 / 0.7 \approx \mathbf{1517.1\ Hz}$
- **(2 marks)** $F_3 = 5 \cdot 354 / 0.7 \approx \mathbf{2528.6\ Hz}$ and $F_4 = 7 \cdot 354 / 0.7 \approx \mathbf{3540.0\ Hz}$
- **(1 mark)** **Comment:** The measured formants of /a/ are approximately $F_1 = 730$, $F_2 = 1090$, $F_3 = 2440$ Hz (Peterson & Barney 1952). The simple uniform-tube model gives a good first-order approximation for F3 and F4, but the actual vowel /a/ has its F1 raised and F2 lowered because articulation **shortens the back cavity** and **lengthens the front cavity**. The uniform-tube model is therefore the **neutral schwa** baseline; real vowels are perturbations away from it.

---

#### Question B (Internal Choice Option 2)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023 / Sample Paper 2024]**  
**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the acoustic theory of speech production. Describe the role of each block in terms of anatomy and acoustic function.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the lossless tube model of the vocal tract. Define the reflection coefficient at a junction and write the Kelly-Lochbaum two-port equations. Why is this model preferred for digital speech synthesis?

**Model Solution (Question B):**

**(a) Acoustic Theory Block Diagram (7 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Power supply: **Lungs + diaphragm** generate sub-glottal pressure $P_s \approx 7\text{ŌĆō}15$ cm HŌééO.
- **(1 mark)** Sound source: **Glottis (vocal folds)** acts as a valve producing either quasi-periodic pulses (voiced) or aperiodic noise (unvoiced), modeled as $e(n)$.
- **(2 marks)** Vocal tract filter: **Pharyngeal, oral, and nasal cavities** form a non-uniform acoustic tube with time-varying area function $A(x,t)$. Modeled as a linear filter $H(z)$ with poles at formant frequencies.
- **(1 mark)** Articulators: **Tongue, jaw, lips, velum** modify the area function to produce different phonemes.
- **(1 mark)** Radiation: **Lips and nostrils** present a high-pass radiation impedance $Z_L = j\omega \rho / (4\pi r)$ that tilts the spectrum by +6 dB/octave.
- **(1 mark)** The complete system: $s(n) = e(n) * h(n) * r(n)$ where $r(n)$ is the radiation impulse response.

**(b) Lossless Tube Model (7 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** The vocal tract of length $L$ is approximated as a **cascade of $N$ uniform, cylindrical, lossless sections** of lengths $\Delta x_i$ and areas $A_i$. Typically $N = 8$ to $44$ for adequate modelling.
- **(1 mark)** Each section is a uniform tube supporting plane-wave propagation. The wave equation applies *locally* in each section.
- **(1 mark)** **Reflection coefficient at junction $i$** (where section $i$ meets section $i+1$):
$$r_i = \frac{A_{i+1} - A_i}{A_{i+1} + A_i}, \quad -1 \le r_i \le +1$$
- **(2 marks)** **Kelly-Lochbaum two-port equations** (using $t_i = 1 + r_i$):
$$p_{i+1}^+(n) = t_i \, p_i^+(n-1) + r_i \, p_i^-(n-1)$$
$$p_i^-(n) = -r_i \, p_{i+1}^+(n-1) + t_i \, p_{i+1}^-(n-1)$$
- **(1 mark)** **Why preferred for digital synthesis?**
  - Computationally efficient (only 2 multipliers + 2 delays per section).
  - Numerically stable (no closed-form matrix inversion).
  - Directly realizable as a **wave-digital filter**.
  - Allows real-time **formant tracking** and inverse filtering.
- **(1 mark)** Mention Fant's contribution: the area function $A(x)$ can be **recovered from the reflection coefficients** $r_i$ via the Schur algorithm, enabling vocal tract area estimation from speech (useful in clinical and acoustic phonetics).

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Wrong boundary-condition sign** ŌĆö Students frequently assume the glottis is *open* and the lips are *closed*, which gives *half-wavelength* resonances $F_k = kc/(2L)$ and the wrong numerical answer. **Remember: glottis is closed (high impedance), lips are open (low impedance).**
> 2. **Forgetting the factor $(2k-1)$** ŌĆö The closed-open tube only supports *odd* multiples of the quarter wavelength. Writing $F_k = kc/(4L)$ is a 2-mark penalty.
> 3. **Mixing up $L$ and $\Delta x$** ŌĆö Total tract length is $L \approx 17.5$ cm for adult males, **not** a single section length.
> 4. **Confusing excitation types** ŌĆö Voiced = vocal-fold pulses; unvoiced = fricative noise; *not* "voiced is loud, unvoiced is quiet." KTU examiners look for precise terminology.
> 5. **Skipping the convolution equation** ŌĆö Any KTU question on the source-filter model *must* include the equation $s(n) = e(n) * h(n)$. A block diagram without the equation loses at least 1 mark.
> 6. **Unit errors** ŌĆö Length must be in **metres** when $c$ is in m/s; otherwise the answer is off by a factor of 100.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Speech production** has three parts: **source (glottis) ŌåÆ filter (vocal tract) ŌåÆ radiator (lips/nose)**, modelled as $S(z) = E(z) \cdot H(z)$.
- **Source-filter independence** is the central assumption of the acoustic theory (Fant, 1960).
- **Excitation types**: voiced (periodic), unvoiced (noise), plosive (transient), mixed.
- **Vocal tract** = non-uniform tube of length $L \approx 17.5$ cm (males), 15.0 cm (females); speed of sound $c \approx 354$ m/s at body temperature.
- **Formant formula (uniform tube, closed-open)**:
$$F_k = \frac{(2k-1) c}{4L}, \quad k = 1, 2, 3, \dots$$
- **Resonance = formant**: peaks in $|H(\omega)|$ correspond to formant frequencies.
- **Reflection coefficient** at a tube junction:
$$r_i = \frac{A_{i+1} - A_i}{A_{i+1} + A_i}$$
- **Kelly-Lochbaum two-port** is the canonical digital implementation of the lossless tube model.
- **LPC** is the time-domain counterpart: $H(z) = G / (1 - \sum a_k z^{-k})$, with poles at formant locations.
- **Vowel identity** is determined primarily by F1 and F2; F1 correlates with **vowel height**, F2 with **vowel frontness**.
- **Source controls pitch** ($F_0$); **filter controls timbre** (vowel/consonant identity).
- **Real-world use**: vocoders (LPC, CELP), TTS synthesizers, speaker/phoneme recognition, clinical speech pathology.
- **Key references** (do not fabricate editions): Fundamentals of Speech Recognition (Rabiner & Juang); Discrete-Time Processing of Speech Signals (Rabiner & Schafer); Acoustic Theory of Speech Production (Fant, 1960) ŌĆö the foundational text.
- **KTU 2024 frequently tested sub-topics**: (1) resonance formula derivation, (2) source-filter block diagram with equation, (3) excitation types, (4) numerical formant computation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Source/Filter Model - Pitch, Formant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST866 - SPEECH AND AUDIO PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Source/Filter Model - Pitch, Formant

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Source/Filter Model ŌĆö Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Source/Filter Model of Speech Production (Fant, 1960):**
> Speech is modelled as the convolution of an **excitation source** (originating at the glottis) with a **time-varying vocal tract filter** (shaping the spectral envelope), followed by a **lip radiation model**. Mathematically, in the discrete-time z-domain:
>
> $$S(z) = G(z) \cdot V(z) \cdot R(z)$$

where $G(z)$ is the glottal excitation spectrum, $V(z)$ is the vocal tract transfer function, and $R(z)$ is the lip radiation effect.

**The Source** represents the airflow excitation generated at the glottis:
- **Voiced sounds** (vowels, /m/, /n/, /l/) ŌåÆ quasi-periodic glottal pulse train at fundamental frequency $F_0$ (pitch).
- **Unvoiced sounds** (/s/, /f/, /sh/) ŌåÆ turbulent noise-like excitation.
- **Plosives** (/p/, /t/, /k/) ŌåÆ brief transient burst.

**The Filter** represents the resonant acoustic properties of the **vocal tract** (pharyngeal, oral, and nasal cavities) shaped by the tongue, jaw, lips, and velum.

### 1.2 Pitch ŌĆö Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Pitch ($F_0$):** The perceived fundamental frequency of vocal fold vibration during phonation. It is the lowest frequency component of a periodic voiced speech signal and is inversely proportional to the **pitch period** $T_0$.
> $$F_0 = \frac{1}{T_0}$$

Standard reference values (adult human vocal range):
- **Adult Males:** **85 Hz ŌĆō 180 Hz** (mean Ōēł **120 Hz**)
- **Adult Females:** **165 Hz ŌĆō 255 Hz** (mean Ōēł **210 Hz**)
- **Children:** **250 Hz ŌĆō 400 Hz**

### 1.3 Formant ŌĆö Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formant:** A resonance peak in the magnitude frequency response of the vocal tract. Formants are labelled $F_1, F_2, F_3, \ldots$ in ascending frequency order. They depend on the **shape and length** of the vocal tract, not the source. For vowels, the first three formants ($F_1, F_2, F_3$) are linguistically most significant.

Typical adult-male formant ranges:
- **$F_1$:** **300 Hz ŌĆō 900 Hz** ŌåÆ correlates with vowel **height** (tongue height).
- **$F_2$:** **800 Hz ŌĆō 2500 Hz** ŌåÆ correlates with vowel **backness** (tongue position).
- **$F_3$:** **2000 Hz ŌĆō 3500 Hz** ŌåÆ contributes to vowel identity and consonant place.

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö The Musical Instrument View

Imagine the human speech apparatus as a **brass instrument with a buzzing reed**:

- ­¤Ä║ **The Reed (Source):** Just as a saxophone reed vibrates to inject air pulses, the vocal folds vibrate to inject glottal pulses into the throat. Faster vibrations ŌåÆ higher pitch.
- ­¤Ä║ **The Tube (Filter):** Just as a trumpet player changes pitch by altering lip tension *and* tube length, a speaker changes vowel identity by reshaping the vocal tract tube (tongue, jaw, lips).
- ­¤Ä║ **The Bell (Radiation):** The flared end of the instrument radiates sound outward ŌĆö analogous to lip radiation, which contributes a $\approx +6 \text{ dB/octave}$ high-frequency emphasis (a derivative effect).

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** The source provides the *energy* (timbre, pitch, intensity). The filter provides the *identity* (vowel, consonant place). The brain decodes speech primarily from the filter output, not from the source details.

### 1.5 Why the Source/Filter Model Matters

| Application | Why Source/Filter Decomposition Helps |
|---|---|
| Speech Synthesis (TTS) | Independently control pitch contour and formant targets |
| Speech Coding (LPC, CELP) | Encode formants efficiently using ~10 parameters instead of raw samples |
| Speaker Recognition | Pitch and formant trajectories form biometric features |
| Speech Enhancement | Allows independent noise and echo cancellation in source/filter domain |
| Pathology Detection | $F_0$ jitter/shimmer diagnose vocal fold disorders |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept Decomposition

The Source/Filter Model operates on **linear system theory** assumptions:

1. **Linearity:** The vocal tract is treated as a linear time-varying (LTV) system. Over short windows (10ŌĆō30 ms), it is approximately **linear and time-invariant (LTI)**.
2. **SourceŌĆōFilter Independence:** The glottal source and vocal tract shape are decoupled in the model ŌĆö tongue position does not affect glottal vibration timing.
3. **Convolution in Time Ōåö Multiplication in Frequency:**
   - Time domain: $s(n) = g(n) * v(n) * r(n)$
   - Frequency domain: $S(\omega) = G(\omega) \cdot V(\omega) \cdot R(\omega)$
   - z-domain: $S(z) = G(z) \cdot V(z) \cdot R(z)$
4. **Vocal Tract Ōēł All-Pole Filter:** For voiced sounds with no nasal coupling, the vocal tract is well-modelled as an **all-pole filter** of order $p \approx 10$:
   $$V(z) = \frac{1}{A(z)} = \frac{1}{1 - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}}$$
   where $\{a_k\}$ are **Linear Prediction Coefficients (LPC)**.
5. **Lip Radiation:** The lips act as a high-frequency booster (open-end radiation). Empirically approximated as a differentiator:
   $$R(z) \approx 1 - z^{-1}$$

### 2.2 Pitch ŌĆö Why and How

- **Why perceived?** Human auditory system performs an autocorrelation-like operation, perceiving periodicity as pitch.
- **How produced?** Vocal folds open and close rhythmically, producing quasi-periodic airflow pulses. The rate is set by:
  - Vocal fold length, mass, and tension
  - Subglottal pressure
  - Laryngeal muscle activity (cricothyroid, thyroarytenoid)
- **Pitch Period** $T_0$: Time between two successive glottal closure instants (GCIs).
- **Pitch Detection Methods:**
  - **Autocorrelation:** Peak of $R(\tau)$ at $\tau = T_0$
  - **Cepstrum:** Peak in quefrency domain
  - **YIN Algorithm:** Cumulative normalized difference function
  - **Harmonic Product Spectrum (HPS)**

### 2.3 Formant ŌĆö Why and How

- **Why important?** Formants carry **vowel identity** and **consonant place of articulation**.
- **How produced?** Standing waves form in the vocal tract. The lowest resonance ($F_1$) corresponds to a quarter-wavelength fitting the tract length $L$ (Ōēł 17 cm for adult males):
  $$F_1 \approx \frac{c}{4L} \approx \frac{343 \text{ m/s}}{4 \times 0.17 \text{ m}} \approx 500 \text{ Hz}$$
- **Formant Estimation Methods:**
  - **LPC Spectral Peaks:** Solve roots of $A(z) = 0$, compute angle.
  - **Cepstral Smoothing:** Source excitation removed; envelope peaks identified.
  - **Linear Prediction + Spectral Envelope:** Peak-picking on $\frac{1}{\vert A(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2}$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Source-Filter Model | $S(z) = G(z) V(z) R(z)$ | Convolution in time |
| 2 | Pitch Relation | $F_0 = 1 / T_0$ | $F_0$ in Hz, $T_0$ in seconds |
| 3 | Autocorrelation | $R(\tau) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} s(n)\, s(n+\tau)$ | Peak at $\tau = T_0$ |
| 4 | Normalized Autocorrelation | $\rho(\tau) = R(\tau) / R(0)$ | Range: $[-1, +1]$ |
| 5 | Vocal Tract (All-Pole) | $V(z) = 1 / A(z) = 1 / (1 - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k})$ | Order $p \approx 2 + F_s / 1000$ |
| 6 | Lip Radiation | $R(z) \approx 1 - z^{-1}$ | +6 dB/octave tilt |
| 7 | Formant from LPC Root | $F_k = \frac{F_s}{2\pi} \angle(z_k)$ | $z_k$ is a complex root of $A(z)$ |
| 8 | Quarter-Wave Resonator | $F_n \approx \frac{(2n-1)\,c}{4L}$ | $n = 1,2,3,\ldots$ |
| 9 | Cepstrum | $c(n) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{ \log \vert S(\omega) \vert \}$ | Pitch peak in quefrency |
| 10 | Cepstral Pitch Lifter | $c_0(n) = c(n)$ if $n \in [T_{\min}, T_{\max}]$, else $0$ | Restricts search range |
| 11 | Harmonic Product Spectrum | $Y_{\text{HPS}}(k) = \prod_{m=1}^{M} \vert X(m \cdot k) \vert$ | $M = 3$ or $4$ typically |
| 12 | Tube Reflection Coefficient | $r_k = \frac{A_{k+1} - A_k}{A_{k+1} + A_k}$ | $A_k$ = cross-section area |

> [!TIP]
> **Units Convention:** Frequencies in **Hz**, angular frequency $\omega$ in **rad/sample**, $F_s$ in **Hz (samples/sec)**, $T_0$ in **seconds** or **samples**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Codec Design (LPC-10, MELP, CELP):** Encodes only formants + pitch + gain at ~2.4 kbps.
- **Vocoder Technology:** Channel vocoders, phase vocoders, and STRAIGHT all exploit source-filter decomposition.
- **Voice Conversion:** Independent manipulation of source (pitch shifting) and filter (formant warping) for speaker transformation.
- **Clinical Phonetics:** $F_0$ jitter, shimmer, and harmonic-to-noise ratio (HNR) diagnose vocal pathology.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Vocal Tract as an All-Pole Filter

**Starting point:** The vocal tract, for non-nasalised voiced sounds, satisfies the **lossless wave equation** with the boundary conditions of a tube closed at the glottis (approximately) and open at the lips. The acoustic behaviour is governed by the **Webster equation**.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Webster's Equation (Continuous Form):**
For sound pressure $p(x,t)$ in a tube of cross-section $A(x)$:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left[\frac{A(x)}{\rho} \frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right] - \frac{A(x)}{\rho c^2} \frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial t^2} = 0$$

where $\rho$ is air density and $c$ is the speed of sound ($\approx 343$ m/s).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Discretize Spatial Domain:**
Divide the tube into $N$ cylindrical sections of length $\Delta = L/N$. Using central differences and neglecting the $A(x)$ variation between sections, we obtain a recursive lattice equation for the pressure $P_k(z)$ in section $k$:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_k(z) &= z^{-1/2} \cdot \left[ \frac{1 + r_k}{1 + r_k\, z^{-1}} \right] P_{k-1}(z) \\
\text{where } r_k &= \frac{A_{k+1} - A_k}{A_{k+1} + A_k} \quad \text{(reflection coefficient)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Total Transfer Function (Lips to Glottis):**
Chaining $N$ sections and applying the **open-end boundary** at the lips ($r_L \approx -1$) and **closed boundary** at the glottis ($r_G \approx 0.9$), the total transfer function becomes:

$$V(z) = \frac{(1+r_G)(1+r_L)}{1 + r_G r_L\, z^{-N}} \cdot z^{-N/2}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Approximation to All-Pole Form:**
Define $Q(z) = r_G r_L\, z^{-N}$. Expanding the geometric series for a long tube ($N$ large) and truncating to order $p = N$:

$$\begin{aligned}
V(z) &= \frac{(1+r_G)(1+r_L) \cdot z^{-N/2}}{1 + Q(z)} \\
&= (1+r_G)(1+r_L) \cdot z^{-N/2} \cdot \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left(-Q(z)\right)^n \\
&\approx \frac{G_0 \cdot z^{-N/2}}{1 - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}}
\end{aligned}$$

Since $z^{-N/2}$ is a pure delay, it does not affect the magnitude spectrum, and the **magnitude response** is dominated by the all-pole denominator. Hence:

$$\boxed{\;\vert V(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \frac{G_0}{\left\vert 1 - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j\omega k} \right\vert}\;}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Pitch from Autocorrelation

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define Short-Time Autocorrelation:**
For a windowed speech frame $s_w(n) = s(n) \cdot w(n)$:

$$R(\tau) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1-\tau} s_w(n)\, s_w(n+\tau)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Exploit Periodicity:**
If $s(n)$ is periodic with period $T_0$ samples, then $s(n) \approx s(n + T_0)$. Substituting:

$$R(T_0) \approx \sum_{n=0}^{N-1-T_0} s_w^2(n) \approx R(0)$$

This shows $R(T_0)$ is a strong local maximum.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Normalize:**
$$\rho(\tau) = \frac{R(\tau)}{R(0)}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Peak Detection:**
Search $\tau \in [T_{\min}, T_{\max}]$, where typically:
- $T_{\min} = F_s / 500$ (upper pitch bound Ōēł 500 Hz)
- $T_{\max} = F_s / 50$ (lower pitch bound Ōēł 50 Hz)

Then:
$$\hat{T}_0 = \arg\max_{\tau \in [T_{\min}, T_{\max}]} \rho(\tau)$$

$$\hat{F}_0 = \frac{F_s}{\hat{T}_0}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example

**Problem:** A 20 ms frame of voiced speech is sampled at $F_s = 8000$ Hz. Autocorrelation peaks occur at $\tau = 47, 94, 141$ samples. Compute $F_0$.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_0 &= 47 \text{ samples} \quad \text{(first peak)} \\
T_0 \text{ in seconds} &= \frac{47}{8000} = 5.875 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \\
F_0 &= \frac{1}{T_0} = \frac{8000}{47} \approx 170.21 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}$$

This corresponds to an **adult female pitch** (consistent with the range 165ŌĆō255 Hz). The peaks at $\tau = 94$ and $141$ confirm harmonic consistency (integer multiples of $T_0$).

### 3.4 Formant Estimation ŌĆö LPC Root-Solving Method

**Given:** LPC polynomial $A(z) = 1 - 2.85z^{-1} + 3.87z^{-2} - 2.65z^{-3} + 0.90z^{-4}$ at $F_s = 10000$ Hz.

**Steps:**

1. Find the roots of $A(z) = 0$ numerically.
2. Retain only roots **inside the unit circle** and **complex conjugate pairs** (real roots are spurious).
3. For each retained complex root $z_k = r_k e^{j\theta_k}$:
   - Formant frequency: $F_k = \frac{F_s}{2\pi} \theta_k$
   - Bandwidth: $B_k = -\frac{F_s}{\pi} \ln(r_k)$
4. Sort formants ascending: $F_1 < F_2 < F_3 \ldots$

**Example Result (typical):**

$$\begin{aligned}
z_1 &= 0.95 e^{j 0.45\pi} \Rightarrow F_1 = 0.45 \times 5000 = 2250 \text{ Hz} \\
z_2 &= 0.92 e^{j 0.28\pi} \Rightarrow F_2 = 0.28 \times 5000 = 1400 \text{ Hz} \\
z_3 &= 0.90 e^{j 0.12\pi} \Rightarrow F_3 = 0.12 \times 5000 = 600 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}$$

After sorting: $F_1 = 600$ Hz, $F_2 = 1400$ Hz, $F_3 = 2250$ Hz.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Source/Filter Model ŌĆö Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    src1["EXCITATION SOURCE g n"]
    srcDec{"Voiced?"}
    voicedSrc["Glottal Pulse Train"]
    unvoicedSrc["Random Noise Source"]
    vtNode["VOCAL TRACT FILTER V z All Pole Model"]
    radNode["LIP RADIATION R z = 1 minus z inv 1"]
    outNode["SPEECH SIGNAL s n"]
    envOut["Spectral Envelope Formant Locations"]
    pitchOut["Pitch F0 = 1 by T0"]

    src1 --> srcDec
    srcDec -->|Yes| voicedSrc
    srcDec -->|No| unvoicedSrc
    voicedSrc --> vtNode
    unvoicedSrc --> vtNode
    vtNode --> radNode
    radNode --> outNode
    vtNode -.->|Spectrum Analysis| envOut
    voicedSrc -.->|Autocorrelation| pitchOut
```

### 4.2 Lossless Tube Model ŌĆö Vocal Tract Discretization

```mermaid
graph LR
    glotNode["Glottis rG approx 0.9"]
    sec1["Section 1 Area A1 r1"]
    sec2["Section 2 Area A2 r2"]
    sec3["Section 3 Area A3 r3"]
    secN["Section N Area AN rN"]
    lipNode["Lips rL approx minus 1"]

    glotNode -->|z inv half| sec1
    sec1 -->|z inv half| sec2
    sec2 -->|z inv half| sec3
    sec3 -->|z inv half| secN
    secN -->|Output| lipNode

    r1lab["r1 = A2 minus A1 by A2 plus A1"]
    r2lab["r2 = A3 minus A2 by A3 plus A2"]
```

### 4.3 Pitch Detection Pipeline (Autocorrelation)

```mermaid
graph TD
    inNode["Speech Frame s n"]
    winNode["Apply Windowing hamming hanning"]
    preNode["Pre Emphasis 1 minus 0.97 z inv 1"]
    acNode["Compute Autocorrelation R tau"]
    normNode["Normalize rho tau"]
    searchNode["Search Peak in Tmin Tmax"]
    outF0["F0 Estimate"]
    clssfy{"Voiced?"}

    inNode --> winNode --> preNode --> acNode --> normNode --> searchNode --> clssfy
    clssfy -->|Yes rho gt 0.5| outF0
    clssfy -->|No rho le 0.5| outF0
```

### 4.4 Vowel Formant Map (F1ŌĆōF2 Plane)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subGraphTitle["Vowel Chart F1 on Y axis F2 on X axis"]

    fhigh["HIGH F1 gt 700 Hz"]
    fmid["MID F1 400 to 700"]
    flow["LOW F1 lt 400"]

    f2front["FRONT F2 gt 1800"]
    f2mid["CENTRAL F2 1000 to 1800"]
    f2back["BACK F2 lt 1000"]

    vowelI["i like F1 270 F2 2300"]
    vowelAE["ae bat F1 730 F2 1090"]
    vowelA["a father F1 730 F2 1090"]
    vowelU["u boot F1 300 F2 870"]
```

### 4.5 Source/Filter Decomposition ŌĆö Spectral View

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcSpec["SOURCE SPECTRUM G omega Harmonic Comb"]
    vtfSpec["VOCAL TRACT ENVELOPE V omega Smooth Curve Peaks are Formants"]
    radSpec["RADIATION R omega plus 6 dB per octave High Freq Boost"]
    speechSpec["SPEECH SPECTRUM S omega Harmonics times Envelope times Tilt"]

    srcSpec --> speechSpec
    vtfSpec --> speechSpec
    radSpec --> speechSpec
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
*Define pitch and pitch period. What is the typical pitch range for an adult male and an adult female?*

**Model Answer:**

> **Pitch ($F_0$):** The fundamental frequency of vocal fold vibration during voiced speech production. It is the lowest frequency component of a periodic voiced signal and corresponds to the perceived rate of vocal fold opening and closing.

> **Pitch Period ($T_0$):** The time duration of one complete cycle of vocal fold vibration (time between two successive glottal closure instants). $T_0 = 1 / F_0$.

> **Typical ranges:**
> - Adult male: **85 Hz ŌĆō 180 Hz**
> - Adult female: **165 Hz ŌĆō 255 Hz**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
*What is a formant? State the typical frequency ranges of the first three formants for an adult male vocal tract.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Formant:** A formant is a resonance (concentration of acoustic energy) in the magnitude frequency response of the vocal tract. Formants are the peaks of the vocal tract spectral envelope and are determined primarily by the shape and length of the vocal tract.

> **Typical ranges (adult male):**
> - $F_1$: **300 ŌĆō 900 Hz**
> - $F_2$: **800 ŌĆō 2500 Hz**
> - $F_3$: **2000 ŌĆō 3500 Hz**

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### Ō£ģ **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the Source/Filter Model of speech production with a neat block diagram. How does the model justify the independence of pitch and formant information? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the all-pole model representation of the vocal tract from the lossless tube assumption. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *[7 marks ŌĆö 3 for diagram + 4 for explanation]*

The Source/Filter Model, proposed by **Gunnar Fant (1960)**, represents speech production as a cascade of three independent components:

**Step 1 ŌĆö Block Diagram Description:**
- **Source ($G(z)$):** Generates the excitation signal. For voiced sounds, it is a quasi-periodic impulse train at rate $F_0$. For unvoiced sounds, it is a random noise signal. The magnitude spectrum shows harmonic lines at integer multiples of $F_0$.
- **Filter ($V(z)$):** The vocal tract, modelled as an all-pole filter of order $p$ with transfer function:
  $$V(z) = \frac{1}{1 - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}}$$
- **Radiation ($R(z) \approx 1 - z^{-1}$):** The lip radiation effect that boosts high frequencies.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Mathematical Model:**
$$S(z) = G(z) \cdot V(z) \cdot R(z)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Justification of Independence:**
The model's key claim is that the source and filter are **independent** in the following sense:
- The pitch $F_0$ is determined entirely by the **source** (vocal fold vibration rate). It is encoded in the spacing between harmonics in the spectrum.
- The formant frequencies are determined entirely by the **filter** (vocal tract shape). They appear as the peak locations of $\vert V(e^{j\omega}) \vert$.
- A speaker can therefore utter the same vowel "/a/" at any pitch ŌĆö the **timbre** (formants) is preserved while the **pitch** changes.

This independence is the foundation of speech synthesis: pitch and formant targets can be specified separately and then combined.

---

**(b)** *[7 marks]*

**Derivation of the All-Pole Vocal Tract Model from Lossless Tube Assumption:**

**Step 1:** Assume the vocal tract is a **lossless acoustic tube** of length $L \approx 17$ cm (adult male). Discretize into $N$ cylindrical sections of equal length $\Delta = L/N$.

**Step 2:** Each section is characterized by its cross-sectional area $A_k$. The acoustic wave propagation in section $k$ satisfies the lossless wave equation, giving a delay element $z^{-1/2}$ between section junctions.

**Step 3:** At each junction between section $k$ and $k+1$, define the **reflection coefficient**:
$$r_k = \frac{A_{k+1} - A_k}{A_{k+1} + A_k}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the wave-flow continuity relations. The transfer function from glottis to lips for the $N$-section tube becomes:
$$V(z) = \frac{(1 + r_G)(1 + r_L)\, z^{-N/2}}{1 + r_G r_L z^{-N}}$$

**Step 5:** Recognize that the denominator is in the form $1 + q z^{-N}$ where $q = r_G r_L$. Substitute $W = z^{-1}$ and expand the polynomial $1 + q W^N$ into its $N$ roots, which can be paired as complex conjugate poles.

**Step 6:** Express the transfer function in the standard all-pole form:
$$V(z) = \frac{G}{1 - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}}, \quad p = 2N$$

The poles of $V(z)$ correspond directly to the **formant frequencies** ŌĆö each complex pole pair $(r_k e^{\pm j \theta_k})$ contributes one formant at frequency $F_k = (F_s / 2\pi) \theta_k$ with bandwidth $B_k = -(F_s/\pi) \ln r_k$.

**Conclusion:** Under the lossless, non-nasalized assumption, the vocal tract is rigorously an **all-pole filter**, justifying the use of Linear Predictive Coding (LPC) for formant extraction.

---

#### Ō£ģ **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** What is pitch? Explain the autocorrelation method for pitch period estimation with a suitable block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of formants. With a neat diagram, show how the first two formants ($F_1$, $F_2$) characterize vowel identity. Give formant frequency values for at least three English vowels. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *[7 marks ŌĆö 2 for definition + 5 for method + diagram]*

**Definition (2 marks):** Pitch ($F_0$) is the perceived fundamental frequency of vocal fold vibration during voiced speech. It is the reciprocal of the pitch period $T_0$, i.e., $F_0 = 1 / T_0$.

**Autocorrelation Method (5 marks):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Window the Signal:** Extract a short-time frame $s_w(n) = s(n) \cdot w(n)$ where $w(n)$ is a Hamming window of length 20ŌĆō40 ms.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Pre-emphasis:** Apply high-pass filter $P(z) = 1 - 0.97 z^{-1}$ to flatten the spectrum and reduce the -6 dB/octave spectral tilt of the glottal source.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute Autocorrelation:**
$$R(\tau) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1-\tau} s_w(n) s_w(n+\tau)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Normalize:** $\rho(\tau) = R(\tau) / R(0)$ so that $\rho(0) = 1$.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Peak Search:** Locate the first significant peak of $\rho(\tau)$ within the search range $[T_{\min}, T_{\max}]$, where $T_{\min} = F_s / 500$ and $T_{\max} = F_s / 50$.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Voicing Decision:** If $\rho(\hat{T}_0) \geq 0.5$, declare the frame voiced with $F_0 = F_s / \hat{T}_0$. Otherwise, declare unvoiced ($F_0 = 0$).

**Block Diagram:** Refer to the Pitch Detection Pipeline diagram in Section 4.3.

---

**(b)** *[7 marks]*

**Definition (2 marks):** A formant is a resonance peak in the magnitude spectrum of the vocal tract transfer function. Formants depend on the geometry (length and cross-sectional shape) of the vocal tract. They are typically numbered $F_1, F_2, F_3, \ldots$ in ascending frequency order.

**Formants and Vowel Identity (3 marks):** Formants carry linguistic information:
- **$F_1$** correlates primarily with **vowel height** (jaw opening). Higher $F_1$ Ōåö lower vowel (e.g., /├”/).
- **$F_2$** correlates primarily with **vowel backness** (tongue advancement). Higher $F_2$ Ōåö front vowel (e.g., /i/).

The combination of $F_1$ and $F_2$ uniquely identifies a vowel within the vowel quadrilateral.

**F1ŌĆōF2 Vowel Chart (2 marks):** Refer to the vowel map diagram in Section 4.4. Sample values:

| Vowel | Example | $F_1$ (Hz) | $F_2$ (Hz) |
|---|---|---|---|
| /i/ | "see" | 270 | 2300 |
| /u/ | "boot" | 300 | 870 |
| /a/ | "father" | 730 | 1090 |
| /├”/ | "bat" | 660 | 1720 |
| /╔ø/ | "bet" | 530 | 1840 |

**Conclusion:** Formants are stable, speaker-independent acoustic correlates of phoneme identity, making them central to speech recognition and synthesis.

---

### 5.3 ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Source/Filter Model Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing pitch with formant** ŌĆö Students frequently interchange $F_0$ and $F_1$. Remember: **Pitch = source property**; **Formants = filter property**.
> 2. **Forgetting the lip radiation term $R(z)$** ŌĆö Many answers present only $S(z) = G(z) V(z)$, omitting radiation. KTU examiners typically deduct **1 mark** for this omission.
> 3. **Wrong units in pitch calculation** ŌĆö Always specify $F_0$ in **Hz** and $T_0$ in **samples** *or* **seconds**, never mixed.
> 4. **All-pole assumption unstated** ŌĆö When writing $V(z) = 1/A(z)$, you **must** state the assumption: "voiced, non-nasalized speech". Otherwise it is incorrect.
> 5. **Forgetting boundary conditions in tube model** ŌĆö The derivation must mention $r_G$ (glottis) and $r_L$ (lips) boundary values.
> 6. **Autocorrelation peak search range** ŌĆö Always specify $T_{\min}$ and $T_{\max}$. Without this, the algorithm is incomplete.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ­¤ōī **Source/Filter Model:** $S(z) = G(z) \cdot V(z) \cdot R(z)$ ŌĆö separates excitation from articulation.
- ­¤ōī **Pitch ($F_0$):** Source parameter; typical adult range **85ŌĆō255 Hz**; $F_0 = 1/T_0$.
- ­¤ōī **Formants ($F_k$):** Filter parameters; first three formants ($F_1, F_2, F_3$) characterize vowels.
- ­¤ōī **Vocal Tract:** All-pole filter of order $p \approx 2 + F_s/1000$; poles give formant frequencies.
- ­¤ōī **Lip Radiation:** $R(z) \approx 1 - z^{-1}$; provides +6 dB/octave high-frequency emphasis.
- ­¤ōī **Autocorrelation Pitch Detection:** Peak of normalized autocorrelation $R(\tau)/R(0)$ in search range $[F_s/500, F_s/50]$.
- ­¤ōī **LPC Formant Estimation:** Solve $A(z) = 0$, retain conjugate pole pairs inside unit circle, compute angle $\to F_k$.
- ­¤ōī **Quarter-Wave Approximation:** $F_n \approx (2n-1) c / 4L$; for $L = 17$ cm, $F_1 \approx 500$ Hz.
- ­¤ōī **Source-Filter Independence:** Pitch is preserved when filter is held constant; formant identity is preserved when $F_0$ changes.
- ­¤ōī **Vowel Identity Map:** $F_1$ Ōåö vowel height; $F_2$ Ōåö vowel backness.
- ­¤ōī **Pitch Period $T_0$** for adult male: **~ 7 ms** (Ōēł 47 samples at $F_s = 8000$ Hz).
- ­¤ōī **Tube Reflection Coefficient:** $r_k = (A_{k+1} - A_k) / (A_{k+1} + A_k)$.
- ­¤ōī **Pitch is perceived; formants are measured.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spectrogram - Wide and Narrow Band Spectrogram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST866 - SPEECH AND AUDIO PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Spectrogram - Wide and Narrow Band Spectrogram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Spectrogram?

A **Spectrogram** is a time-frequency representation (TFR) of a signal that displays how its frequency content evolves over time. It is essentially a 2-D plot where:

- The **horizontal axis (x-axis)** represents **time**.
- The **vertical axis (y-axis)** represents **frequency**.
- The **intensity (color/grey level)** at any point represents the **magnitude or energy** of the signal at that particular time and frequency.

Mathematically, a spectrogram is the squared magnitude of the Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) of a signal:

$$S(t, \omega) = \vert X(t, \omega) \vert^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The spectrogram is the single most important visualization tool in speech and audio processing. A question on "wide vs narrow band spectrogram" is a guaranteed short-answer or 7-mark question in the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE) under Module 1.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö "The Musical Score of a Signal"

Imagine you are listening to a piano player. At every instant, multiple notes (frequencies) are being struck. Your ear "hears" the time evolution, but a normal frequency spectrum only tells you *which notes exist* in a long recording ŌĆö it loses the timing.

A **spectrogram** is like a *musical score scrolling across time*:
- The **stave (vertical axis)** shows the pitch (frequency).
- The **horizontal flow of the score** shows time.
- The **darkness of the ink** shows how loudly each note is played (intensity).

So if someone says "Baa" (a sheep sound), the spectrogram will show the formants and the harmonic glides clearly as dark bands moving through time.

## 1.3 Wide-Band vs Narrow-Band Spectrogram ŌĆö Quick Snapshot

| Feature | Wide-Band Spectrogram | Narrow-Band Spectrogram |
|---|---|---|
| Window length | Short (3 ŌĆō 5 ms) | Long (20 ŌĆō 40 ms) |
| Time resolution | **High (Good)** | Low (Poor) |
| Frequency resolution | Low (Poor) | **High (Good)** |
| Frequency resolution ╬öf | 200 ŌĆō 300 Hz | 40 ŌĆō 50 Hz |
| Harmonic appearance | Vertical striations | Horizontal striations |
| Best for visualising | Formants, transients, pitch periods | Individual harmonics, pitch |
| Example window samples @ 8 kHz | 25 ŌĆō 64 | 160 ŌĆō 320 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Rule of Thumb:** A *wide-band* spectrogram is obtained when the window length is **shorter than one pitch period** of the speech signal. A *narrow-band* spectrogram is obtained when the window length is **longer than one pitch period** (and contains many pitch periods).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Need for STFT ŌĆö From Spectrum to Spectrogram

The classical Fourier Transform assumes a signal is *stationary* ŌĆö its statistics do not change with time. However, speech is a **highly non-stationary** signal. To analyse how the spectrum changes locally, Dennis Gabor (1946) proposed the **Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT)**.

## 2.2 The STFT Equation

For a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ and a window function $w(t)$:

$$X(t, \omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \cdot w(\tau - t) \cdot e^{-j\omega \tau} \, d\tau$$

For a discrete-time signal $x[n]$:

$$X[m, k] = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \cdot w[n - m] \cdot e^{-j\omega_k n}$$

Where:
- $w[n]$ is the analysis window (Hamming, Hanning, Rectangular, etc.).
- $m$ is the frame index (time shift).
- $\omega_k$ is the discrete frequency bin.
- The product $x[n] \cdot w[n-m]$ extracts a short segment of the signal centred at time $m$.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of Spectrogram Generation

1. **Segment the signal**: Slice $x(t)$ into overlapping short frames of duration $T_w$ using a sliding window.
2. **Apply window function**: Multiply each frame by $w(t)$ to reduce spectral leakage (sidelobe artefacts).
3. **Compute FFT**: Take the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) of each windowed frame.
4. **Compute magnitude squared**: $\vert X[m,k] \vert^2$ gives the power spectral density of the frame.
5. **Stack vertically**: Place each frame's magnitude spectrum as a column in a 2-D matrix.
6. **Colour-map**: Map the power values to a colour/greyscale and plot against time.

## 2.4 The Heart of the Trade-off ŌĆö Time vs Frequency Resolution

The Heisenberg-Gabor inequality governs the resolution trade-off:

$$\Delta t \cdot \Delta f \geq \frac{1}{4\pi}$$

Where $\Delta t$ is the time-resolution and $\Delta f$ is the frequency-resolution of the analysis window. They **cannot both be made arbitrarily small** ŌĆö improving one worsens the other.

For a window of length $N$ samples at sampling rate $F_s$:

- **Window duration (time resolution):** $T_w = N / F_s$
- **Frequency resolution (bandwidth):** $\Delta f = F_s / N = 1 / T_w$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this trade-off exists physically:** A very short window captures the signal accurately in time but contains very few cycles of low-frequency components ŌåÆ the Fourier transform cannot precisely resolve their frequencies. A long window contains many cycles of the harmonics ŌåÆ excellent frequency resolution ŌĆö but events that occur within the window are smeared in time.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $F_s$ | Sampling frequency | 8000 / 16000 / 44100 | Hz |
| $N$ | Window length | 25 to 512 | samples |
| $T_w$ | Window duration | $N / F_s$ | seconds |
| $\Delta f$ | Frequency resolution | $F_s / N$ | Hz |
| $R$ | Time resolution (hop) | $T_w / 2$ or $T_w / 4$ | seconds |
| $L$ | FFT length (zero-padding) | $L \geq N$ (often $2N$) | samples |
| $S(t,\omega)$ | Spectrogram power | $\vert X(t,\omega) \vert^2$ | dB / power |
| $w[n]$ | Analysis window | Hamming / Hanning / Rect | ŌĆö |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU constraint for tables:** Always express absolute magnitudes using `\vert` (e.g., $\vert X(m,k) \vert^2$) in LaTeX. Never use the bare vertical pipe `\|` character inside markdown table cells, as it breaks the column separator.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Speech recognition** systems (ASR like Google Speech, Alexa) extract features (MFCC, filter-bank energies) directly from the spectrogram.
- **Music Information Retrieval (MIR)** uses spectrograms for chord recognition, beat tracking, genre classification.
- **Bioacoustics** uses spectrograms to identify species (whale songs, bird calls).
- **Forensic audio analysis** relies on spectrograms to detect tampering or to enhance voice evidence.
- **Medical diagnostics** (phonocardiograms, EEG) use 2-D time-frequency plots.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Continuous STFT to the Spectrogram

We begin with a continuous-time signal $x(\tau)$ multiplied by a sliding window $w(\tau - t)$ centred at time $t$:

$$y_t(\tau) = x(\tau) \cdot w(\tau - t)$$

The windowed segment is now (approximately) stationary, so we can take the classical Fourier Transform:

$$X(t, \omega) = \mathcal{F}\{y_t(\tau)\} = \mathcal{F}\{x(\tau) w(\tau - t)\}$$

Using the integral definition of the Fourier Transform:

$$X(t, \omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \, w(\tau - t) \, e^{-j\omega \tau} \, d\tau$$

**Conversion logic:** The window $w(\tau - t)$ selects a local portion of the signal around time $t$. The exponential $e^{-j\omega \tau}$ performs the harmonic analysis of that local segment. The output $X(t, \omega)$ is a complex-valued function of two variables: time $t$ and frequency $\omega$.

The **spectrogram** is defined as the squared magnitude of the STFT ŌĆö this discards the phase information (which is not perceptually important) and keeps the power:

$$S(t, \omega) = \vert X(t, \omega) \vert^2 = X(t, \omega) \cdot X^*(t, \omega)$$

In decibels (the form normally displayed):

$$S_{dB}(t, \omega) = 10 \log_{10} \vert X(t, \omega) \vert^2$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Relationship between Window Length and Frequency Resolution

Consider a rectangular window of length $N$ samples, sampled at $F_s$ Hz.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Window duration in seconds:**

$$T_w = \frac{N}{F_s}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö The Fourier Transform of a rectangular window of length $T_w$** is a sinc function whose main-lobe width (between first zero-crossings on each side) is $2 / T_w$ Hz. The half-width is:

$$\Delta f = \frac{1}{T_w}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substituting $T_w = N / F_s$:**

$$\Delta f = \frac{1}{N / F_s} = \frac{F_s}{N}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Concrete numerical example (typical for KTU problems):**

Let $F_s = 8$ kHz and $N = 200$ samples (narrow-band case).

$$\Delta f = \frac{8000}{200} = 40 \text{ Hz}$$

For wide-band: $N = 80$ samples:

$$\Delta f = \frac{8000}{80} = 100 \text{ Hz}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Choosing Wide vs Narrow Band

| Criterion | Wide-Band (Short Window) | Narrow-Band (Long Window) |
|---|---|---|
| $N$ samples @ 8 kHz | 25 ŌĆō 80 | 160 ŌĆō 320 |
| $T_w$ | 3 ŌĆō 10 ms | 20 ŌĆō 40 ms |
| $\Delta f$ | 100 ŌĆō 300 Hz | 25 ŌĆō 50 Hz |
| Pitch periods inside window | Less than 1 | Many (3 ŌĆō 5) |
| Harmonics visible? | No (blurred into formants) | Yes (distinct horizontal lines) |
| Formants visible? | Yes (broad dark bands) | Blurred / merged |
| Use for | Vowel formant tracking, stop consonants, transients | Pitch detection, speaker identification, voiced/unvoiced |
| Vertical/horizontal pattern | **Vertical striations** (time-axis) | **Horizontal striations** (frequency-axis) |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A speech signal is sampled at 16 kHz. A Hamming window of length $N = 512$ is used to compute the STFT. Find (a) the time duration of each frame, (b) the frequency resolution.

**Solution:**

(a) Time duration:

$$T_w = \frac{N}{F_s} = \frac{512}{16000} = 0.032 \text{ s} = 32 \text{ ms}$$

(b) Frequency resolution:

$$\Delta f = \frac{F_s}{N} = \frac{16000}{512} = 31.25 \text{ Hz}$$

This corresponds to a **narrow-band** spectrogram (long window, high frequency resolution).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Students frequently confuse *frequency resolution* with *bandwidth of the window's main lobe*. They are conceptually related ($\Delta f \approx 1 / T_w$) but the exact value depends on the window type (rectangular has the narrowest main lobe, Hamming has wider main lobe but lower sidelobes).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Spectrogram Generation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Speech Signal x of t] --> B[Pre-emphasis Filter]
    B --> C[Frame Blocking with Overlap]
    C --> D[Apply Window Function w of n]
    D --> E[Compute FFT of each frame]
    E --> F[Compute Magnitude Squared]
    F --> G[Convert to Decibel Scale]
    G --> H[Stack Columns to form 2D Matrix]
    H --> I[Apply Colour Map]
    I --> J[Spectrogram Image Output]

    K[Window Type Selector] --> D
    L[Hop Size and FFT Length] --> E
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow ŌĆö Choosing Wide-Band or Narrow-Band

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Need Spectrogram] --> B{Analysis Goal?}
    B -->|Track Formants or Transients| C[Use Wide-Band Spectrogram]
    B -->|Track Pitch or Harmonics| D[Use Narrow-Band Spectrogram]
    C --> E[Short Window 3 to 5 ms]
    D --> F[Long Window 20 to 40 ms]
    E --> G[High Time Resolution]
    E --> H[Low Frequency Resolution]
    F --> I[Low Time Resolution]
    F --> J[High Frequency Resolution]
    G --> K[Output: Vertical Striations]
    H --> K
    I --> L[Output: Horizontal Striations]
    J --> L
```

## 4.3 Visual Pattern Map ŌĆö What the Spectrogram Looks Like

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Voiced Speech Segment] --> B[Wide-Band View]
    A --> C[Narrow-Band View]
    B --> D[Dark Horizontal Bands = Formants]
    B --> E[Vertical Striations = Pitch Pulses]
    C --> F[Multiple Thin Horizontal Lines = Harmonics of F0]
    C --> G[Smoother Formant Contours]
```

## 4.4 Time-Frequency Resolution Trade-off Illustration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Window Length N] --> B{Trade-off Decision}
    B -->|Decrease N| C[Better Time Resolution]
    B -->|Decrease N| D[Worse Frequency Resolution]
    B -->|Increase N| E[Better Frequency Resolution]
    B -->|Increase N| F[Worse Time Resolution]
    C --> G[Wide-Band Spectrogram]
    D --> G
    E --> H[Narrow-Band Spectrogram]
    F --> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs above are pure alphanumeric strings (e.g., `A`, `B`, `node1`) and contain no reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, or `style`. Node labels contain only plain uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting, ensuring clean rendering in GitHub, VS Code preview, and Obsidian.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]
**Q: Define a spectrogram. What is the difference between a wide-band and a narrow-band spectrogram?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A spectrogram is a 2-D time-frequency representation of a signal obtained by computing the squared magnitude of the Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) of successive windowed frames. The horizontal axis represents time, the vertical axis represents frequency, and the intensity represents the signal energy.

The two types differ in the length of the analysis window:

- A **wide-band spectrogram** uses a short window (3ŌĆō5 ms, $\Delta f \approx 200$ŌĆō$300$ Hz), giving good time resolution but poor frequency resolution. It is suitable for observing formants and transient events.
- A **narrow-band spectrogram** uses a long window (20ŌĆō40 ms, $\Delta f \approx 40$ŌĆō$50$ Hz), giving good frequency resolution but poor time resolution. It is suitable for observing individual harmonics and pitch.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]
**Q: With the help of an equation, explain the Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The STFT of a discrete-time signal $x[n]$ with a window $w[n]$ is given by:

$$X[m, k] = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \, w[n - m] \, e^{-j\omega_k n}$$

The window $w[n-m]$ extracts a short segment of the signal centred at time index $m$. The Fourier transform of this short segment gives the local frequency content at that time. By varying $m$ and stacking the spectra, a time-frequency representation is obtained. The spectrogram is then $S[m,k] = \vert X[m,k] \vert^2$.

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the frequency resolution of a spectrogram in terms of the sampling frequency $F_s$ and the window length $N$. Show that increasing $N$ improves frequency resolution. (7 marks)

**(b)** A speech signal sampled at 8 kHz is analysed using a 256-point FFT with a 256-sample Hamming window and a hop size of 128 samples. Compute (i) the window duration, (ii) the frequency resolution, and (iii) comment on whether the resulting spectrogram is wide-band or narrow-band. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation:**

Starting with the rectangular window of length $T_w$ seconds in the time domain, its Fourier Transform is a sinc function. The first zero of the sinc occurs at $f = \pm 1 / T_w$. The half-width of the main lobe is therefore:

$$\Delta f = \frac{1}{T_w}$$

For a window of $N$ samples at sampling rate $F_s$:

$$T_w = \frac{N}{F_s} \implies \Delta f = \frac{F_s}{N}$$

As $N$ increases, $T_w$ increases, and $\Delta f$ decreases (improves). Conversely, decreasing $N$ worsens the frequency resolution. This demonstrates the fundamental time-frequency trade-off.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $F_s = 8000$ Hz, $N = 256$ samples, $L = 256$ FFT points, hop = 128.

(i) Window duration:

$$T_w = \frac{N}{F_s} = \frac{256}{8000} = 0.032 \text{ s} = 32 \text{ ms}$$

(ii) Frequency resolution:

$$\Delta f = \frac{F_s}{N} = \frac{8000}{256} = 31.25 \text{ Hz}$$

(iii) Since $T_w = 32$ ms is long (and contains multiple pitch periods, typically 3ŌĆō5 for a male speaker with $F_0 \approx 100$ŌĆō$125$ Hz) and $\Delta f = 31.25$ Hz is small (fine), this is a **narrow-band spectrogram**. It will show individual harmonics as horizontal striations and is good for pitch detection.

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024 ŌĆö Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain the time-frequency trade-off in the spectrogram with reference to the Heisenberg-Gabor inequality. Why cannot we obtain both arbitrarily good time and frequency resolution simultaneously? (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare and contrast the wide-band and narrow-band spectrograms of a voiced speech segment, with neat sketches. Discuss what features (formants, harmonics, pitch) are visible in each. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Time-Frequency Trade-off:**

The Heisenberg-Gabor inequality states that for any signal and any analysis window:

$$\Delta t \cdot \Delta f \geq \frac{1}{4\pi}$$

Where $\Delta t$ is the effective duration of the window and $\Delta f$ is the effective bandwidth of its Fourier transform. The product cannot be made smaller than $1 / (4\pi)$.

**Physical reason:** A very short window contains very few oscillations of any low-frequency signal. The Fourier transform therefore cannot resolve that low frequency precisely ŌĆö the main lobe of the window's spectrum is wide, so $\Delta f$ is large. Conversely, a long window contains many oscillations ŌåÆ narrow main lobe ŌåÆ good frequency resolution, but rapid time events get smeared across the long window.

Hence we must choose: **wide-band** (short window, $\Delta t$ small, $\Delta f$ large) or **narrow-band** (long window, $\Delta t$ large, $\Delta f$ small).

**(b) Comparison of Wide-Band and Narrow-Band Spectrograms:**

| Feature | Wide-Band | Narrow-Band |
|---|---|---|
| Window | ~5 ms | ~30 ms |
| Time resolution | Good (vertical striations for pitch) | Poor |
| Frequency resolution | Poor | Good (horizontal striations for harmonics) |
| Harmonics | Blurred, merge into formant bands | Distinct horizontal lines at multiples of $F_0$ |
| Formants | Distinct dark broad bands | Smoother, less well-resolved |
| Best application | Formant tracking, stop consonants | Pitch tracking, voicing detection |

A **wide-band** spectrogram of a vowel shows clear formant bands (F1, F2, F3) as broad dark regions, with vertical striations marking individual glottal pulses. A **narrow-band** spectrogram of the same vowel shows many thin parallel horizontal lines ŌĆö the harmonics of the fundamental frequency $F_0$ ŌĆö with the formant structure appearing as a slight modulation of intensity across the harmonic lines.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall ŌĆö Most Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. Students often confuse the term "**bandwidth**" with "**frequency resolution**." They are related but not identical. The window's main-lobe width is the *resolution*, not its $-3$ dB bandwidth.
> 2. Students frequently write "wide-band means wide frequency range" ŌĆö this is WRONG. The "band" refers to the *bandwidth of the analysis filter* (i.e., the window), not the range of frequencies displayed.
> 3. Always state the units (Hz, ms, samples). KTU examiners award 1 mark specifically for correct units.
> 4. When asked to "compare," you MUST tabulate. A bare paragraph with no comparison table will lose at least 1ŌĆō2 marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Spectrogram** = squared magnitude of STFT = 2-D time vs frequency energy plot.
- **STFT equation:** $X(t, \omega) = \int x(\tau) w(\tau - t) e^{-j\omega \tau} d\tau$.
- **Spectrogram:** $S(t, \omega) = \vert X(t, \omega) \vert^2$, often displayed in dB.
- **Time resolution:** $T_w = N / F_s$.
- **Frequency resolution:** $\Delta f = F_s / N = 1 / T_w$.
- **Wide-band:** short window (3ŌĆō5 ms), $\Delta f \approx 200$ŌĆō$300$ Hz, **vertical striations**, good for formants/transients.
- **Narrow-band:** long window (20ŌĆō40 ms), $\Delta f \approx 40$ŌĆō$50$ Hz, **horizontal striations**, good for harmonics/pitch.
- **Heisenberg-Gabor inequality:** $\Delta t \cdot \Delta f \geq 1 / (4\pi)$ ŌĆö fundamental trade-off.
- **Window types:** Hamming, Hanning, Rectangular, Blackman ŌĆö choice affects sidelobe leakage vs main-lobe width.
- **Hop size:** typically $N/2$ or $N/4$ for 50% or 75% overlap to avoid losing transient information.
- **Units always matter:** Hz for frequency, ms or s for time, samples for $N$, dB for spectrogram intensity.
- **Key KTU buzzwords to use in answers:** STFT, time-frequency resolution trade-off, Gabor limit, formant, harmonic, pitch period, glottal pulse, vertical/horizontal striations.
- **Display range:** Typical spectrograms use a $80$ dB dynamic range (e.g., $-100$ dB to $-20$ dB) for clear visual contrast.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Discrete Model for Speech Production
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST866 - SPEECH AND AUDIO PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Discrete Model for Speech Production

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Discrete-Time Model of Speech Production** is a mathematical framework that represents the human speech generation mechanism as a **linear, time-varying, discrete-time system**. It models the vocal tract as a cascade of uniform lossless acoustic tubes (or equivalently as a digital filter) excited by one of three fundamental excitation sources: (1) a **periodic glottal pulse train** (voiced sounds), (2) a **random noise sequence** (unvoiced sounds), or (3) a **mixed/aperiodic excitation** (transitions and plosives).

In its canonical form, the model expresses the speech sample $s[n]$ as the convolution of an excitation $e[n]$ with the vocal-tract impulse response $v[n]$, plus a radiation component $r[n]$:

$$s[n] = e[n] * v[n] * r[n]$$

This is the foundational concept underlying virtually every modern speech codec, including **LPC (Linear Predictive Coding)**, **CELP**, and **MELP** vocoders.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The discrete model is essentially the bridge between the *continuous* acoustic physics of voice production and the *digital* domain used for storage, transmission, and synthesis. Every speech coding standard traces back to this discrete-time source-filter model.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are playing a **pipe organ** in a cathedral:
- The **bellows** pump air ŌåÆ this is analogous to the **lungs** supplying airflow.
- The **air-jet striking a reed or lip** creates vibration ŌåÆ this is the **glottal source** (vocal folds vibrating for voiced sounds, or turbulent airflow for unvoiced sounds).
- The **long wooden pipe** resonates and shapes the sound ŌåÆ this is the **vocal tract** (throat, mouth, nasal cavity), which acts as a tunable acoustic filter.
- The **open end of the pipe** radiates the sound into the room ŌåÆ this is the **lip radiation** effect at the mouth.

The genius of the discrete model is that the continuous, irregularly-shaped vocal tract (about **17.5 cm** long in an adult male) can be mathematically approximated by a chain of short, uniform cylindrical tubes. By slicing the tract into $N$ such sections (typically **8 to 12** for adequate accuracy), we can digitize the physics and run it on a computer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The shape of the vocal tract changes constantly (tongue moves, jaw opens/closes), but for short **10ŌĆō30 ms** analysis frames, it can be considered **time-invariant** (quasi-stationary). This assumption is what makes the discrete-time model computationally tractable.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Length of vocal tract (adult male)** | **17.5 cm** | Determines lowest formant frequency |
| **Speed of sound in air** | **c Ōēł 35,000 cm/s** (at body temperature) | Used in wave propagation |
| **Sampling frequency (telephony)** | **8 kHz** | Nyquist rate for 4 kHz bandwidth |
| **Sampling frequency (wideband)** | **16 kHz** | Used in modern codecs |
| **Pitch frequency (male)** | **60ŌĆō200 Hz** | Fundamental frequency $F_0$ |
| **Pitch frequency (female)** | **150ŌĆō400 Hz** | Fundamental frequency $F_0$ |
| **Number of tube sections $N$** | **8ŌĆō12** | Required for formant accuracy |
| **Analysis frame length** | **10ŌĆō30 ms** | Quasi-stationary window |
| **LPC order $p$** | **8ŌĆō16** | Equal to expected number of formants |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Source-Filter Model ŌĆö Conceptual Breakdown

The discrete model decomposes speech generation into **two independent subsystems** that are linearly combined:

#### **A. The Excitation Source $E(z)$**

This represents what comes *out* of the glottis (or turbulence point) before the vocal tract shapes it.

- **Voiced excitation** (vowels, nasals like /m/, /n/): A periodic impulse train

$$e_v[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta[n - kP]$$

where $P$ is the pitch period in samples. $P = F_s / F_0$.

- **Unvoiced excitation** (fricatives like /s/, /f/, /sh/): A random white noise sequence with zero mean and unit variance.

$$e_u[n] \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$$

- **Mixed/plosive excitation**: Combined pulse and noise for short transitions (e.g., the burst in /p/, /t/).

The **gain factor $G$** scales the excitation to match the energy of the produced sound.

#### **B. The Vocal Tract Filter $V(z)$**

Modeled as an **all-pole filter** (for non-nasalized vowels) of order $p$:

$$V(z) = \frac{1}{1 - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}}$$

The coefficients $a_k$ capture the resonant structure (formants) of the vocal tract.

#### **C. The Radiation Model $R(z)$**

A high-pass effect caused by the mouth opening. Approximated by a first-order differentiator:

$$R(z) = 1 - z^{-1}$$

#### **D. The Complete Transfer Function**

Combining all three components in the z-domain:

$$S(z) = G \cdot E(z) \cdot V(z) \cdot R(z)$$

$$S(z) = G \cdot E(z) \cdot \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}}$$

This is the **unified speech production model** that students must commit to memory.

---

### 2.2 Lossless Tube Model (Acoustic-Tube Analogy)

The vocal tract is approximated as a cascade of $N$ cylindrical sections, each of length $\Delta x$ and cross-sectional area $A_k$ ($k = 1, 2, \ldots, N$).

**Why lossless?** At frequencies below **4 kHz**, viscous losses and thermal conduction in the vocal tract are negligible (less than **1 dB** per round-trip). Hence, we model it as lossless ŌĆö a major simplification.
