The wave propagation in such tubes leads to **forward-traveling** $u_k^+(n)$ and **backward-traveling** $u_k^-(n)$ volume velocity waves, governed by:

$$u_k^+(n) = (1 + r_k)\, u_{k-1}^+(n-1) + r_k\, u_k^-(n)$$

$$u_k^-(n) = -r_k\, u_{k-1}^+(n-1) + (1 - r_k)\, u_k^-(n)$$

where $r_k$ is the **reflection coefficient** at junction $k$.

#### **Reflection Coefficient Formula**

$$r_k = \frac{A_{k+1} - A_k}{A_{k+1} + A_k}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability Constraint:** For the model to be physically realizable (and stable), every reflection coefficient must satisfy $\vert r_k \vert < 1$ for all $k$. This is equivalent to requiring all tube areas to be strictly positive.

---

### 2.3 Linear Prediction (LPC) Connection

The lattice filter coefficients $k_k$ (called **PARCOR coefficients**) are *identical* to the reflection coefficients $r_k$ of the tube model. They are computed by the **Levinson-Durbin recursion** from the autocorrelation of the speech frame.

The LPC analysis yields a direct prediction of the current sample from past $p$ samples:

$$s[n] = \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, s[n-k] + G\, e[n]$$

This is the *linear predictor* form, and the prediction error $e[n]$ is precisely the residual excitation fed to the model.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula Name | Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Complete source-filter model | $S(z) = G\, E(z)\, V(z)\, R(z)$ | Master equation for speech generation |
| Vocal tract transfer function | $V(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}}$ | All-pole filter of order $p$ |
| Radiation model | $R(z) = 1 - z^{-1}$ | Lip-radiation high-pass effect |
| Reflection coefficient | $r_k = \dfrac{A_{k+1} - A_k}{A_{k+1} + A_k}$ | Ratio of area mismatch |
| Stability condition | $\vert r_k \vert < 1$ for all $k$ | Required for physical realizability |
| Pitch period relation | $P = F_s / F_0$ | Samples per pitch period |
| Formant-to-area inversion | $F_i \approx \dfrac{(2i-1)\, c}{4\, L}$ | Approx. for uniform tube of length $L$ |
| LPC prediction | $\hat{s}[n] = \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, s[n-k]$ | Linear predictor |
| LPC residual | $e[n] = s[n] - \hat{s}[n]$ | Excitation signal |
| Total tube length | $L = N \cdot \Delta x$ | Geometric constraint |
| Wave travel time per section | $T = \Delta x / c$ | Time for wave to cross one section |
| Two-way travel across section | $2T = 2\Delta x / c$ | Used in lattice update equations |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Speech codecs (LPC-10, MELP, CELP)**: Transmit only the parameters $\{G, F_0, a_k, V/UV\}$ at low bit rates (e.g., **2.4 kbps** for LPC-10e).
- **Text-to-Speech (TTS) synthesis**: Build $V(z)$ and $R(z)$ filters from articulatory rules and excite them with synthetic $e[n]$.
- **Speaker identification & verification**: Formant trajectories and pitch contours are speaker-specific biomarkers.
- **Biomedical applications**: Vocal-tract models diagnose speech disorders, cleft-palate assessment, and post-stroke rehabilitation.
- **Noise-robust ASR front-ends**: Modern Whisper and Kaldi pipelines use neural approximations of this classical model.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Lossless Tube Wave Equations

We start from the **continuity equation** (conservation of mass) and the **momentum equation** (Newton's second law) for sound in a lossless tube of cross-section $A$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define volume velocity and pressure.**

Let $u(x, t)$ = volume velocity (volume flow rate) and $p(x, t)$ = acoustic pressure at position $x$ and time $t$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Write the continuity equation.**

The rate of change of mass in a small slab $[x, x+dx]$ equals the net mass flow in:

$$-A \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = \rho \frac{\partial u}{\partial t}$$

where $\rho$ is air density.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Write the momentum (force) equation.**

A pressure gradient accelerates the air column:

$$-\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = \frac{\rho}{A} \frac{\partial u}{\partial t}$$

Wait ŌĆö combining correctly: the **Webster horn equation** for uniform tube simplifies to two coupled first-order equations:

$$-\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = \frac{\rho}{A} \frac{\partial u}{\partial t}$$

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = -\frac{A}{\rho c^2} \frac{\partial p}{\partial t}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Separate variables into forward and backward waves.**

Assume solutions of the form $p(x,t) = f(x-ct) + g(x+ct)$ and $u(x,t) = \frac{A}{\rho c}\bigl[f(x-ct) - g(x+ct)\bigr]$.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Discretize into $N$ tube sections.**

Let the $k$-th section be bounded by junctions $k-1$ and $k$ with areas $A_k$. Define the **characteristic impedance** of section $k$ as $Z_k = \dfrac{\rho c}{A_k}$.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Define forward and backward pressure waves.**

At the input of section $k$:
- Forward wave: $p_k^+(n) = $ pressure of right-going wave at time $n$ entering junction $k$.
- Backward wave: $p_k^-(n) = $ pressure of left-going wave at time $n$ entering junction $k$.

**Step 7 ŌĆö Apply boundary condition at junction $k$.**

Continuity of pressure and volume velocity at the junction gives:

$$p_k^+ + p_k^- = p_{k+1}^+ + p_{k+1}^-$$

$$\frac{p_k^+ - p_k^-}{Z_k} = \frac{p_{k+1}^+ - p_{k+1}^-}{Z_{k+1}}$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Solve for the transmitted and reflected waves.**

Solving the two linear equations for $p_{k+1}^+$ (transmitted) and $p_k^-$ (reflected) yields:

$$p_{k+1}^+(n) = (1 + r_k)\, p_k^+(n-1) + r_k\, p_k^-(n)$$

$$p_k^-(n) = -r_k\, p_k^+(n-1) + (1 - r_k)\, p_k^-(n)$$

where the reflection coefficient is:

$$r_k = \frac{Z_k - Z_{k+1}}{Z_k + Z_{k+1}} = \frac{A_{k+1} - A_k}{A_{k+1} + A_k}$$

(The one-sample delay on the forward wave accounts for the wave's travel time through section $k$.)

This completes the derivation of the **lattice filter update equations** used in the discrete speech production model.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Formant Frequencies for a Uniform Tube

For a uniform tube of length $L$ with one end closed (glottis) and one end open (lips), the **boundary conditions** are:

- Pressure maximum, velocity zero at the closed end ŌåÆ $p(0) = $ max, $\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x}\vert_{x=0} = 0$.
- Pressure zero, velocity maximum at the open end ŌåÆ $p(L) = 0$.

The general solution of the wave equation is $p(x,t) = \cos\!\bigl(\frac{(2k-1)\pi x}{2L}\bigr) \cos(\omega t)$.

**Applying $p(L) = 0$:**

$$\cos\!\left(\frac{(2k-1)\pi L}{2L}\right) = \cos\!\left(\frac{(2k-1)\pi}{2}\right) = 0 \checkmark$$

This is automatically satisfied for odd harmonics.

**Quantization of resonant frequencies:**

$$F_k = \frac{(2k-1)\, c}{4\, L}, \quad k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

**Numerical example for adult male** ($L = 17.5$ cm, $c = 35{,}000$ cm/s):

$$F_1 = \frac{1 \cdot 35000}{4 \cdot 17.5} = 500 \text{ Hz}$$

$$F_2 = \frac{3 \cdot 35000}{4 \cdot 17.5} = 1500 \text{ Hz}$$

$$F_3 = \frac{5 \cdot 35000}{4 \cdot 17.5} = 2500 \text{ Hz}$$

These match the observed **average formant frequencies** for the neutral vowel /╔Ö/ (schwa) ŌĆö a powerful validation of the tube model.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of LPC Prediction Error

Starting from the all-pole model:

$$S(z)\bigl(1 - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}\bigr) = G\, E(z)\, R(z)$$

Taking the inverse z-transform and ignoring $R(z)$ for simplicity:

$$s[n] - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, s[n-k] = G\, e[n]$$

Rearranging to isolate the predicted sample:

$$s[n] = \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, s[n-k] + G\, e[n]$$

Define the **linear prediction** of $s[n]$:

$$\hat{s}[n] = \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, s[n-k]$$

Then the **prediction error** (residual) is:

$$e_{\text{res}}[n] = s[n] - \hat{s}[n] = G\, e[n]$$

The LPC coefficients $a_k$ are chosen to **minimize the mean-squared error**:

$$E = \sum_n \bigl(s[n] - \hat{s}[n]\bigr)^2 = \sum_n \bigl(s[n] - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, s[n-k]\bigr)^2$$

Setting $\dfrac{\partial E}{\partial a_k} = 0$ for each $k$ yields the **YuleŌĆōWalker equations**:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{p} a_j\, R[\vert k - j \vert] = R[k], \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, p$$

where $R[m]$ is the short-time autocorrelation of the speech frame. These are solved efficiently by the **LevinsonŌĆōDurbin recursion** in $O(p^2)$ time, which simultaneously produces the lattice (PARCOR) coefficients $k_1, k_2, \ldots, k_p$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram ŌĆö Complete Discrete Speech Production Model

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the high-level flow of the source-filter model, from physiological sources to radiated speech.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[LUNGS - Air Pressure Source] --> B[GLOTTIS - Excitation Generator]
    B --> C{Voiced or Unvoiced Decision}
    C -->|Voiced Vowels Nasals| D[Periodic Pulse Train eV]
    C -->|Unvoiced Fricatives| E[Random Noise eU]
    C -->|Plosives Mixed| F[Pulse Plus Noise]
    D --> G[Gain Amplifier G]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Vocal Tract Filter Vz All Pole]
    H --> I[Lip Radiation R equals 1 minus zinv]
    I --> J[Output Speech Signal sn]
    H -. Formants F1 F2 F3 .-> K[Resonance Frequencies]
    A -. Subglottal Pressure .-> B
```

---

### 4.2 Lattice Filter Structure (Inside the Vocal Tract)

This Mermaid diagram shows the **multi-stage lattice filter** that implements the lossless tube model. Each stage corresponds to one tube section.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Input from Glottis] --> S1[Stage 1 k1]
    S1 --> S2[Stage 2 k2]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3 k3]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4 k4]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5 k5]
    S5 --> S6[Stage 6 k6]
    S6 --> S7[Stage 7 k7]
    S7 --> S8[Stage 8 k8]
    S8 --> OUT[Output to Lips]
    S1 -. Reflected r1 .-> REF1[Reflected Wave 1]
    S2 -. Reflected r2 .-> REF2[Reflected Wave 2]
    S3 -. Reflected r3 .-> REF3[Reflected Wave 3]
    S4 -. Reflected r4 .-> REF4[Reflected Wave 4]
    S5 -. Reflected r5 .-> REF5[Reflected Wave 5]
    S6 -. Reflected r6 .-> REF6[Reflected Wave 6]
    S7 -. Reflected r7 .-> REF7[Reflected Wave 7]
    S8 -. Reflected r8 .-> REF8[Reflected Wave 8]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Forward arrows represent $p_k^+(n)$ (right-going pressure wave). The dotted backward arrows represent $p_k^-(n)$ (left-going reflected wave). Each stage's $k_i$ equals the reflection coefficient $r_i$ at junction $i$.

---

### 4.3 Source-Filter Functional Architecture

This block diagram emphasizes how the *excitation* and *filter* subsystems are mathematically independent, and how the parameters can be encoded/transmitted.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SOURCE[Excitation Source Subsystem]
        S1[Voiced Pulse Train] --> MUX
        S2[Unvoiced Random Noise] --> MUX
        S3[Mixed Excitation] --> MUX
        MUX[Excitation Switch] --> GAIN[Gain Factor G]
    end
    GAIN --> FILTER
    subgraph FILTER[Vocal Tract and Radiation]
        VT[Vocal Tract All Pole Vz] --> RAD[Lip Radiation Hz]
    end
    FILTER --> OUT[Output sn]
    VT -.Coefficients ak.-> PARAMS[(Parameter Set G F0 a1..ap UV flag)]
    GAIN -.Gain.-> PARAMS
    MUX -.Voicing Flag.-> PARAMS
    PARAMS -.Encoded for Transmission.-> CODEC[Speech Codec Storage or Network]
```

---

### 4.4 Acoustic Tube Geometry (Cylindrical Sections)

A schematic representation of the **vocal tract as a cascade of cylindrical tubes** of varying cross-section.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Section 1 Area A1] --> A2[Section 2 Area A2]
    A2 --> A3[Section 3 Area A3]
    A3 --> A4[Section 4 Area A4]
    A4 --> A5[Section 5 Area A5]
    A5 --> A6[Section 6 Area A6]
    A6 --> A7[Section 7 Area A7]
    A7 --> A8[Section 8 Area A8]
    A8 --> A9[Section 9 Area A9]
    A9 --> A10[Section 10 Area A10]
    A1 -. Junction r1 .-> A2
    A2 -. Junction r2 .-> A3
    A3 -. Junction r3 .-> A4
    A4 -. Junction r4 .-> A5
    A5 -. Junction r5 .-> A6
    A6 -. Junction r6 .-> A7
    A7 -. Junction r7 .-> A8
    A8 -. Junction r8 .-> A9
    A9 -. Junction r9 .-> A10
```

**Interpretation:** Each section $k$ has area $A_k$. At the junction between sections $k$ and $k+1$, a partial reflection of the acoustic wave occurs, quantified by the reflection coefficient $r_k = (A_{k+1} - A_k)/(A_{k+1} + A_k)$. The aggregate effect of all reflections produces the resonant peaks (formants) that characterize vowels.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### ­¤ōś PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define the discrete model for speech production. With a neat block diagram, explain the source-filter model.**

**Model Answer:**

The **discrete-time model for speech production** represents human speech generation as a linear, time-varying digital system in which the vocal tract is modeled as a discrete filter excited by one of three types of excitation signals.

The **source-filter model** is a specific realization consisting of two cascaded subsystems:

- **Source**: Produces the excitation $e[n]$, which is either a **periodic pulse train** (voiced), a **random noise sequence** (unvoiced), or a **mixed** combination.
- **Filter**: Models the vocal tract as an **all-pole filter** $V(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}}$ and lip radiation as a differentiator $R(z) = 1 - z^{-1}$.

Mathematically:

$$S(z) = G\, E(z)\, V(z)\, R(z)$$

The output $S(z)$ is the produced speech signal, $G$ is the gain, and the parameters $\{G, F_0, a_k, \text{UV flag}\}$ are sufficient to reconstruct intelligible speech.

*(Examiner allocates ~1 mark for definition, ~1 mark for block diagram, ~1 mark for equation.)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**What are reflection coefficients in the context of the discrete speech production model? State the stability condition.**

**Model Answer:**

In the **lossless tube model**, the vocal tract is approximated as a cascade of cylindrical sections with different cross-sectional areas $A_k$. At the **junction** between sections $k$ and $k+1$, an incident acoustic wave is partially transmitted and partially reflected.

The **reflection coefficient** $r_k$ quantifies this fraction:

$$r_k = \frac{A_{k+1} - A_k}{A_{k+1} + A_k}$$

It is also equal to the **PARCOR coefficient** $k_k$ in the lattice filter formulation. Physically, $r_k = 0$ means no area mismatch (no reflection), $r_k \to +1$ means a large expansion (total reflection), and $r_k \to -1$ means a large contraction.

**Stability Condition:** The model is stable (and physically realizable) if and only if:

$$\vert r_k \vert < 1 \quad \text{for all } k = 1, 2, \ldots, N$$

Equivalently, this requires all tube areas to be strictly positive: $A_k > 0$ for all $k$. If any $\vert r_k \vert \geq 1$, the filter becomes unstable, and the synthesized speech will diverge or become noise-like.

---

### ­¤ōĢ PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a clear block diagram, describe the **discrete-time model for speech production**, detailing the excitation source, vocal tract filter, and radiation model. Discuss the three types of excitation signals with examples of corresponding speech sounds. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **lattice filter update equations** for the lossless acoustic tube model and explain the role of the reflection coefficient. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram and Components** *(7 marks)*

The block diagram (as shown in **Section 4.1** above) consists of three main stages:

**(i) Excitation Source** *(~2 marks)*

Three categories of excitation $e[n]$ are used:

| Type | Signal | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Voiced** | Periodic impulse train with period $P = F_s/F_0$ | Vowels (/a/, /i/, /u/), nasals (/m/, /n/) |
| **Unvoiced** | Zero-mean white noise | Fricatives (/s/, /f/, /sh/) |
| **Mixed** | Pulse + noise superposition | Plosive bursts, voiced fricatives (/z/, /v/) |

A binary flag (UV/Voiced) and the pitch period $P$ (or $F_0$) are transmitted.

**(ii) Vocal Tract Filter** *(~2 marks)*

Modeled as an all-pole filter of order $p$ (typically 8ŌĆō16):

$$V(z) = \frac{1}{1 - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}}$$

The poles of $V(z)$ correspond to the **formant frequencies** $F_1, F_2, \ldots, F_p$, which encode the phonetic identity of vowels.

**(iii) Radiation Model** *(~1 mark)*

A first-order differentiator $R(z) = 1 - z^{-1}$ that introduces a +6 dB/octave high-frequency emphasis, compensating for the -6 dB/octave natural roll-off of the glottal source. The net spectrum is approximately flat.

**(iv) Overall Equation** *(~2 marks)*

$$S(z) = G\, E(z)\, \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}}$$

The gain $G$ and the voicing parameters are updated every 10ŌĆō30 ms; the filter coefficients may be updated at the same rate for time-varying analysis.

---

**(b) Derivation of Lattice Update Equations** *(7 marks)*

Refer to the full derivation in **Section 3.1** above. The final lattice update equations are:

$$p_{k+1}^+(n) = (1 + r_k)\, p_k^+(n-1) + r_k\, p_k^-(n)$$

$$p_k^-(n) = -r_k\, p_k^+(n-1) + (1 - r_k)\, p_k^-(n)$$

with the **reflection coefficient**:

$$r_k = \frac{A_{k+1} - A_k}{A_{k+1} + A_k}$$

**Role of the reflection coefficient** *(~2 marks)*:

- It captures the **acoustic mismatch** between adjacent tube sections.
- A non-zero $r_k$ means energy is reflected back, creating standing-wave resonances (formants).
- The chain of all $N$ reflection coefficients completely characterizes the vocal-tract shape for that analysis frame.
- These $r_k$ are identical to the **PARCOR coefficients** obtained from LPC analysis, providing a direct bridge between the acoustic-tube model and linear prediction.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2 ŌĆö Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **lossless tube model** of the vocal tract. Show that the vocal tract can be approximated as a cascade of cylindrical sections and derive the relationship between tube areas and **reflection coefficients**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **Linear Predictive Coding (LPC)** in the context of speech production? Derive the **LPC prediction equations** and explain the role of the prediction error. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Lossless Tube Model** *(7 marks)*

**Physical Justification** *(~1 mark)*:

The vocal tract is an irregular tube of length $\approx$ **17.5 cm** extending from the glottis to the lips. Below **4 kHz**, viscous and thermal losses are negligible (less than 1 dB per round trip), justifying the lossless assumption.

**Discretization** *(~2 marks)*:

The continuous tract is sliced into $N$ uniform cylindrical sections, each of length $\Delta x = L/N$ and cross-sectional area $A_k$. For $N = 8$ to $12$ sections, the model reproduces formant frequencies within 5% accuracy.

Each section supports two waves: a forward-traveling $p_k^+$ and a backward-traveling $p_k^-$.

**Reflection Coefficient Derivation** *(~3 marks)*:

At a junction between sections $k$ and $k+1$, the boundary conditions (continuity of pressure and volume velocity) give:

$$p_k^+ + p_k^- = p_{k+1}^+ + p_{k+1}^-$$

$$\frac{p_k^+ - p_k^-}{Z_k} = \frac{p_{k+1}^+ - p_{k+1}^-}{Z_{k+1}}$$

where $Z_k = \rho c / A_k$ is the characteristic impedance. Solving for the reflected wave $p_{k+1}^-$:

$$p_{k+1}^- = \frac{Z_{k+1} - Z_k}{Z_{k+1} + Z_k}\, p_k^+ + \text{(backward term)}$$

Therefore, the **reflection coefficient** is:

$$r_k = \frac{Z_k - Z_{k+1}}{Z_k + Z_{k+1}} = \frac{A_{k+1} - A_k}{A_{k+1} + A_k}$$

**Stability** *(~1 mark)*: $\vert r_k \vert < 1$ requires $A_k > 0$ for all $k$.

---

**(b) Linear Predictive Coding** *(7 marks)*

**Concept** *(~1 mark)*:

LPC models the speech sample $s[n]$ as a linear combination of its past $p$ samples plus a residual excitation. It is a direct discrete-time realization of the source-filter model.

**Prediction Equation** *(~2 marks)*:

The linear predictor estimates the current sample as:

$$\hat{s}[n] = \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, s[n-k]$$

The **prediction error** (residual) is:

$$e[n] = s[n] - \hat{s}[n] = s[n] - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, s[n-k]$$

This residual is the **excitation** in the source-filter model. For voiced speech, $e[n]$ approximates a periodic pulse train; for unvoiced speech, it approximates white noise.

**Optimality Criterion** *(~2 marks)*:

The coefficients $a_k$ are chosen to minimize the mean-squared error $E = \sum_n e^2[n]$ over the analysis frame. Setting $\partial E / \partial a_k = 0$ gives the **YuleŌĆōWalker normal equations**:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{p} a_j\, R[\vert k - j \vert] = R[k], \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, p$$

where $R[m]$ is the short-time autocorrelation of the speech frame.

**Solution and Role of Error** *(~2 marks)*:

The Yule-Walker system is a **Toeplitz** system, solved in $O(p^2)$ by the **Levinson-Durbin recursion**, which simultaneously yields the **PARCOR coefficients** $k_1, k_2, \ldots, k_p$ (equal to the reflection coefficients of the equivalent tube model).

The prediction error $e[n]$ has a **flatter spectrum** than $s[n]$ ŌĆö it is "whitened." This is why the all-pole filter $V(z)$ can capture virtually all the spectral structure of $s[n]$; only the residual excitation $e[n]$ needs to be transmitted or modeled separately.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the radiation term $R(z) = 1 - z^{-1}$.** Many students write $S(z) = G E(z) V(z)$ and skip the lip-radiation effect. This loses 1ŌĆō2 marks in derivations.
> 2. **Confusing reflection coefficients with PARCOR coefficients vs. LPC coefficients.** All three are related but not identical: $r_k = k_k$ (PARCOR), but $a_k$ (LPC) is a different set obtained from the $k_k$ via Levinson-Durbin. State this clearly.
> 3. **Skipping the stability condition.** Writing the reflection coefficient formula without the constraint $\vert r_k \vert < 1$ is a half-answer. Examiners expect it.
> 4. **Wrong sign in reflection coefficient formula.** Some texts define it as $(A_{k+1} - A_k)/(A_{k+1} + A_k)$, others as the negative. Use the form consistent with the *Lattice convention* used in your derivation: $\dfrac{A_{k+1} - A_k}{A_{k+1} + A_k}$ is the most common.
> 5. **Mixing continuous-time and discrete-time notation.** Use $s[n]$ and $z$-transforms for discrete models; $s(t)$ and Laplace for continuous. Examiners deduct heavily for this confusion.
> 6. **In LPC questions, forgetting to mention the analysis frame length** (~20ŌĆō30 ms) and why the model is quasi-stationary over that duration.

---

### ­¤Ä» Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Master Equation (must memorize):** $S(z) = G \cdot E(z) \cdot V(z) \cdot R(z)$ with $V(z)$ all-pole and $R(z) = 1 - z^{-1}$.
- **Three excitation types:** Voiced (periodic impulse train), Unvoiced (white noise), Mixed.
- **Vocal tract length:** $\approx$ **17.5 cm** (adult male); discretized into **8ŌĆō12** tube sections.
- **Reflection coefficient:** $r_k = (A_{k+1} - A_k)/(A_{k+1} + A_k)$; stability demands $\vert r_k \vert < 1$.
- **PARCOR $\equiv$ Reflection Coefficient:** $k_k = r_k$; computed via Levinson-Durbin.
- **LPC order:** typically $p = 8$ to $16$ (one per expected formant + a few extra).
- **Pitch range:** Male **60ŌĆō200 Hz**, Female **150ŌĆō400 Hz**; pitch period in samples $P = F_s / F_0$.
- **Formant frequencies** for uniform tube: $F_k = (2k-1) c / (4 L)$.
- **LPC residual** = excitation source; white-noise-like for unvoiced, periodic-pulse-like for voiced.
- **Quasi-stationarity:** Speech can be treated as time-invariant over **10ŌĆō30 ms** frames.
- **Sampling rates used in practice:** **8 kHz** (telephony), **16 kHz** (wideband speech).
- **Lossless assumption valid** below **4 kHz**.
- **Lattice structure** = direct digital implementation of the lossless tube model.
- **Levinson-Durbin recursion:** solves Yule-Walker in $O(p^2)$; used to extract LPC + PARCOR simultaneously.
- **Speech codecs** based on this model: **LPC-10e** (2.4 kbps), **MELP** (2.4 kbps), **CELP** family.
- **Engineering applications:** TTS, ASR front-ends, speaker ID, biomedical vocal-tract diagnostics, low-bit-rate communication.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Short-Time Speech Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST866 - SPEECH AND AUDIO PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Short-Time Speech Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Short-Time Speech Analysis** is a foundational signal processing framework used in speech and audio engineering that analyses the non-stationary speech signal $x(n)$ by examining short overlapping (or non-overlapping) segments of length $N$ samples, obtained by multiplying the original signal with a sliding window function $w(n)$. Because the vocal tract shape, glottal excitation, and articulatory configuration of a speaker remain approximately invariant over **10ŌĆō30 ms** intervals, the speech signal is assumed to be *quasi-stationary* within each short frame. This principle enables the use of classical linear time-invariant (LTI) analysis techniques on individual frames.

The general mathematical model of short-time analysis is expressed as:

$$Q_n = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} T[x(m)] \cdot w(n-m)$$

where:
- $x(m)$ is the discrete-time speech signal
- $w(n-m)$ is the analysis window of length $N$ shifted by $n$ samples
- $T[\cdot]$ is a time-invariant linear or non-linear transformation
- $Q_n$ is the resulting short-time feature sequence indexed by $n$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The short-time analysis concept is the *backbone* of nearly every speech processing algorithm ŌĆö from pitch detection and voiced/unvoiced classification to spectrograms, MFCCs, and modern deep-learning ASR front-ends.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are reading a long, dense book written in a language where the *meaning, grammar, and sentence structure keep changing every 30 milliseconds*. You cannot translate the whole book at once because the rules keep shifting. So, what do you do?

You read it **one tiny paragraph at a time** ŌĆö pausing after each paragraph to apply the *current* set of rules. Each pause-and-look action is your **"analysis window"**. After processing a paragraph, you slide the window forward to the next one.

In speech processing:
- The **book** = continuous speech signal $x(n)$ (non-stationary)
- The **paragraphs** = short frames of 10ŌĆō30 ms (quasi-stationary)
- The **window** = the mathematical "magnifying glass" $w(n)$ that isolates one frame
- The **set of rules** = LTI transforms (energy, autocorrelation, FFT, etc.)

This is precisely the philosophy behind short-time analysis.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Parameters

| Parameter | Standard Value (KTU Convention) |
|---|---|
| Frame length ($N$) | **160 to 400 samples** (10ŌĆō25 ms at 16 kHz) |
| Frame shift / hop size | $N/2$ or $N/3$ samples (50ŌĆō66.67% overlap) |
| Sampling frequency ($F_s$) | **8 kHz / 16 kHz / 44.1 kHz** |
| Quasi-stationary duration | **10ŌĆō30 ms** for voiced speech |
| Window types | Rectangular, Hamming, Hann, Blackman, Kaiser |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quasi-stationarity assumption** is the most critical conceptual pillar. A student who cannot justify this assumption cannot score full marks on any KTU question about why we use short-time analysis.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Do We Need Short-Time Analysis?

Speech signals are **non-stationary** in the strict sense. This means their statistical properties (mean, variance, autocorrelation) change with time. Therefore, classical DSP tools that assume stationarity cannot be applied directly to the entire utterance.

**Key insight:** While the *entire* signal is non-stationary, *any sufficiently small* segment behaves as if it were stationary because the vocal tract and glottal source vary slowly. This is the **quasi-stationarity** property.

## 2.2 The Windowing Process ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Select a window function** $w(n)$ of finite length $N$ (e.g., Hamming).
2. **Multiply** the input speech signal $x(n)$ by $w(n)$ to extract a frame: $x_n(m) = x(m) \cdot w(n-m)$.
3. **Compute** a specific feature $T[\cdot]$ (e.g., energy, ZCR) on the windowed segment.
4. **Slide** the window by $R$ samples (hop size) to the next position: $n \rightarrow n+R$.
5. **Repeat** until the entire signal has been processed.

## 2.3 Common Short-Time Features

### 2.3.1 Short-Time Energy (STE)
The energy of the $n^{th}$ frame:

$$E_n = \sum_{m=n}^{n+N-1} [x(m) \cdot w(m-n)]^2 = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} [x(n+m) \cdot w(m)]^2$$

### 2.3.2 Short-Time Average Magnitude
A cheaper alternative to STE (avoids the squaring operation):

$$M_n = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} \vert x(n+m) \vert \cdot w(m)$$

### 2.3.3 Short-Time Zero-Crossing Rate (ZCR)
Counts the number of sign changes within a frame:

$$Z_n = \frac{1}{2N} \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} \vert \text{sgn}[x(n+m)] - \text{sgn}[x(n+m-1)] \vert$$

### 2.3.4 Short-Time Autocorrelation Function (STACF)
Measures the self-similarity of the signal at a time lag $k$ within frame $n$:

$$R_n(k) = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1-k} x(n+m) \cdot w(m) \cdot x(n+m+k) \cdot w(m+k)$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table ŌĆö these are the **most-frequently-asked** equations in KTU 2024 ESE for Module 1.

| Feature | Mathematical Expression | Primary Application |
|---|---|---|
| Short-Time Energy | $E_n = \displaystyle\sum_{m=0}^{N-1} [x(n+m) \cdot w(m)]^2$ | Voiced/unvoiced detection, amplitude envelope |
| Short-Time Magnitude | $M_n = \displaystyle\sum_{m=0}^{N-1} \vert x(n+m) \vert w(m)$ | Energy estimate (cheaper) |
| Short-Time ZCR | $Z_n = \dfrac{1}{2N} \displaystyle\sum_{m=0}^{N-1} \vert \text{sgn}[x(n+m)] - \text{sgn}[x(n+m-1)] \vert$ | Voiced/unvoiced classification, fricative detection |
| Short-Time Autocorrelation | $R_n(k) = \displaystyle\sum_{m=0}^{N-1-k} x(n+m) w(m) x(n+m+k) w(m+k)$ | Pitch period detection, formant estimation |
| Hamming Window | $w(m) = 0.54 - 0.46 \cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi m}{N-1}\right)$, $0 \le m \le N-1$ | Default choice in speech coding |
| Hann Window | $w(m) = 0.5\!\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi m}{N-1}\right)\right]$ | Spectrograms, smoother spectrum |
| Rectangular Window | $w(m) = 1$ for $0 \le m \le N-1$, else $0$ | Maximum resolution, more leakage |
| Frame Rate | $F_r = F_s / R$ | Determines temporal granularity |

> [!NOTE]
> Note the use of `\vert` in the magnitude expressions to avoid breaking markdown table syntax ŌĆö this is the *KTU Examiner's preferred notation* for absolute value in tables.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Voice Activity Detection (VAD):** STE + ZCR thresholding identifies speech vs. silence in VoIP, GSM codecs, and hearing aids.
- **Pitch Tracking:** STACF peaks reveal the fundamental period $T_0$, enabling prosody and intonation analysis.
- **Speech Coding (CELP, LPC):** Short-time linear prediction models the vocal tract envelope per frame.
- **Speaker Recognition:** Time sequences of STE and ZCR form discriminative low-level features.
- **Noise Robustness:** Adaptive frame-wise energy normalization compensates for background noise.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Short-Time Energy Function

**Statement:** Starting from the continuous-time definition of instantaneous power, derive the discrete short-time energy $E_n$ for a windowed speech frame.

**Given:**
- Discrete-time speech signal $x(n)$
- Window $w(m)$ of length $N$
- Frame index $n$

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Continuous-time energy of a signal segment:**
The energy of a signal $x(t)$ over a finite interval $[t_1, t_2]$ is:

$$E = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Discretization using the sampling interval $T = 1/F_s$:**
Replace $dt$ by $T$, $x(t)$ by $x(m)$, and the integration bounds by the frame index:

$$E = T \sum_{m=n}^{n+N-1} \vert x(m) \vert^2$$

Since $T$ is a constant scaling factor, it is often dropped in DSP formulations. We retain it for completeness.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the sliding window $w(m-n)$:**
Multiplying $x(m)$ by $w(m-n)$ selects the $n^{th}$ frame and tapers its edges:

$$E_n = T \sum_{m=n}^{n+N-1} \vert x(m) \cdot w(m-n) \vert^2$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Change of index to start from 0:**
Let $m' = m - n$, so $m = m' + n$. When $m = n \Rightarrow m' = 0$, and when $m = n+N-1 \Rightarrow m' = N-1$:

$$E_n = T \sum_{m'=0}^{N-1} \vert x(n+m') \cdot w(m') \vert^2$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Drop the constant $T$ (KTU convention):**
Since $T$ only scales and does not change the feature's behaviour:

$$E_n = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} [x(n+m) \cdot w(m)]^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the exact form KTU examiners expect in derivations. Marks are typically lost when students forget the **squared windowed multiplication** and only write $\sum x^2(n+m)$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Short-Time Zero Crossing Rate

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define the signum function for discrete signals:**

$$\text{sgn}[x(n)] = \begin{cases} 1, & x(n) \ge 0 \\ -1, & x(n) < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Detect a zero crossing between samples $x(n+m-1)$ and $x(n+m)$:**
A sign change occurs when $\text{sgn}[x(n+m)] \neq \text{sgn}[x(n+m-1)]$, which means:

$$\vert \text{sgn}[x(n+m)] - \text{sgn}[x(n+m-1)] \vert = 2$$

and equals $0$ when no sign change occurs.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Sum over the frame and normalize by $2N$:**

$$Z_n = \frac{1}{2N} \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} \vert \text{sgn}[x(n+m)] - \text{sgn}[x(n+m-1)] \vert$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Interpretation:**
- $Z_n \approx 0$ for **voiced** speech (low frequency content, few crossings).
- $Z_n$ is **high** for **unvoiced / fricative** speech (noise-like, high frequency).

## 3.3 Derivation of Short-Time Autocorrelation and Pitch Detection

**Step 1 ŌĆö Classical autocorrelation of a deterministic frame:**
For a finite energy sequence $x(m)$ of length $N$, autocorrelation at lag $k$ is:

$$R(k) = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1-k} x(m) \cdot x(m+k)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the sliding window for short-time analysis:**
The frame is extracted as $x_n(m) = x(n+m) \cdot w(m)$, $0 \le m \le N-1$:

$$R_n(k) = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1-k} [x(n+m) w(m)] \cdot [x(n+m+k) w(m+k)]$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Use for pitch detection:**
For a periodic voiced signal of period $P$, $R_n(k)$ attains a strong peak at $k = P$ (and multiples $2P, 3P, \dots$). The **first maximum** after $k=0$ gives the **pitch period $P_0$**, and the **fundamental frequency** is:

$$F_0 = \frac{F_s}{P_0}$$

**Analytical justification:**
At $k = 0$, $R_n(0) = E_n$ (maximum value, since all terms are positive). For periodic signals of period $P$, the alignment between $x(n+m)$ and $x(n+m+P)$ restores high correlation, creating a local maximum.

## 3.4 Effect of Window Length on STE and ZCR

| Parameter | Short Window ($N$ small) | Long Window ($N$ large) |
|---|---|---|
| Time resolution | **High** | **Low** |
| Frequency resolution | **Low** | **High** |
| STE smoothness | Rapid fluctuations | Smooth contour |
| ZCR variance | **High** variance | **Low** variance |
| Pitch accuracy | Poor (less periodicity) | Better (more cycles captured) |
| Formant accuracy | Poor | Better |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often quote "longer window is always better." This is FALSE. There is a fundamental **time-frequency resolution trade-off** controlled by $N$. Always justify your choice of $N$ with respect to the application (e.g., $N \approx 2 \times P_0$ for pitch detection).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Short-Time Analysis

The diagram below illustrates the complete processing pipeline, from raw speech input to short-time feature extraction. Each block is annotated with its corresponding mathematical operation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Speech Signal xn] --> B[Pre-emphasis Filter Hpre z]
    B --> C[Frame Blocking into 10-30 ms Segments]
    C --> D[Apply Sliding Window wm Hamming or Hann]
    D --> E1[Branch 1: Compute STE En]
    D --> E2[Branch 2: Compute ZCR Zn]
    D --> E3[Branch 3: Compute STACF Rnk]
    D --> E4[Branch 4: Compute Average Magnitude Mn]
    E1 --> F[Concatenate Feature Vectors Frame by Frame]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G[Output Short-Time Feature Stream Qn]
    G --> H1[Voiced vs Unvoiced Decision]
    G --> H2[Pitch Period Estimation]
    G --> H3[Spectrogram Generation]
    G --> H4[VAD and Endpoint Detection]
    H1 --> I[Downstream Applications Codec ASR Biometrics]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
```

## 4.2 Windowing Process Schematic

The following diagram explains how the window slides across the signal and how overlapping frames relate to the hop size $R$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Speech Signal xn continuous] --> W1[Window 1 at n equals 0]
    W1 --> F1[Frame 1 samples 0 to N-1]
    S1 --> W2[Window 2 at n equals R]
    W2 --> F2[Frame 2 samples R to R plus N-1]
    S1 --> W3[Window 3 at n equals 2R]
    W3 --> F3[Frame 3 samples 2R to 2R plus N-1]
    S1 --> W4[Window 4 at n equals 3R]
    W4 --> F4[Frame 4 samples 3R to 3R plus N-1]
    F1 --> O1[Feature Q0]
    F2 --> O2[Feature Q1]
    F3 --> O3[Feature Q2]
    F4 --> O4[Feature Q3]
```

## 4.3 Voiced vs Unvoiced Classification Logic (STE + ZCR)

This is a classic KTU application problem ŌĆö using STE and ZCR thresholds together to distinguish speech categories.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Compute En and Zn per frame] --> C1{En is greater than Eth?}
    C1 -->|No| Silence[Silence or Background]
    C1 -->|Yes| C2{Zn is less than Zth?}
    C2 -->|Yes| Voiced[Voiced Speech Vowels Nasals]
    C2 -->|No| Unvoiced[Unvoiced Speech Fricatives Stops]
    Voiced --> Out1[STE high ZCR low Pitch detectable]
    Unvoiced --> Out2[STE medium ZCR high Noise like spectrum]
    Silence --> Out3[STE low ZCR variable]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Threshold values $E_{th}$ and $Z_{th}$ are typically adaptive and depend on the noise floor, signal level, and $F_s$. In KTU numerical problems, fixed empirical values are often provided.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`
**Define short-time energy of a speech signal. Why is windowing necessary before computing short-time energy?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Short-time energy $E_n$ of a speech signal $x(n)$ over a frame of length $N$ starting at sample $n$ is defined as:

$$E_n = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} [x(n+m) \cdot w(m)]^2$$

where $w(m)$ is the analysis window function (e.g., Hamming).

**Necessity of windowing (1 mark):** Speech is a non-stationary signal whose statistics change rapidly. By applying a window of short duration (10ŌĆō30 ms), the signal within the frame is assumed to be quasi-stationary, allowing meaningful energy estimation. The window also tapers the frame edges to reduce spectral leakage.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`
**Explain the concept of short-time zero crossing rate. How is it useful in distinguishing voiced and unvoiced speech?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Concept (1.5 marks):** Short-time zero crossing rate $Z_n$ measures the number of times the speech signal changes sign within a frame of length $N$:

$$Z_n = \frac{1}{2N} \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} \vert \text{sgn}[x(n+m)] - \text{sgn}[x(n+m-1)] \vert$$

**Utility (1.5 marks):**
- **Voiced speech** (vowels, nasals) has **low ZCR** because the signal is quasi-periodic with low dominant frequency.
- **Unvoiced speech** (fricatives, stops) has **high ZCR** because the signal is noise-like with high-frequency content.
- Therefore, ZCR acts as a simple but effective feature for voiced/unvoiced classification.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 1 internal choice is provided. **Answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`**

**(a)** Derive the mathematical expression for short-time autocorrelation function $R_n(k)$ of a speech signal. Explain how it is used for pitch period detection. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4 kHz speech signal is analyzed using frames of 20 ms duration with a Hamming window. Compute the number of samples per frame, the first zero of the Hamming window's frequency response, and comment on whether this frame is suitable for detecting a pitch of 100 Hz. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) ŌĆö Derivation of STACF and pitch detection (7 marks):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Frame extraction using window (1 mark):**
The $n^{th}$ windowed frame of length $N$ is:

$$x_n(m) = x(n+m) \cdot w(m), \quad 0 \le m \le N-1$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Define autocorrelation at lag $k$ (2 marks):**
For a deterministic finite-length sequence, autocorrelation at lag $k$ is:

$$R_n(k) = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1-k} x_n(m) \cdot x_n(m+k)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute the windowed frame (2 marks):**

$$R_n(k) = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1-k} [x(n+m) \cdot w(m)] \cdot [x(n+m+k) \cdot w(m+k)]$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Pitch detection logic (2 marks):**
For a periodic voiced signal with fundamental period $P_0$, the autocorrelation $R_n(k)$ attains local maxima at $k = P_0, 2P_0, 3P_0, \dots$ The **first non-zero maximum** identifies the pitch period:

$$P_0 = \arg\max_{k > k_{min}} R_n(k)$$

and the pitch frequency is:

$$F_0 = \frac{F_s}{P_0}$$

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Number of samples per frame (2 marks):**

$$N = F_s \times T_{frame} = 4000 \times 0.020 = 80 \text{ samples}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö First zero of Hamming window's frequency response (3 marks):**
For a Hamming window, the main-lobe width is $\dfrac{4\pi}{N}$ and the first null occurs at frequency:

$$f_{null} = \frac{F_s}{N} \times 2 = \frac{4000}{80} \times 2 = 100 \text{ Hz}$$

*(Because the first zero of a Hamming window is at $\pm 2/(N-1)$ in normalized frequency, equivalent to $\pm 2 F_s / N$ in Hz.)*

**Step 3 ŌĆö Suitability for 100 Hz pitch (2 marks):**
Since $f_{null} = 100$ Hz matches the target $F_0 = 100$ Hz, the **main lobe is too wide** to resolve this pitch. The frame is **not suitable**; a longer frame (e.g., 30ŌĆō40 ms) is needed to capture at least 2ŌĆō3 pitch cycles and achieve adequate frequency resolution.

$$\text{Pitch cycles in frame} = F_0 \times T_{frame} = 100 \times 0.020 = 2 \text{ cycles}$$

While 2 cycles is the *bare minimum*, KTU's preferred rule is $N \ge 2 \times P_0$, so the choice is marginal.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Explain the short-time energy and short-time average magnitude functions. Compare their computational complexity and noise sensitivity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A speech signal sampled at 8 kHz is segmented into frames of 25 ms with 50% overlap. Determine (i) frame length in samples, (ii) hop size in samples, (iii) number of frames in a 4-second recording, and (iv) sketch the relative positions of frames 1, 2, 3 on a sample-index axis. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) ŌĆö Comparison of STE and STAM (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Short-Time Energy (STE) | Short-Time Average Magnitude (STAM) |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $E_n = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} [x(n+m) \cdot w(m)]^2$ | $M_n = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} \vert x(n+m) \vert \cdot w(m)$ |
| Operation (1 mark) | Square + sum | Absolute value + sum |
| Computational cost (2 marks) | Higher: needs multiplication for squaring | Lower: only addition and magnitude |
| Noise sensitivity (2 marks) | **High** ŌĆö squaring amplifies large outliers | **Lower** ŌĆö linear magnitude is more robust |
| Dynamic range (2 marks) | **Wide** ŌĆö emphasizes high-energy peaks | **Compressed** ŌĆö better for low-amplitude regions |

**Conclusion:** STAM is preferred in low-power real-time systems; STE is preferred when amplitude discrimination is critical.

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Frame length (2 marks):**

$$N = 8000 \times 0.025 = 200 \text{ samples}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Hop size (1 mark):**
With 50% overlap, the hop size is half the frame length:

$$R = \frac{N}{2} = 100 \text{ samples}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Number of frames in 4 seconds (2 marks):**
Total samples in 4 seconds: $8000 \times 4 = 32000$ samples.
Number of frames:

$$N_{frames} = \left\lfloor \frac{L - N}{R} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor \frac{32000 - 200}{100} \right\rfloor + 1 = 318 + 1 = 319 \text{ frames}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Sample-index sketch of frames 1, 2, 3 (2 marks):**

| Frame | Start sample | End sample |
|---|---|---|
| Frame 1 | 0 | 199 |
| Frame 2 | 100 | 299 |
| Frame 3 | 200 | 399 |

```
Sample index:   0      100     200     300     400
                |       |       |       |       |
Frame 1:   [=============================]
Frame 2:            [=============================]
Frame 3:                    [=============================]
```

Each frame overlaps the next by 100 samples, confirming the 50% overlap.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the window $w(m)$** inside the summation ŌĆö students write $\sum x^2(n+m)$ instead of $\sum [x(n+m) w(m)]^2$. Lose **1ŌĆō2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing $N$ (frame length) with $R$ (hop size)** in numerical problems. Always state both clearly.
> 3. **Dropping the factor $1/2N$** in ZCR formula ŌĆö full marks require the exact normalized expression.
> 4. **Claiming voiced speech has high ZCR** ŌĆö this is the *opposite* of the truth. Vowels are low-frequency ŌåÆ low ZCR.
> 5. **Using rectangular window for pitch detection** without justification ŌĆö examiners expect a tapered window (Hamming/Hann) to be mentioned for leakage reduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Short-Time Speech Analysis**

- **Quasi-stationarity:** Speech is stationary over **10ŌĆō30 ms**; this is the *fundamental assumption* enabling short-time analysis.
- **Windowing operation:** $x_n(m) = x(n+m) \cdot w(m)$, $0 \le m \le N-1$.
- **Hamming window (default):** $w(m) = 0.54 - 0.46 \cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi m}{N-1}\right)$.
- **STE formula:** $E_n = \sum [x(n+m) w(m)]^2$ ŌĆö high for voiced, low for silence/unvoiced.
- **STAM formula:** $M_n = \sum \vert x(n+m) \vert w(m)$ ŌĆö cheaper than STE.
- **ZCR formula:** $Z_n = \dfrac{1}{2N} \sum \vert \text{sgn}[x(n+m)] - \text{sgn}[x(n+m-1)] \vert$ ŌĆö high for unvoiced, low for voiced.
- **STACF formula:** $R_n(k) = \sum x(n+m) w(m) x(n+m+k) w(m+k)$ ŌĆö peak at lag $P_0$ gives pitch.
- **Pitch frequency:** $F_0 = F_s / P_0$.
- **Frame length vs. hop size:** $N = F_s \cdot T_{frame}$, $R = N \cdot (1 - \text{overlap ratio})$.
- **Time-frequency trade-off:** Short $N$ ŌåÆ better time resolution, poorer frequency resolution.
- **Use STE + ZCR together** for robust voiced/unvoiced/silence classification.
- **Always use a tapered window** (Hamming/Hann) for pitch and formant estimation to minimize spectral leakage.
- **Voiced speech:** Periodic, low ZCR, high STE, clear pitch in STACF.
- **Unvoiced speech:** Aperiodic, high ZCR, lower STE, no clear STACF peak.
- **Silence:** Very low STE, low ZCR (in clean conditions).

> **End of Module 1 Notes ŌĆö Short-Time Speech Analysis** Ō£ö
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Windowing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST866 - SPEECH AND AUDIO PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Windowing # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Windowing** in Speech and Audio Processing is the operation of multiplying a discrete-time signal $x[n]$ by a finite-length, time-limited weighting function $w[n]$ of duration $N$ samples, producing a truncated (windowed) segment $x_w[n] = x[n] \cdot w[n]$, such that $0 \le n \le N-1$. The window function is non-zero only over a finite interval and typically tapers smoothly to zero at the boundaries to control spectral leakage during short-time spectral analysis. In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020)** framework, windowing is the foundational pre-processing block for *short-time (short-term) analysis* of non-stationary signals such as speech. Since speech is a **quasi-stationary** signal ŌĆö its statistics remain approximately constant only over short durations of **20 ms to 40 ms** ŌĆö windowing allows us to apply stationary DSP techniques (like the DFT) locally in time. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Photo Frame" View > [!NOTE] > **Analogy:** Imagine you are photographing a long mural painted on a wall (the continuous speech signal). A camera cannot capture the whole mural in one sharp image because the light and the painting change across its length. So you take many overlapping photographs through a rectangular viewfinder (the **window**). Each photograph shows only a *slice* of the mural. The viewfinder's shape (rectangular, oval, soft-edged) affects how much "spillover" or "blur" appears at the edges of each photo. In DSP, that blur is mathematically called **spectral leakage**. - **Sharp frame edges** (Rectangular window) ŌåÆ bright, clear center, but distorted edges (high side lobes). - **Soft, feathered edges** (Hamming / Hanning window) ŌåÆ slightly dimmer center, but smooth, clean edges (low side lobes). - **Vignetted frame** (Blackman / Kaiser) ŌåÆ dimmer overall, but extremely clean spectrum (very low side lobes, wider main lobe). ## 1.3 Why Windowing is Indispensable | Reason | Engineering Justification | |---|---| | **Finite Observation** | Real DSP hardware can only process finite-length blocks. | | **Stationarity Enforcement** | Speech statistics are constant over **20ŌĆō40 ms** (pitch period stable). | | **Spectral Leakage Control** | Abrupt truncation causes sinc-like ripples in the frequency domain. | | **Filter Design Basis** | All FIR filters are designed by windowing the *ideal* impulse response. | | **STFT Foundation** | The Short-Time Fourier Transform is defined as $X_w(\omega) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} w[n] \, x[n] e^{-j\omega n}$. | > [!TIP] > **Standard KTU Definition to Memorize:** *"Windowing is the process of selecting a finite segment of a longer signal by multiplying it with a window function, enabling localized frequency analysis and reducing spectral leakage caused by signal truncation."* # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mathematical Formulation Given an infinite (or long) discrete signal $x[n]$, the windowed signal is: $$ x_w[n] = x[n] \cdot w[n], \qquad 0 \le n \le N-1 $$ where $w[n]$ is the **window sequence** of length $N$ samples, and $w[n] = 0$ outside $[0, N-1]$. In the **frequency domain**, by the multiplicationŌĆōconvolution duality of the DTFT: $$ X_w(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\theta}) \, W(e^{j(\omega - \theta)}) \, d\theta $$ That is, **windowing in time** = **periodic convolution in frequency** between the true spectrum and the window's spectrum. ## 2.2 Properties of an Ideal Window (Theoretical Goals) A perfect window would have: 1. **Impulse-like frequency response** ŌĆö energy concentrated only at $\omega = 0$. 2. **Zero side lobes** ŌĆö no spectral leakage. 3. **Narrowest possible main lobe** ŌĆö best frequency resolution. 4. **No amplitude distortion** in the time domain. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Heisenberg-type Trade-off:** No finite-duration window can simultaneously have a narrow main lobe AND low side lobes. This is the central engineering trade-off in window design ŌĆö see Section 2.4 below. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Window Name | Time-Domain Equation $w[n]$, $0 \le n \le N-1$ | Main Lobe Width (bins) | Peak Side Lobe Level (dB) | Side Lobe Roll-off (dB/oct) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Rectangular** | $w[n] = 1$ | $\dfrac{2}{N}$ (narrowest) | $-13 \text{ dB}$ | $-6$ | | **Bartlett (Triangular)** | $w[n] = 1 - \dfrac{\left\vert n - \frac{N-1}{2} \right\vert}{\frac{N-1}{2}}$ | $\dfrac{4}{N}$ | $-26 \text{ dB}$ | $-12$ | | **Hanning (Hann)** | $w[n] = 0.5 - 0.5 \cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right)$ | $\dfrac{4}{N}$ | $-31 \text{ dB}$ | $-18$ | | **Hamming** | $w[n] = 0.54 - 0.46 \cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right)$ | $\dfrac{4}{N}$ | $-41 \text{ dB}$ | $-6$ | | **Blackman** | $w[n] = 0.42 - 0.5 \cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right) + 0.08 \cos\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi n}{N-1}\right)$ | $\dfrac{6}{N}$ | $-57 \text{ dB}$ | $-18$ | | **BlackmanŌĆōHarris (4-term)** | Sum of 4 cosines, optimized coefficients | $\dfrac{8}{N}$ | $-92 \text{ dB}$ | $-6$ | | **Kaiser ($\beta$ tunable)** | $w[n] = \dfrac{I_0\!\left(\beta \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{2n}{N-1} - 1\right)^2}\right)}{I_0(\beta)}$ | tunable via $\beta$ | tunable | tunable | > **Notation Key:** $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes absolute value; $I_0(\cdot)$ is the **zeroth-order modified Bessel function of the first kind**; $N$ is the window length in samples. ## 2.4 The Three Pillars of Window Trade-off (KTU High-Yield) 1. **Main Lobe Width** $\leftrightarrow$ **Frequency Resolution** Narrow main lobe ŌåÆ can distinguish closely spaced frequency components. 2. **Side Lobe Level** $\leftrightarrow$ **Spectral Leakage** Low side lobes ŌåÆ weak frequencies are not masked by strong ones. 3. **Side Lobe Roll-off Rate** $\leftrightarrow$ **Wideband Leakage Decay** Steep roll-off ŌåÆ leakage from a strong tone dies out quickly as you move away in frequency. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **STFT-based Vocoders** (used in MP3, AAC codecs) ŌåÆ window choice determines codec quality. - **Speech Recognition Front-Ends** (Kaldi, ESPnet) ŌåÆ typically use **Hamming** or **Hann** windows on 25 ms frames with 10 ms shift. - **FIR Filter Design** (ParksŌĆōMcClellan, windowed-sinc) ŌåÆ window choice sets stop-band attenuation. - **Spectral Analysis & Pitch Tracking** ŌåÆ BlackmanŌĆōHarris used in lab-grade measurement; Kaiser used in adaptive systems. - **Biomedical Signal Processing** (ECG, EEG) ŌåÆ Blackman window suppresses 50/60 Hz mains leakage during FFT analysis. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation ŌĆö DTFT of the Rectangular Window The **rectangular window** of length $N$ is defined as: $$ w_R[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 \le n \le N-1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ We evaluate its Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) explicitly. $$ W_R(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} w_R[n] \, e^{-j\omega n} = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{-j\omega n} $$ **Step 1 ŌĆö Recognize the finite geometric series.** This is a geometric progression with common ratio $e^{-j\omega}$ and $N$ terms. Apply the closed-form identity: $$ \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} r^{n} = \frac{1 - r^{N}}{1 - r}, \quad r \neq 1 $$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute $r = e^{-j\omega}$:** $$ W_R(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1 - e^{-j\omega N}}{1 - e^{-j\omega}} $$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Multiply numerator and denominator by $e^{j\omega N/2}$ and $e^{j\omega/2}$ to symmetrize:** $$ W_R(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{e^{-j\omega N/2}\left(e^{j\omega N/2} - e^{-j\omega N/2}\right)}{e^{-j\omega/2}\left(e^{j\omega/2} - e^{-j\omega/2}\right)} = \frac{\sin(\omega N/2)}{\sin(\omega/2)} \cdot e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2} $$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Separate magnitude and phase:** $$ \vert W_R(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \left\vert \frac{\sin(\omega N/2)}{\sin(\omega/2)} \right\vert $$ $$ \angle W_R(e^{j\omega}) = -\omega \cdot \frac{N-1}{2} + \pi \cdot \mathbb{1}\!\left[\text{sin term negative}\right] $$ > **Engineering Interpretation:** The magnitude $\vert W_R(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ is the famous **periodic sinc / Dirichlet kernel**. Its first zero occurs when $\sin(\omega N/2) = 0$ with $\omega \neq 0$, i.e., at $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi}{N}$. The **first side-lobe peak** sits at roughly $\omega \approx \dfrac{3\pi}{N}$ and is **13 dB below** the main lobe ŌĆö this is the source of the worst spectral leakage of any common window. ## 3.2 Derivation ŌĆö Hamming Window as a Truncated Cosine Series A general **raised-cosine** family window is: $$ w[n] = A - B \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right) + C \cos\!\left(\frac{4\pi n}{N-1}\right) - \cdots $$ Setting $A = 0.54$, $B = 0.46$ yields the **Hamming window**: $$ w_H[n] = 0.54 - 0.46 \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right), \qquad 0 \le n \le N-1 $$ **Step 1 ŌĆö Express the cosine as sum of complex exponentials:** $$ \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right) = \frac{1}{2} e^{j 2\pi n/(N-1)} + \frac{1}{2} e^{-j 2\pi n/(N-1)} $$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute and expand:** $$ w_H[n] = 0.54 - 0.23 \, e^{j 2\pi n/(N-1)} - 0.23 \, e^{-j 2\pi n/(N-1)} $$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Take DTFT term-by-term (linearity):** $$ W_H(e^{j\omega}) = 0.54 \, W_R(e^{j\omega}) - 0.23 \, W_R\!\left(e^{j\left(\omega - \frac{2\pi}{N-1}\right)}\right) - 0.23 \, W_R\!\left(e^{j\left(\omega + \frac{2\pi}{N-1}\right)}\right) $$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Engineering meaning:** The Hamming spectrum is the **sum of three shifted Dirichlet kernels**: - A main lobe of magnitude **0.54** centered at $\omega = 0$. - Two negative lobes of magnitude **0.23** centered at $\omega = \pm \dfrac{2\pi}{N-1}$ ŌĆö these **partially cancel** the side lobes of the main lobe. - The careful choice $0.54 / 0.46$ minimizes the **maximum side-lobe level** for the 2-term raised-cosine family, achieving **$-41$ dB**. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Most Likely KTU Exam Table) | Property | Rectangular | Bartlett | Hanning | Hamming | Blackman | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Continuity at edges | **Discontinuous** | Continuous (zero) | Continuous (zero) | **Discontinuous** | Continuous (zero) | | Main lobe width (bins) | **2** | 4 | 4 | 4 | 6 | | Peak side-lobe (dB) | $-13$ | $-26$ | $-31$ | $\mathbf{-41}$ | $-57$ | | Roll-off (dB/oct) | $-6$ | $-12$ | $-18$ | $-6$ | $-18$ | | Best for | Resolution | General | Speech | **Speech + leakage** | High-precision | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Memory Trick ŌĆö "HDDD HHH" :** > **H**amming ŌåÆ best **H**idden tones (lowest side lobe among 2-term). > **H**anning ŌåÆ best **H**armonic, zero at edges. > **B**lackman ŌåÆ **B**ig main lobe, lowest leakage. > **R**ectangular ŌåÆ **R**esolution king, worst leakage. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture ŌĆö Role of Windowing in a Speech Analysis Front-End ```mermaid flowchart LR A[INPUT SPEECH
x of n] --> B[PRE-EMPHASIS
FILTER H of z] B --> C[FRAMING AND
WINDOWING
w of n] C --> D[DFT or FFT
N POINT] D --> E[MAGNITUDE
SPECTRUM
X of k] E --> F[FEATURE
EXTRACTION
MFCC LOG FILTER] F --> G[OUTPUT
FEATURE
VECTOR] H[WINDOW
FUNCTION
w of n] --> C I[WINDOW
LENGTH N] --> H J[FRAME
SHIFT M] --> C ``` > **Diagram interpretation (KTU board-exam phrasing):** The raw speech $x[n]$ first passes through a **pre-emphasis high-pass filter** $H(z) = 1 - \alpha z^{-1}$ (typically $\alpha \approx 0.97$) to flatten the spectrum. The windowed block $w[n]$ then multiplies each $N$-sample frame, the FFT computes the short-time magnitude spectrum, and the MFCC log-filterbank produces compact features used by ASR / speaker-ID systems. ## 4.2 Conceptual Map ŌĆö Window Selection Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[START CHOOSE
A WINDOW] --> B{IS THE GOAL
PURE FREQUENCY
RESOLUTION} B -- YES --> C[USE RECTANGULAR
WINDOW] B -- NO --> D{IS STRONG
SIDELOBE
LEAKAGE A PROBLEM} D -- YES --> E{IS SHARP EDGE
TOLERATED} E -- YES --> F[USE HAMMING
WINDOW] E -- NO --> G[USE HANNING
OR BLACKMAN] D -- NO --> H{IS TUNABLE
SHAPE NEEDED} H -- YES --> I[USE KAISER
WITH BETA] H -- NO --> J[DEFAULT USE
HANNING WINDOW] ``` > **Diagram interpretation:** This decision tree mirrors the KTU lab-manual heuristics: rectangular for synthetic unit tests, Hamming for real-time ASR front-ends (no zeros at frame boundaries ŌåÆ smoother overlap-add), and Kaiser when an adaptive $\beta$ parameter is required (e.g., adaptive filter design). ## 4.3 Convolution Diagram ŌĆö Time ├Ś Frequency Duality ```mermaid flowchart LR A[SIGNAL
X of omega] --> C[CONVOLUTION
IN FREQUENCY] B[WINDOW
W of omega] --> C C --> D[OUTPUT
XW of omega] E[SIGNAL
x of n] --> F[POINTWISE
MULTIPLY] G[WINDOW
w of n] --> F F --> H[OUTPUT
xw of n] A --- X1[DTFT
PAIR] E --- X1 B --- X2[DTFT
PAIR] G --- X2 D --- X3[IDTFT
PAIR] H --- X3 ``` > **Key Takeaway:** Multiplying in the time domain corresponds to convolving in the frequency domain. The "smeared" output $X_W(e^{j\omega})$ is the true spectrum $X(e^{j\omega})$ convolved with the window spectrum $W(e^{j\omega})$, which is precisely what produces **spectral leakage**. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > All questions below are modeled strictly on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern: 3-mark short answers in Part A and 14-mark long answers in Part B with **module-internal choice** (Module 1 Ōåö Module 1). --- ## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. Define windowing in the context of speech signal analysis. Why is it necessary?** *`[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2024]`* *(Module 1 | CO1 | Bloom: Remember/Understand | 3 marks)* **Model Answer:** Windowing is the process of multiplying a long, non-stationary speech signal $x[n]$ by a finite-duration weighting function $w[n]$ of length $N$ samples, yielding $x_w[n] = x[n] \cdot w[n]$ for $0 \le n \le N-1$. It is necessary because: 1. Speech is **quasi-stationary** ŌĆö its statistical properties (energy, spectrum) are approximately constant only over **20ŌĆō40 ms** intervals. Windowing isolates these short stationary segments so that classical DSP tools (DFT, autocorrelation, LPC) remain valid. 2. The DFT assumes **periodic extension** of the input. Without tapering, an abrupt truncation produces severe **spectral leakage** ŌĆö energy from one frequency bin "leaks" into adjacent bins, distorting the spectrum. 3. Windowing enables the **Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT)**, the foundation of every speech feature extractor (spectrogram, MFCC, mel-filterbank). > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall:** Students often write *"windowing removes noise."* This is **incorrect**. Windowing selects a segment and controls leakage; **noise reduction is a separate stage** (Wiener filtering, spectral subtraction, or deep-learning denoisers). --- ### **Q2. State and briefly explain three desirable properties of a window function used in speech processing.** *`[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`* *(Module 1 | CO1 | Bloom: Understand | 3 marks)* **Model Answer:** The three desirable properties are: 1. **Narrow Main Lobe Width** ŌĆö The window's frequency response should have a narrow main lobe so that two closely spaced sinusoids in the input signal appear as two distinct peaks in the spectrum. The main-lobe width is inversely proportional to $N$ (e.g., $2/N$ for rectangular, $4/N$ for Hamming). 2. **Low Side-Lobe Level** ŌĆö Side lobes of the window's frequency response cause weak frequency components to be masked by strong ones (spectral leakage). A low peak side-lobe level (e.g., $-41$ dB for Hamming, $-57$ dB for Blackman) reduces this masking. 3. **High Side-Lobe Roll-off Rate** ŌĆö The rate at which side-lobe amplitudes decay (in dB per octave) determines how quickly the leakage dies away. A fast roll-off (e.g., $-18$ dB/oct for Hanning) is preferred because leakage from a strong tone becomes negligible within a few bins. > **Supplementary note (if asked):** A fourth desirable property is **linear phase** ŌĆö most standard windows (Rectangular, Hamming, Hanning, Blackman) are *symmetric* and therefore produce only a constant group delay, no phase distortion. --- ## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Note:** Per KTU 2024 regulations, the ESE paper offers an **internal choice within a module**. The two questions below are independent alternatives; the student attempts **one**. --- ### **QUESTION A ŌĆö 14 Marks** *`[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2024]`* *(Module 1 | CO1, CO2 | Bloom: Apply/Analyze | 14 marks)* **(a)** With the help of a neat mathematical derivation, explain the concept of **windowing** as a time-domain multiplication operation. State the **multiplicationŌĆōconvolution duality** that links the time-domain product $x[n] \cdot w[n]$ to its frequency-domain behavior. Define the terms *spectral leakage* and *main lobe width* in this context. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **DTFT of the Rectangular window** of length $N$, $w_R[n] = 1$ for $0 \le n \le N-1$. Identify the **first null**, the **peak side-lobe location**, and the **side-lobe level in dB**. Comment on why the rectangular window gives the **best frequency resolution but the worst spectral leakage**. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a) **Step 1.** The windowed finite-length segment is defined as $$ x_w[n] = x[n] \cdot w[n], \qquad 0 \le n \le N-1 $$ where $w[n]$ is zero outside the window interval. This is a **pointwise (Hadamard) product** in the discrete-time domain. **Step 2.** The DTFT of the windowed signal is $$ X_w(e^{j\omega}) = \text{DTFT}\{x[n] w[n]\} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\theta}) \, W(e^{j(\omega - \theta)}) \, d\theta $$ This is **periodic convolution** between the true spectrum $X(e^{j\omega})$ and the window's spectrum $W(e^{j\omega})$. **Step 3.** Define *main lobe width*: the frequency interval between the first two zeros of $\vert W(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ on either side of the central peak. For the rectangular window, this is $\Delta\omega = \dfrac{2\pi}{N}$ rad/sample, or equivalently $\dfrac{2}{N}$ in normalized bins. **Step 4.** Define *spectral leakage*: the appearance of spurious spectral energy in frequency bins that ideally should contain zero signal. It is caused by the side lobes of $W(e^{j\omega})$ smearing the true spectrum during the convolution operation. > **Engineering takeaway:** A "good" window makes $W(e^{j\omega})$ as close to a Dirac delta as possible ŌĆö narrow main lobe (good resolution) and minimal side-lobe energy (low leakage). #### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) **Step 1.** Define the rectangular window: $$ w_R[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 \le n \le N-1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ **Step 2.** Compute its DTFT as a finite geometric series: $$ W_R(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{-j\omega n} = \frac{1 - e^{-j\omega N}}{1 - e^{-j\omega}} $$ **Step 3.** Symmetrize numerator and denominator by multiplying top and bottom by $e^{j\omega N/2}$ and $e^{j\omega/2}$ respectively: $$ W_R(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{e^{-j\omega N/2}\left(e^{j\omega N/2} - e^{-j\omega N/2}\right)}{e^{-j\omega/2}\left(e^{j\omega/2} - e^{-j\omega/2}\right)} = \frac{\sin(\omega N/2)}{\sin(\omega/2)} \cdot e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2} $$ **Step 4.** The magnitude is the **Dirichlet kernel**: $$ \vert W_R(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \left\vert \frac{\sin(\omega N/2)}{\sin(\omega/2)} \right\vert $$ **Step 5.** Identify the **first null**: set $\sin(\omega N/2) = 0$ with $\omega \neq 0$: $$ \omega_1 = \frac{2\pi}{N} \implies f_1 = \frac{1}{N} \text{ (cycles/sample)} $$ The main lobe width (null-to-null) is therefore $\Delta\omega = \dfrac{4\pi}{N}$ rad/sample, or **$2$ bins**. **Step 6.** The **peak side lobe** occurs at $\omega \approx \dfrac{3\pi}{N}$. Using the small-angle approximation for the denominator: $$ \left\vert W_R \right\vert_{\text{side}} \approx \left\vert \frac{1}{\frac{3\pi}{2N}} \right\vert = \frac{2N}{3\pi} \cdot \frac{1}{N} = \frac{2}{3\pi} \approx 0.212 $$ The main lobe peak is $N$. So the ratio is $$ 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.212}{1}\right) \approx -13 \text{ dB} $$ The **first side lobe is therefore $-13$ dB below the main lobe**. **Step 7.** Comment: The rectangular window has the **narrowest main lobe** ($2/N$ bins) and hence the **best frequency resolution** ŌĆö it can resolve two sinusoids separated by less than $4\pi/N$ rad/sample. However, its side lobes decay slowly (at $-6$ dB/octave) and are only $-13$ dB down, so any strong tone will leak heavily into neighbouring bins, smearing the spectrum of weak components. This is the canonical **resolutionŌĆōleakage trade-off**. --- ### **QUESTION B ŌĆö 14 Marks** *`[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`* *(Module 1 | CO1, CO2 | Bloom: Apply/Analyze | 14 marks)* **(a)** Compare the **Hamming**, **Hanning**, and **Blackman** windows. Tabulate the time-domain equations and the key spectral properties (main lobe width, peak side-lobe level, roll-off). State one specific speech-processing application where each is preferred. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A speech signal is sampled at $f_s = 16$ kHz. Frames of length **$N = 400$ samples (25 ms)** are extracted using a **Hamming window**. For a pure tone at $f_0 = 1$ kHz, calculate the **expected main-lobe width (in Hz)** of the spectral peak, and the **peak side-lobe attenuation in dB** below the main lobe. Briefly explain how the choice of window affects the design of a **speech pitch tracker**. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a) **Step 1 ŌĆö Time-domain equations (to be tabulated in the answer):** | Window | Equation $w[n]$, $0 \le n \le N-1$ | |---|---| | Hamming | $0.54 - 0.46 \cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right)$ | | Hanning | $0.5 - 0.5 \cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right)$ | | Blackman | $0.42 - 0.5 \cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right) + 0.08 \cos\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi n}{N-1}\right)$ | **Step 2 ŌĆö Spectral properties:** | Property | Hamming | Hanning | Blackman | |---|---|---|---| | Main lobe width (bins) | $4/N$ | $4/N$ | $6/N$ | | Peak side-lobe (dB) | $-41$ | $-31$ | $-57$ | | Roll-off (dB/oct) | $-6$ | $-18$ | $-18$ | | Edge value | $0.08$ (non-zero) | $0$ (zero) | $0$ (zero) | **Step 3 ŌĆö Preferred applications:** - **Hamming** ŌĆö Preferred for **real-time ASR (Automatic Speech Recognition)** front-ends and **overlap-add STFT** systems. Reason: it does **not** touch zero at the frame edges, so consecutive overlapped frames can be linearly combined (overlap-add) without amplitude dips, while still suppressing leakage to $-41$ dB. - **Hanning** ŌĆö Preferred for **pitch estimation** and **musical pitch analysis** where the zero-edge property eliminates end-point discontinuities, and the $-18$ dB/oct roll-off is sufficient for voiced-speech harmonic tracking. - **Blackman** ŌĆö Preferred for **laboratory-grade spectral measurements** of speech (e.g., formant tracking, glottal-source analysis) where leakage below $-57$ dB is required to resolve weak formants near a strong harmonic. #### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) **Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the frequency resolution (main-lobe width) for the Hamming window.** The null-to-null main-lobe width of the Hamming window in bins is $$ \Delta k = \frac{4}{N} \text{ (in normalized DFT bins)} $$ Converting to physical frequency: $$ \Delta f = \Delta k \cdot \frac{f_s}{N} = \frac{4}{N} \cdot \frac{f_s}{N} = \frac{4 f_s}{N^2} $$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Plug in $f_s = 16{,}000$ Hz and $N = 400$:** $$ \Delta f = \frac{4 \times 16{,}000}{400^2} = \frac{64{,}000}{160{,}000} = 0.4 \text{ Hz} $$ So the **expected main-lobe width of the 1 kHz spectral peak is $\Delta f \approx 0.4$ Hz**. > **Note:** This is the *minimum separable spacing*, not the -3 dB bandwidth. The -3 dB bandwidth of the Hamming main lobe is approximately $\Delta f_{3\text{dB}} \approx 0.86 \cdot \dfrac{2 f_s}{N} = 68.8$ Hz, which is the more practically quoted figure. **Step 3 ŌĆö Peak side-lobe level for Hamming.** From the formula table: $$ L_{\text{side}} = -41 \text{ dB} $$ So the first side lobe is **41 dB below the main peak**. For a 1 kHz tone of amplitude $A$, the first side-lobe ripple will have amplitude $A \cdot 10^{-41/20} \approx 0.0089 \, A$, i.e., about **0.9%** of the main peak. **Step 4 ŌĆö Effect on a speech pitch tracker.** A pitch tracker estimates the fundamental frequency $F_0$ (typically 80ŌĆō400 Hz for human speech) by locating harmonics in the short-time spectrum. The choice of window affects the tracker in three ways: 1. **Resolution ($N$ large, main lobe narrow):** A long window gives a narrow main lobe, allowing closely spaced harmonics to be resolved. For a male speaker with $F_0 = 100$ Hz, harmonics are 100 Hz apart; with $N=400$ and a Hamming window the 0.4 Hz null-to-null width easily resolves them. 2. **Leakage control:** If a pitch tracker mistakenly identifies a side lobe as a harmonic, it can halve or double the estimated $F_0$ (the **octave error**). The $-41$ dB attenuation of the Hamming window ensures that even the strongest harmonic's first side lobe is far below the weakest true harmonic, reducing octave errors. 3. **TimeŌĆōfrequency trade-off:** Longer windows ŌåÆ better $F_0$ resolution but poorer *time* resolution of pitch changes (e.g., intonation, emotion). A compromise: 25ŌĆō40 ms windows with **Hamming** for telephony-grade trackers, **Blackman** for offline high-precision trackers, and **Hann** for intermediate accuracy. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout ŌĆö Common Mark Losses:** > 1. **Confusing the main-lobe width formula:** Rectangular = $2/N$, Hamming/Hann = $4/N$, Blackman = $6/N$. Mixing these up costs **1ŌĆō2 marks**. > 2. **Forgetting the $f_s / N$ factor:** Students often quote $\Delta f$ in *bins* but the question demands **Hz**. Always multiply by $f_s / N$. > 3. **Side-lobe level units:** Use **dB**, not linear. Convert using $20 \log_{10}(\text{ratio})$ for voltage/amplitude ratios. > 4. **Time-domain vs. frequency-domain:** The question is about the *spectrum* of a windowed tone ŌĆö students often describe the *time* waveform instead and lose **2ŌĆō3 marks**. > 5. **Hamming vs. Hanning confusion:** Hamming is **0.54 / 0.46**, Hanning is **0.5 / 0.5**. They are NOT the same window. Mixing them up is an automatic deduction. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **High-density rapid-revision checklist ŌĆö read this 30 minutes before the exam.** - [x] **Core definition:** Windowing = $x_w[n] = x[n] \cdot w[n]$ over $0 \le n \le N-1$. - [x] **Why window?** Speech is quasi-stationary over 20ŌĆō40 ms ŌåÆ enable DFT-based localized analysis; reduce spectral leakage. - [x] **Duality:** Time-domain multiplication $\Leftrightarrow$ Frequency-domain periodic convolution. - [x] **Three trade-off parameters:** Main-lobe width (resolution), peak side-lobe level (leakage), roll-off rate (decay). - [x] **Rectangular window** $w[n]=1$: narrowest main lobe ($2/N$ bins), worst leakage ($-13$ dB), slow roll-off ($-6$ dB/oct). - [x] **Hanning window** $w[n] = 0.5 - 0.5\cos(2\pi n/(N-1))$: main lobe $4/N$, $-31$ dB, roll-off $-18$ dB/oct, zero at edges. - [x] **Hamming window** $w[n] = 0.54 - 0.46\cos(2\pi n/(N-1))$: main lobe $4/N$, $-41$ dB, roll-off $-6$ dB/oct, edge value $0.08$ (not zero). - [x] **Blackman window**: main lobe $6/N$, $-57$ dB, roll-off $-18$ dB/oct, zero at edges. - [x] **Kaiser window**: tunable via parameter $\beta$; uses Bessel function $I_0(\cdot)$. - [x] **DTFT of Rectangular window:** $W_R(e^{j\omega}) = \dfrac{\sin(\omega N/2)}{\sin(\omega/2)} \cdot e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2}$. - [x] **First null of rectangular:** $\omega_1 = 2\pi/N \Rightarrow f_1 = 1/N$ cycles/sample. - [x] **Conversion to Hz:** $\Delta f_{\text{Hz}} = (\Delta k) \cdot f_s / N$. - [x] **Standard speech parameters:** $f_s = 8$ kHz (telephony) or $16$ kHz (wideband), $N = 160$ŌĆō$640$ samples (20ŌĆō40 ms). - [x] **STFT uses:** Windowed frames shifted by $M$ samples (typically $M = N/2$ or $M = N/3$) ŌĆö overlap-add / overlap-save. - [x] **FIR filter design:** Windowed-sinc method ŌĆö ideal $h_d[n]$ multiplied by $w[n]$ to obtain causal, finite-length, linear-phase FIR coefficients. - [x] **Spectral leakage** arises because the *rectangular* time-domain truncation implicitly multiplies by a *sinc* in frequency. - [x] **Linear phase:** Symmetric windows (Rectangular, Hamming, Hanning, Blackman) all give **linear phase** with constant group delay $(N-1)/2$.

topics.title: STFT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST866 - SPEECH AND AUDIO PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** STFT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT)** is a fundamental time-frequency analysis technique used in speech and audio processing to analyze how the frequency content of a non-stationary signal evolves over time. Mathematically, the continuous-time STFT of a signal $x(t)$ is defined as:

$$
X(\tau, \omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, w(t - \tau)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt
$$

where:
- $x(t)$ ŌĆö the input (speech/audio) signal under analysis
- $w(t - \tau)$ ŌĆö a window function centered at time $\tau$
- $\tau$ ŌĆö time-shift parameter (location of the analysis window on the time axis)
- $\omega$ ŌĆö angular frequency variable
- $e^{-j\omega t}$ ŌĆö complex exponential kernel inherited from the classical Fourier Transform

The STFT converts a **one-dimensional time-domain signal** $x(t)$ into a **two-dimensional function of time and frequency** $X(\tau, \omega)$, thereby revealing the local spectral characteristics of the signal at every instant $\tau$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The STFT is the cornerstone of almost every modern speech analysis algorithm, including spectrograms, mel-spectrograms, voice activity detection, pitch estimation, and feature extraction (MFCCs). Mastering STFT is **mandatory** for this module.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are reading a long book printed in a microscopic font, and you have a small magnifying glass. You cannot read the entire page at once, so you slide the magnifying glass from left to right. At each position, the glass allows you to read only a small portion (a few words) clearly. The magnifying glass is your **window function** $w(t - \tau)$, and as you slide it, you are effectively performing an STFT.

- The **width of the glass** = window length $N$
- The **position of the glass** = time-shift $\tau$
- The **text size you can read** = frequency resolution $\Delta f$
- The **text you can see at each instant** = time resolution $\Delta t$

If you use a **wide magnifying glass**, you read more text at once (better frequency resolution), but you cannot tell exactly *where* in the page a particular word appears (poorer time resolution). Conversely, a **narrow glass** gives you precise location information but each character looks blurry in the frequency domain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The HeisenbergŌĆōGabor Uncertainty Principle for Signals:** For any STFT implementation, the product of time resolution and frequency resolution is bounded below:
> $$\Delta t \cdot \Delta f \geq \frac{1}{4\pi}$$
> This means you **cannot** simultaneously achieve arbitrarily high resolution in both time and frequency. Choosing a window length is always a compromise.

## 1.3 Discrete-Time Formulation

Since real-world speech and audio are sampled, we use the **Discrete STFT (DSTFT)**:

$$
X(m, k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n + mH)\, w(n)\, e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}
$$

where:
- $m$ ŌĆö frame index (discrete time-shift)
- $k$ ŌĆö discrete frequency bin index, $k = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$
- $N$ ŌĆö window length (in samples)
- $H$ ŌĆö hop size (samples between successive frames)
- $w(n)$ ŌĆö discrete window function of length $N$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Parameters in Speech Processing (KTU Lab Context):**
> - Sampling rate $f_s$ = **16 kHz** (wideband) or **8 kHz** (narrowband telephony)
> - Window length $N$ = **20ŌĆō40 ms** (e.g., 320 samples @ 16 kHz)
> - Hop size $H$ = **50% overlap** typical, i.e., $H = N/2$
> - Window type = **Hamming** or **Hann** (preferred over rectangular for speech)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet
