
> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide:**
> - The **Linear STFT** has equal frequency spacing (e.g., 100 Hz between consecutive bins), wasting resolution at high frequencies.
> - The **Perceptual Filter Bank** has **wide bands at low frequencies** (preserving fine pitch harmonics) and **wide spacing at high frequencies** (where the ear is less sensitive). This matches critical-band theory.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define critical band. Explain why the human auditory system uses a non-uniform filter bank rather than a uniform one.**

**Model Answer:**

A **critical band** is the frequency range within the human auditory system over which a second tone begins to mask the first tone. It represents the simultaneous frequency-analyzing resolution of the ear.

The human auditory system uses a **non-uniform filter bank** because:
1. **Psychoacoustic experiments** (Zwicker, 1961) show critical bandwidth increases with center frequency.
2. At low frequencies (~500 Hz), the critical bandwidth is ~100 Hz; at high frequencies (~4 kHz), it is ~600 Hz.
3. A uniform filter bank would waste resolution at high frequencies and provide insufficient resolution at low frequencies where speech information density is highest (formants, pitch harmonics).
4. This non-uniformity motivates the use of **Bark and Mel scales**, which map linear Hz to a perceptually-meaningful scale.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for explanation)*

---

#### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between Bark scale and Mel scale. State the formula relating frequency in Hz to Bark.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Bark Scale | Mel Scale |
|---------|-----------|-----------|
| **Origin** | Zwicker (1961) ŌĆö masking experiments | Stevens & Volkman (1940) ŌĆö pitch perception |
| **Reference** | 1 kHz Ōēł 8.5 Bark | 1 kHz = 1000 Mel |
| **Use** | PLP analysis, vocoders, MP3 masking | MFCC, speech recognition |
| **Physical basis** | Critical bandwidth for masking | Subjective pitch doubling |

**Formula (Hz ŌåÆ Bark):**

$$
z = \frac{26.81 \cdot f}{1960 + f} - 0.53
$$

where $f$ is in Hz and $z$ is in Bark.

*(3 marks: 2 marks differentiation + 1 mark formula)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks ŌĆö Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (Choice 1)** ŌĆö 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the process of filter bank analysis for speech feature extraction. List the steps and justify why each is required. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the Bark and Mel scale values for the following frequencies: 500 Hz, 1 kHz, 2 kHz, 4 kHz. Comment on the non-linear behavior of both scales. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The filter bank analysis process for speech feature extraction follows these steps:

1. **Pre-emphasis Filtering:** Boosts high-frequency energy that is naturally attenuated during speech production. Transfer function: $H(z) = 1 - 0.97 z^{-1}$.

2. **Frame Blocking:** Speech is segmented into overlapping frames of 20ŌĆō30 ms duration (typically 25 ms with 10 ms hop). Short frames are required because speech is **quasi-stationary** only over short intervals.

3. **Windowing:** Each frame is multiplied by a Hamming/Hanning window to reduce spectral leakage:
   $$w(n) = 0.54 - 0.46 \cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right), \quad 0 \le n \le N-1$$

4. **FFT Computation:** $N$-point FFT (e.g., $N = 512$) converts the time-domain frame to the frequency domain, producing $X(k)$.

5. **Mel/Bark Filter Bank Application:** The magnitude spectrum $|X(k)|$ is passed through a bank of $M$ triangular bandpass filters spaced uniformly on a Mel or Bark scale:
   $$Y(m) = \sum_{k} |X(k)| \cdot H_m(k), \quad m = 1, 2, \ldots, M$$

6. **Logarithmic Compression:** Takes the logarithm of filter bank energies: $\tilde{Y}(m) = \log(Y(m))$. This models the ear's intensity perception (Weber-Fechner law).

7. **Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT):** Decorrelates the log-filter-bank energies into cepstral coefficients (MFCCs):
   $$c_n = \sum_{m=1}^{M} \tilde{Y}(m) \cos\left(\frac{\pi n (m-0.5)}{M}\right), \quad n = 1, 2, \ldots, L$$

The output is an $L$-dimensional feature vector (e.g., 13 MFCCs) used for ASR, speaker ID, etc. *(7 marks: 1 for diagram + 4 for steps + 2 for justification)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Bark Scale Computation:** Using $z = \dfrac{26.81 f}{1960 + f} - 0.53$

| Frequency (Hz) | Computation | Bark value |
|:--------------:|:-----------:|:----------:|
| 500 | $\frac{26.81 \times 500}{1960+500} - 0.53 = \frac{13405}{2460} - 0.53 = 5.45 - 0.53$ | **4.92 Bark** |
| 1000 | $\frac{26.81 \times 1000}{1960+1000} - 0.53 = \frac{26810}{2960} - 0.53 = 9.06 - 0.53$ | **8.53 Bark** |
| 2000 | $\frac{26.81 \times 2000}{1960+2000} - 0.53 = \frac{53620}{3960} - 0.53 = 13.54 - 0.53$ | **13.01 Bark** |
| 4000 | $\frac{26.81 \times 4000}{1960+4000} - 0.53 = \frac{107240}{5960} - 0.53 = 17.99 - 0.53$ | **17.46 Bark** |

**Mel Scale Computation:** Using $m = 2595 \cdot \log_{10}\left(1 + \dfrac{f}{700}\right)$

| Frequency (Hz) | Computation | Mel value |
|:--------------:|:-----------:|:---------:|
| 500 | $2595 \cdot \log_{10}(1 + 0.714) = 2595 \cdot 0.2041$ | **529.6 Mel** |
| 1000 | $2595 \cdot \log_{10}(2.428) = 2595 \cdot 0.3852$ | **999.6 Mel** |
| 2000 | $2595 \cdot \log_{10}(3.857) = 2595 \cdot 0.5864$ | **1521.7 Mel** |
| 4000 | $2595 \cdot \log_{10}(6.714) = 2595 \cdot 0.8270$ | **2146.1 Mel** |

**Comment on Non-linearity:**

- The **Bark scale** grows from 4.92 ŌåÆ 8.53 ŌåÆ 13.01 ŌåÆ 17.46 Bark as frequency quadruples from 500 Hz to 4 kHz. The increment is *decreasing* in Bark per Hz at high frequencies, indicating compressed high-frequency resolution.
- The **Mel scale** grows from 529.6 ŌåÆ 999.6 ŌåÆ 1521.7 ŌåÆ 2146.1 Mel. Same trend.
- Both scales confirm the **logarithmic nature of human pitch perception** and justify using a non-uniform filter bank in speech processing. *(7 marks: 3 marks Bark + 3 marks Mel + 1 mark comment)*

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2)** ŌĆö 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Define the following frequency-domain parameters of speech: (i) Pitch (ii) Formant (iii) Spectral Tilt. For each, state the typical range for adult male speakers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vowel has formants at F1 = 500 Hz, F2 = 1500 Hz, F3 = 2500 Hz. Estimate the vocal tract length using the lossless tube model. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**(i) Pitch (Fundamental Frequency, FŌéĆ):**
Pitch is the rate at which the vocal folds vibrate during voiced speech production, perceived by the listener as the "highness" or "lowness" of a voice. Mathematically, it is the **fundamental frequency** ŌĆö the lowest frequency component in the harmonic series of voiced speech.

**Typical range (adult male):** **80 ŌĆō 160 Hz**

**(ii) Formant:**
A formant is a **resonant frequency** of the vocal tract that amplifies specific harmonics of the glottal source. Formants are seen as **spectral peaks** in the short-time spectrum of voiced speech. They are the primary acoustic cues for **vowel identity** (F1 and F2) and **consonant place of articulation**.

**Typical range (adult male):**
- F1: **300 ŌĆō 800 Hz**
- F2: **800 ŌĆō 2500 Hz**
- F3: **2000 ŌĆō 3500 Hz**

**(iii) Spectral Tilt:**
Spectral tilt measures the **rate of decrease of harmonic amplitudes** with increasing frequency. It is computed as the dB difference between the first and second harmonics (H1 ŌĆō H2). It characterizes the **glottal source** and indicates voice quality (breathy, modal, pressed).

**Typical range (adult male, modal voice):** **ŌłÆ2 dB to ŌłÆ12 dB** (negative = harmonics decrease with frequency).

*(7 marks: 1.5 marks per parameter for definition + 1 mark per range)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Lossless Tube Model Assumption:**
The vocal tract is modeled as a uniform lossless tube of length $L$, closed at the glottis and open at the lips. The resonant frequencies (formants) are given by:

$$
F_n = \frac{(2n-1) \cdot v}{4L}
$$

where:
- $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ (formant index)
- $v$ = velocity of sound in air = **343 m/s** (at 20┬░C)
- $L$ = length of the vocal tract

**Rearranging for $L$:**

$$
L = \frac{(2n-1) \cdot v}{4 \cdot F_n}
$$

**Compute $L$ for each formant:**

- Using F1 = 500 Hz, $n = 1$:
  $$L = \frac{(2(1)-1) \cdot 343}{4 \cdot 500} = \frac{1 \cdot 343}{2000} = 0.1715 \text{ m} = 17.15 \text{ cm}$$

- Using F2 = 1500 Hz, $n = 2$:
  $$L = \frac{(2(2)-1) \cdot 343}{4 \cdot 1500} = \frac{3 \cdot 343}{6000} = \frac{1029}{6000} = 0.1715 \text{ m} = 17.15 \text{ cm}$$

- Using F3 = 2500 Hz, $n = 3$:
  $$L = \frac{(2(3)-1) \cdot 343}{4 \cdot 2500} = \frac{5 \cdot 343}{10000} = \frac{1715}{10000} = 0.1715 \text{ m} = 17.15 \text{ cm}$$

**Comment:**
All three formants yield the same vocal tract length $L \approx 17.15$ cm, which is consistent with the **average adult male vocal tract length (17 cm)**. This confirms the validity of the **uniform lossless tube model** for vowel acoustics. In practice, the vocal tract is non-uniform (varying cross-section), which is why real formants deviate slightly from this idealized formula.

*(7 marks: 2 marks model statement + 3 marks computation + 1 mark interpretation + 1 mark comment)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Bark and Mel units** ŌĆö Bark has no unit symbol (it is a rate), while Mel is denoted explicitly. Students often write "Mel = 1.5 kHz" which is wrong. Always write "8.5 Bark" or "1000 Mel."
> 2. **Forgetting to state velocity of sound** ŌĆö In the lossless tube derivation, the constant $v = 343$ m/s must be **explicitly mentioned** for full marks.
> 3. **Wrong log base** ŌĆö Mel formula uses $\log_{10}$ (base 10), NOT natural log $\ln$. Writing $2595 \cdot \ln(1 + f/700)$ gives a different (and incorrect) value unless you adjust the constant.
> 4. **Missing pre-emphasis in the block diagram** ŌĆö Examiners expect a complete pipeline. Skipping pre-emphasis or windowing costs easy marks.
> 5. **Not justifying "why"** ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes **understanding over memorization**. Simply writing formulas without explaining the *psychoacoustic motivation* (why non-uniform, why Mel, why Bark) loses 1ŌĆō2 marks per sub-question.
> 6. **Units inconsistency** ŌĆö Hz vs kHz in critical bandwidth formula. The formula $BW = 25 + 75(1+1.4f^2)^{0.69}$ requires $f$ in **kHz**. Mixing units is a frequent error.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- Ō£ģ **Critical band** = psychoacoustic frequency-analyzing resolution of the ear; bandwidth widens with frequency.
- Ō£ģ **Bark scale formula:** $z = \dfrac{26.81 f}{1960+f} - 0.53$, with 1 kHz Ōēł 8.5 Bark; total ~24 bands over 0ŌĆō16 kHz.
- Ō£ģ **Mel scale formula:** $m = 2595 \log_{10}(1 + f/700)$, with 1 kHz = 1000 Mel.
- Ō£ģ **Pitch (FŌéĆ)** range: Male 80ŌĆō160 Hz, Female 150ŌĆō300 Hz, Child 200ŌĆō400 Hz.
- Ō£ģ **Formants** are vocal tract resonances; F1 (300ŌĆō800 Hz) and F2 (800ŌĆō2500 Hz) identify vowels.
- Ō£ģ **Lossless tube model:** $F_n = (2n-1) v / (4L)$ with $v = 343$ m/s, $L \approx 17$ cm (male).
- Ō£ģ **Uniform filter bank** = equal bandwidth in Hz (poor perceptual match); **non-uniform** = Bark/Mel spacing (mimics ear).
- Ō£ģ **Spectral tilt** = dB difference between H1 and H2; negative value indicates modal voice.
- Ō£ģ **MP3** uses a 32-band polyphase filter bank; **MFCC** uses 20ŌĆō40 triangular Mel filters.
- Ō£ģ **Block diagram order:** Pre-emphasis ŌåÆ Frame blocking ŌåÆ Windowing ŌåÆ FFT ŌåÆ Filter bank ŌåÆ Log ŌåÆ DCT ŌåÆ Features.
- Ō£ģ **Filter bank advantages:** Perceptual relevance, dimensionality reduction, noise robustness in ASR.
- Ō£ģ **Difference between critical band and Mel filter bank:** Critical band = psychoacoustic; Mel filter bank = its engineering approximation using triangular filters.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: STFT Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST866 - SPEECH AND AUDIO PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** STFT Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT)** is a Fourier-related transform used to determine the sinusoidal frequency and phase content of local sections of a non-stationary signal as it changes over time. Mathematically, the STFT of a discrete-time signal $x[n]$ is computed by multiplying the signal by a short sliding window $w[n]$ and computing the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) of the windowed segment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):**
> The STFT is defined as:
> $$X_m(\omega) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \cdot w[n-m] \cdot e^{-j\omega n}$$
> where $w[n-m]$ is a window function shifted by $m$ samples. The result is a 2D complex-valued function representing the signal's frequency content at different time instants.

The STFT essentially transforms a 1D time-domain signal $x[n]$ into a 2D time-frequency representation $X_m(\omega)$, where:
- **Horizontal axis (m):** Represents the *time* localization index (window position).
- **Vertical axis (Žē):** Represents the *angular frequency* content of the signal segment.
- **Magnitude $\vert X_m(\omega) \vert$:** Represents the *energy* present in frequency $\omega$ at time instant $m$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from DFT:**
> The standard DFT assumes the signal is **stationary** (statistical properties do not change with time). Speech and audio signals are **non-stationary**. STFT relaxes this by applying the DFT over short, quasi-stationary segments.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are watching a long musical concert through a small **sliding window** (like a moving picture frame). At each instant, you only see a small slice of the performance through this window.

- The **slide of the window** = advancing time index $m$.
- The **view through the window** = the local signal segment $x[n] \cdot w[n-m]$.
- The **DFT of what you see** = identifying the musical notes (frequencies) being played in that moment.
- The **entire recording** of all window views combined = the **spectrogram**, a complete "musical map" showing *when* each note was played.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a window is needed:**
> Without a window, you would try to analyze the entire 3-hour concert at once ŌĆö and you could only say "it has frequencies from 20 Hz to 20 kHz" (a single DFT of the whole signal), losing all information about *when* specific notes were played. The window localizes the analysis in time.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Sampling Rate ($F_s$):** Standard CD-quality audio = **44,100 Hz**; Speech telephony = **8,000 Hz** or **16,000 Hz**.
- **Window Length ($N$):** Typically **20 ms ŌĆō 40 ms** for speech (Ōēł160ŌĆō640 samples at $F_s = 16$ kHz). This is the quasi-stationary duration of speech.
- **Hop Size / Frame Shift ($R$):** Usually $N/2$ or $N/4$ for **50% or 75% overlap** between consecutive windows.
- **Frequency Resolution:** $\Delta f = F_s / N$ (in Hz).
- **Time Resolution:** $\Delta t = R / F_s$ (in seconds).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heisenberg-Gabor Uncertainty Principle (KTU Frequently Asked):**
> For STFT, $\Delta t \cdot \Delta f \geq \frac{1}{4\pi}$. You **cannot simultaneously** achieve arbitrarily fine time AND frequency resolution. A longer window gives better frequency resolution but worse time resolution, and vice versa.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of STFT Computation

The STFT operation is decomposed into a sequence of logical steps:

- **Step 1: Frame Extraction (Windowing)**
  - Select a segment of the input signal $x[n]$ of length $N$ samples, centered at (or starting at) time index $m$.
  - Multiply point-wise by the window function $w[n]$ to taper the segment's edges to zero, avoiding spectral leakage.

- **Step 2: Spectral Analysis (DFT)**
  - Compute the DFT of the windowed segment. This yields $N$ complex spectral coefficients representing amplitude and phase at $N$ discrete frequencies.
  - Frequency axis is uniformly spaced from $0$ to $F_s$ (or $-\pi$ to $\pi$ in normalized radians).

- **Step 3: Window Shift (Hop)**
  - Slide the window forward by $R$ samples ($R < N$ usually, to allow overlap).
  - Repeat Steps 1ŌĆō2 for the new window position.
  - This produces a 2D matrix where rows = frequencies, columns = time frames.

- **Step 4: Spectrogram Assembly**
  - Compute the squared magnitude $\vert X_m(\omega) \vert^2$ for each frame.
  - Arrange these magnitude spectra in chronological order to form the spectrogram.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why window the signal?**
  Multiplying by $w[n]$ makes the segment appear periodic, reducing the discontinuities at segment boundaries that would otherwise cause **spectral leakage** (energy spreading across frequencies).

- **Why use overlapping windows?**
  Without overlap, transient events occurring near window boundaries could be missed. Overlap ensures no signal content is "lost" between frames and produces smoother spectrograms.

- **Why short windows (20ŌĆō40 ms)?**
  Speech signals are **quasi-stationary** over intervals of 10ŌĆō30 ms. Within this interval, vocal tract shape and source characteristics remain relatively constant, making the DFT a meaningful representation.

### 2.3 Common Window Functions Used in STFT

| Window Name | Time-Domain Definition $w[n]$ (for $0 \leq n \leq N-1$) | Main Lobe Width | Side Lobe Level |
|-------------|--------------------------------------------------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| Rectangular | $w[n] = 1$ | Narrowest ($4\pi/N$) | **ŌłÆ13 dB** (worst) |
| Hann | $w[n] = 0.5 - 0.5\cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right)$ | $8\pi/N$ | **ŌłÆ31 dB** |
| Hamming | $w[n] = 0.54 - 0.46\cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right)$ | $8\pi/N$ | **ŌłÆ41 dB** |
| Blackman | $w[n] = 0.42 - 0.5\cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right) + 0.08\cos\left(\frac{4\pi n}{N-1}\right)$ | $12\pi/N$ | **ŌłÆ58 dB** (best) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Trade-off Interpretation:**
> - **Narrow main lobe** ŌåÆ Better frequency resolution (can distinguish close frequencies).
> - **Low side lobes** ŌåÆ Less spectral leakage (energy stays in the true frequency).
> - You CANNOT have both at once. A wider main lobe implies better side-lobe suppression.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Remarks |
|---|---------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | Continuous-Time STFT | $X(\tau, \omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \cdot w(t-\tau) \cdot e^{-j\omega t}\,dt$ | Time $\tau$ (s), Freq $\omega$ (rad/s) |
| 2 | Discrete-Time STFT | $X_m(\omega) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \cdot w[n-m] \cdot e^{-j\omega n}$ | $m$ = time index (samples) |
| 3 | STFT as Filter Bank | $X_m(\omega_k) = e^{-j\omega_k m} \cdot \big(x[n] \cdot w[n-m] \big)$ evaluated at $\omega_k$ | Each $\omega_k$ acts as bandpass filter |
| 4 | Spectrogram | $S_m(\omega_k) = \vert X_m(\omega_k) \vert^2$ | Power spectral density per frame |
| 5 | Frequency Resolution | $\Delta f = F_s / N$ | Hz (inversely proportional to $N$) |
| 6 | Time Resolution | $\Delta t = R / F_s$ | Seconds (proportional to $R$) |
| 7 | Uncertainty Principle | $\Delta t \cdot \Delta f \geq \frac{1}{4\pi}$ | Fundamental limit of STFT |
| 8 | Overlap Percentage | $\text{Overlap} = \left(1 - \frac{R}{N}\right) \times 100\%$ | 50% overlap ŌćÆ $R = N/2$ |
| 9 | Number of Frames | $N_f = \left\lfloor \frac{L - N}{R} \right\rfloor + 1$ | $L$ = total signal length |
| 10 | Inverse STFT (Synthesis) | $x[n] = \frac{\sum_m w[n-m] \cdot X_m(\omega)}{\sum_m w^2[n-m]}$ | Requires overlap-add condition |
| 11 | Condition for Perfect Reconstruction | $\sum_{m} w[n-m] \cdot \tilde{w}[n-m] = 1$ | Synthesis window $\tilde{w}$ corrects for windowing |
| 12 | Total Energy Relation | $\sum_{n} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sum_m \int \vert X_m(\omega) \vert^2\,d\omega$ | Parseval-like relation for STFT |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Speech Recognition (ASR):** STFT-based MFCC (Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients) features are the backbone of modern speech recognition systems (e.g., Google Assistant, Siri).
- **Audio Codecs:** MP3, AAC, and Opus all use STFT (or MDCT, a related lapped transform) to compress audio.
- **Music Information Retrieval:** Identifying notes, chord progressions, and tempo from spectrograms.
- **Noise Reduction:** Spectral subtraction operates on STFT magnitude to remove stationary background noise.
- **Medical Signal Processing:** Analyzing phonocardiograms (heart sounds) and EEG signals.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: STFT as a Bandpass Filter Bank

The STFT can be interpreted as the output of a parallel bank of bandpass filters. We derive this equivalence.

**Starting Point:** The discrete STFT definition:

$$X_m(\omega_k) = \sum_{n} x[n] \cdot w[n-m] \cdot e^{-j\omega_k n}$$

**Step 1: Substitute variable change.** Let $n' = n - m$ so that $n = n' + m$. The summation becomes:

$$X_m(\omega_k) = \sum_{n'} x[n' + m] \cdot w[n'] \cdot e^{-j\omega_k (n' + m)}$$

**Step 2: Factor out the time-shift phase term.** The exponential splits as $e^{-j\omega_k n'} \cdot e^{-j\omega_k m}$:

$$X_m(\omega_k) = e^{-j\omega_k m} \cdot \sum_{n'} \big(x[n'+m] \cdot w[n'] \cdot e^{-j\omega_k n'}\big)$$

**Step 3: Recognize the convolution structure.** Define the impulse response of the $k$-th bandpass filter as:

$$h_k[n] = w[n] \cdot e^{j\omega_k n}$$

**Step 4: Formulate the filter output.** The output of filtering $x[n]$ through $h_k[-n]$ gives:

$$y_k[m] = \sum_{n'} x[m - n'] \cdot h_k[n'] = (x * h_k)[m]$$

**Step 5: Final interpretation.** Therefore:

$$X_m(\omega_k) = e^{-j\omega_k m} \cdot y_k[m]$$

> This shows that $X_m(\omega_k)$ is the output of a bandpass filter (with impulse response $h_k[n] = w[n] e^{j\omega_k n}$) applied to $x[n]$, multiplied by a pure phase term $e^{-j\omega_k m}$ for time alignment.

### 3.2 Derivation: Time-Frequency Resolution Trade-off

**Given:** A windowed sinusoid $x[n] = A \cos(\omega_0 n)$ observed through a window $w[n]$ of length $N$.

**Step 1: Windowed signal.**
$$x_w[n] = A \cos(\omega_0 n) \cdot w[n]$$

**Step 2: Apply the DTFT.**
$$X_w(\omega) = \frac{A}{2} \big[W(\omega - \omega_0) + W(\omega + \omega_0)\big]$$

This shows the spectrum is the window spectrum $W(\omega)$ shifted to $\pm\omega_0$.

**Step 3: Main lobe width of $W(\omega)$.** For a rectangular window, the main lobe width is $\frac{4\pi}{N}$ (between first zero crossings). For a Hamming window, it is $\frac{8\pi}{N}$ in general.

**Step 4: Frequency resolution.** Two sinusoids at $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$ are resolvable only if their main lobes do not overlap significantly. This requires:

$$\vert \omega_1 - \omega_2 \vert > \frac{4\pi}{N} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta f_{\min} = \frac{2 F_s}{N}$$

**Step 5: Time resolution.** A longer window means the analysis is averaged over a longer time segment:

$$\Delta t = \frac{N}{F_s} \quad \text{(in seconds)}$$

**Step 6: Uncertainty Principle derivation.** The window's effective duration and bandwidth satisfy:

$$\sigma_t^2 = \frac{\sum_n n^2 \vert w[n] \vert^2}{\sum_n \vert w[n] \vert^2}, \quad \sigma_\omega^2 = \frac{\int \omega^2 \vert W(\omega) \vert^2 d\omega}{\int \vert W(\omega) \vert^2 d\omega}$$

By the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, $\sigma_t \cdot \sigma_\omega \geq \frac{1}{2}$, which in discrete time translates to:

$$\Delta t \cdot \Delta f \geq \frac{1}{4\pi}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This is a fundamental physical limit (analogous to the Heisenberg uncertainty principle in quantum mechanics). It is not a limitation of STFT design ŌĆö it is a property of signal analysis itself.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A speech signal sampled at $F_s = 16{,}000$ Hz is analyzed using STFT with:
- Window length $N = 512$ samples
- Hop size $R = 256$ samples
- Hamming window

**Compute (a) Frequency resolution, (b) Time resolution, (c) Overlap percentage, (d) Number of frames for a 2-second signal.**

**Solution:**

**(a) Frequency Resolution:**

$$\Delta f = \frac{F_s}{N} = \frac{16000}{512} = 31.25 \text{ Hz}$$

**(b) Time Resolution:**

$$\Delta t = \frac{R}{F_s} = \frac{256}{16000} = 0.016 \text{ s} = 16 \text{ ms}$$

**(c) Overlap Percentage:**

$$\text{Overlap} = \left(1 - \frac{R}{N}\right) \times 100\% = \left(1 - \frac{256}{512}\right) \times 100\% = 50\%$$

**(d) Number of Frames:**

Total samples $L = F_s \times T = 16000 \times 2 = 32000$ samples.

$$N_f = \left\lfloor \frac{L - N}{R} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor \frac{32000 - 512}{256} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor 122.94 \right\rfloor + 1 = 122 + 1 = 123 \text{ frames}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** In exam problems, always show the substitution step explicitly. Examiners award partial credit for substituting values into the correct formula even if arithmetic is slightly off.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | STFT (Short Window, e.g., 5 ms) | STFT (Long Window, e.g., 50 ms) |
|----------|----------------------------------|---------------------------------|
| Time Resolution | **Excellent** (precise transient detection) | Poor (smears transients) |
| Frequency Resolution | Poor (cannot separate close frequencies) | **Excellent** (sharp spectral peaks) |
| Best Use Case | Plosives, percussive sounds, consonants | Vowels, sustained musical notes, formants |
| Computational Cost | Lower (smaller DFT) | Higher (larger DFT) |
| Spectral Leakage | Higher (if rectangular used) | Lower (smoother spectrum) |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 STFT Computational Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Signal x n] --> B[Frame Extraction at time m]
    B --> C[Multiply by Window w n]
    C --> D[Compute DFT or FFT]
    D --> E[Complex Spectrum X m omega]
    E --> F[Compute Magnitude X m omega]
    E --> G[Compute Phase angle X m omega]
    F --> H[Store as Spectrogram Column m]
    H --> I[Shift m to m plus R]
    I --> B
    G --> J[Phase Information Output]
```

**Interpretation:**
- The input signal flows left to right.
- The feedback loop (B ŌåÆ I ŌåÆ B) represents the sliding window mechanism.
- At each iteration, one column of the spectrogram is produced.
- Magnitude and phase are extracted in parallel from the complex spectrum.

### 4.2 STFT Filter Bank Interpretation (Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    In[Input x n] --> S1[Splitter]
    S1 --> F0[Bandpass Filter h0 centered at omega 0]
    S1 --> F1[Bandpass Filter h1 centered at omega 1]
    S1 --> F2[Bandpass Filter h2 centered at omega 2]
    S1 --> F3[Bandpass Filter hK centered at omega K]
    F0 --> D0[Downsample by R]
    F1 --> D1[Downsample by R]
    F2 --> D2[Downsample by R]
    F3 --> D3[Downsample by R]
    D0 --> O0[Output X m omega 0]
    D1 --> O1[Output X m omega 1]
    D2 --> O2[Output X m omega 2]
    D3 --> O3[Output X m omega K]
```

**Interpretation:**
- The input signal is fed to $K+1$ bandpass filters in parallel.
- Each filter $h_k[n] = w[n] \cdot e^{j\omega_k n}$ extracts a specific frequency band.
- Downsampling by hop size $R$ reduces computational load.
- Outputs collectively form the time-frequency representation.
