The reflection is a *time-reversed* previous-order coefficient. Examiners deduct 1 mark per occurrence. > 2. **Forgetting $R(0)$ is the bootstrap:** $E_0 = R(0)$ is *not* optional. Skipping it collapses the energy chain. > 3. **Sign errors in $\alpha_m$:** The numerator is $R(m) - \sum_{i=1}^{m-1} a_i^{(m-1)} R(m-i)$. Some texts write it reversed; the formula above is the one KTU expects. > 4. **Skipping stability check:** KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly allocates marks for verifying $\vert \alpha_m \vert < 1$ and minimum-phase property. > 5. **Confusing LPC order $p$ with frame length $N$:** $p$ is typically 10ŌĆō16; $N$ is 160ŌĆō320. Mixing them is a common error. > 6. **Not mentioning $\mathcal{O}(p^2)$ complexity:** Examiners award 1 mark specifically for justifying why LevinsonŌĆōDurbin is preferred over direct inversion. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - Ō£ģ The **autocorrelation method** windows speech, computes $R(k)$, and feeds them to the LevinsonŌĆōDurbin algorithm. - Ō£ģ The **YuleŌĆōWalker normal equations** arise from the orthogonality principle $\partial E_p / \partial a_i = 0$. - Ō£ģ The autocorrelation matrix is **Toeplitz, symmetric, and positive-definite** (if $R(0) > 0$). - Ō£ģ **LevinsonŌĆōDurbin** solves the system in $\mathcal{O}(p^2)$ time using the recursion: - $\alpha_m = \dfrac{R(m) - \sum_{i=1}^{m-1} a_i^{(m-1)} R(m-i)}{E_{m-1}}$ - $a_i^{(m)} = a_i^{(m-1)} - \alpha_m a_{m-i}^{(m-1)}$ - $E_m = E_{m-1}(1 - \alpha_m^2)$ - Ō£ģ **Stability** is guaranteed because $\vert \alpha_m \vert < 1$ ŌćÆ minimum-phase ŌćÆ all-pole roots strictly inside the unit circle. - Ō£ģ **PARCOR coefficients** $\alpha_m$ are the same as lattice-filter reflection coefficients. - Ō£ģ **LPC order rule of thumb:** $p \approx F_s (\text{kHz}) + 2$ ŌĆö so $p = 10$ for 8 kHz, $p = 16$ for 16 kHz. - Ō£ģ **Frame length** $N \approx 20$ ms, windowed with **Hamming** $w[n] = 0.54 - 0.46\cos(2\pi n/(N-1))$. - Ō£ģ **Gain** $G = \sqrt{E_p}$ is the standard output for synthesis. - Ō£ģ The all-pole filter is $H(z) = G / A(z)$ where $A(z) = 1 - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}$. - Ō£ģ **Computational savings:** $\mathcal{O}(p^2)$ vs $\mathcal{O}(p^3)$ ŌĆö for $p = 10$, this is roughly $100$ vs $1000$ multiplications. - Ō£ģ **Applications:** LPC-10e, MELP, CELP codecs; speaker ID; hearing aids; TTS vocoders; channel equalization. - Ō£ģ **Variants to know for viva:** Covariance method (uses covariance lags, no window), Burg method (computes reflection coefficients via lattice, also stable), lattice filters, line spectral pairs (LSPs) for quantisation.

topics.title: Speech Enhancement - Spectral Subtraction and Filtering, Harmonic Filtering, Parametric Resynthesis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST866 - SPEECH AND AUDIO PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Speech Enhancement - Spectral Subtraction and Filtering, Harmonic Filtering, Parametric Resynthesis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Speech Enhancement ŌĆö Formal Definition

**Speech Enhancement** refers to the class of signal processing techniques used to improve the **perceptual quality** and **intelligibility** of a speech signal that has been corrupted by **additive background noise**. The corrupted signal is mathematically modeled as:

$$x(n) = s(n) + w(n)$$

where $s(n)$ is the clean speech, $w(n)$ is the uncorrelated additive noise, and $x(n)$ is the observed (noisy) signal. The goal of speech enhancement is to obtain an estimate $\hat{s}(n)$ of the clean speech that minimizes a perceptually meaningful error criterion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Speech enhancement is the process of suppressing background noise from a noisy speech signal to improve its perceptual quality and intelligibility, while minimizing the introduction of artifacts such as musical noise and speech distortion.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö "Cleaning a Noisy Photograph"

Imagine you have an old photograph (your clean speech) that has been damaged by dust spots and scratches (additive noise). **Speech enhancement is like digital restoration**:

- **Spectral Subtraction** is like subtracting an *estimate of the average dust pattern* from every region of the photo. If you estimate the dust level from a "blank" patch, you can subtract it from the entire image.
- **Spectral Filtering (Wiener/MMSE)** is like applying a *smart blur that only affects dusty areas*, leaving the original picture sharp wherever it was already clean.
- **Harmonic Filtering** is like a *specialized filter that enhances repeating patterns* (the "stripes" of pitch harmonics) while suppressing random dust between them.
- **Parametric Resynthesis** is like *redrawing the entire picture using only its essential features* (outlines, colors, shapes), discarding the dust entirely.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Trade-off (KTU High-Yield Concept):**
> - **Noise Suppression** Ōåæ ŌåÆ **Speech Distortion** Ōåæ (the classic trade-off)
> - **Musical Noise**: A "twittering" artifact that appears as residual noise transformed into discrete tonal bursts ŌĆö a hallmark of naive spectral subtraction.

### 1.3 Taxonomy of Speech Enhancement Methods

| Method Family | Principle | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Spectral Subtraction** | Subtract noise magnitude/power spectrum | Low-complexity real-time denoising |
| **Wiener Filtering** | Optimal MSE linear filter in frequency domain | General-purpose enhancement |
| **MMSE Estimators** | Minimum mean-square error of STSA/LSA spectra | High-quality enhancement |
| **Harmonic Filtering** | Pitch-synchronous comb filtering | Voiced speech + tonal noise |
| **Parametric Resynthesis** | Source-filter model reconstruction | Low bit-rate, low-SNR conditions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All methods discussed operate on **short-time frames** (typically **20ŌĆō30 ms** with **50% overlap**) using the **Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT)**, since speech is a *quasi-stationary* signal.

---

### 1.4 Preliminaries ŌĆö STFT and Noise Estimation

For a frame of $N$ samples at time $m$, the STFT is:

$$X(m,k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(mM+n) \, w(n) \, e^{-j2\pi kn/N}$$

where $w(n)$ is the analysis window, $M$ is the hop size, and $k$ is the frequency bin index. A **noise profile** $\vert N(k) \vert^2$ is estimated during speech-absent segments (using a voice activity detector, VAD) and updated during speech pauses.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Spectral Subtraction ŌĆö The Foundational Method

**Spectral Subtraction** was proposed by **Boll (1979)**. The principle is disarmingly simple: estimate the noise spectrum during silence, then subtract it (in magnitude or power) from the noisy speech spectrum.

#### 2.1.1 Magnitude Spectral Subtraction

$$\vert \hat{S}(m,k) \vert = \vert X(m,k) \vert - \vert \hat{N}(k) \vert$$

followed by combining with the noisy phase:

$$\hat{S}(m,k) = \vert \hat{S}(m,k) \vert \, e^{j \angle X(m,k)}$$

The enhanced signal is obtained via inverse STFT + overlap-add.

> [!NOTE]
> The noisy phase $\angle X(m,k)$ is retained because (a) phase estimation is difficult, and (b) the human auditory system is relatively insensitive to absolute phase.

#### 2.1.2 Power Spectral Subtraction

$$\vert \hat{S}(m,k) \vert^2 = \vert X(m,k) \vert^2 - \vert \hat{N}(k) \vert^2$$

> **Why power subtraction?** Subtracting in the power domain produces a smoother residual noise, reducing musical noise. The square-root relationship makes large spectral values dominate, providing implicit weighting.

#### 2.1.3 Over-Subtraction Factor (Boll's Improvement)

To account for noise over-estimation and to reduce musical noise:

$$\vert \hat{S}(m,k) \vert = \vert X(m,k) \vert - \alpha \vert \hat{N}(k) \vert$$

with the constraint that the result is non-negative (half-wave rectification):

$$\vert \hat{S}(m,k) \vert = \max\left( \vert X(m,k) \vert - \alpha \vert \hat{N}(k) \vert,\ 0 \right)$$

where:
- $\alpha \in [1, 5]$ is the **over-subtraction factor** (typically $\alpha \approx 2$ for car noise, higher for babble).
- A **spectral floor** $\beta \vert \hat{N}(k) \vert$ is often added back: $\vert \hat{S} \vert = \max(\cdot, \beta \vert \hat{N} \vert)$ with $\beta \in [0.05, 0.2]$ to avoid silent gaps.

#### 2.1.4 Why "Musical Noise" Appears

Musical noise is the bane of spectral subtraction. It arises because:
1. **Random spectral fluctuations** of the noise are subtracted in a frame-by-frame, bin-by-bin manner.
2. **Half-wave rectification** clips negative estimates to zero, creating isolated non-zero bins.
3. After OLA reconstruction, these isolated bins manifest as **tonal "pops" and "twitters"**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Insight:** Musical noise is NOT removed by simply increasing the subtraction factor. The $\alpha$ and $\beta$ parameters trade off between residual noise (low $\alpha$) and speech distortion (high $\alpha$).

---

### 2.2 Spectral Filtering ŌĆö Wiener and MMSE Approaches

#### 2.2.1 Wiener Filter

The **Wiener filter** is the **optimal linear Minimum Mean-Square Error (MMSE)** filter in the frequency domain. It minimizes:

$$E\left[ \vert S(m,k) - H(m,k) X(m,k) \vert^2 \right]$$

The optimal transfer function is:

$$H(m,k) = \frac{P_{ss}(m,k)}{P_{ss}(m,k) + P_{nn}(k)}$$

where $P_{ss}(m,k) = E[\vert S(m,k) \vert^2]$ and $P_{nn}(k) = E[\vert N(k) \vert^2]$ are the speech and noise **power spectral densities (PSDs)**.

In practice, these are estimated as:

$$H(m,k) = \frac{\vert X(m,k) \vert^2 - \vert \hat{N}(k) \vert^2}{\vert X(m,k) \vert^2} = 1 - \frac{1}{\xi(m,k)}$$

where $\xi(m,k) = \frac{\vert \hat{N}(k) \vert^2}{\vert \hat{N}(k) \vert^2}$ ŌĆ” wait, let me correct this. Define the **a posteriori SNR** and **a priori SNR**:

$$\gamma(m,k) = \frac{\vert X(m,k) \vert^2}{\vert \hat{N}(k) \vert^2}, \quad \xi(m,k) = \frac{E[\vert S(m,k) \vert^2]}{\vert \hat{N}(k) \vert^2}$$

Then:

$$H(m,k) = \frac{\xi(m,k)}{\xi(m,k) + 1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **a priori SNR** $\xi$ is estimated using the **Decision-Directed (DD) method** of **Ephraim & Malah (1984)**:
> $$\hat{\xi}(m,k) = \alpha \frac{\vert \hat{S}(m-1,k) \vert^2}{\vert \hat{N}(k) \vert^2} + (1-\alpha) \max(\gamma(m,k)-1,\ 0)$$
> with $\alpha \approx 0.98$. This single equation is one of the **most important KTU exam concepts**.

#### 2.2.2 MMSE Short-Time Spectral Amplitude (MMSE-STSA)

**Ephraim & Malah (1984)** derived the optimal estimator of the spectral **amplitude** (not magnitude-squared) under the assumption of Gaussian-distributed spectral coefficients:

$$\hat{G}(m,k) = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} \frac{\sqrt{v(m,k)}}{\gamma(m,k)} \exp\left(-\frac{v(m,k)}{2}\right) \left[ (1+v(m,k)) I_0\left(\frac{v(m,k)}{2}\right) + v(m,k) I_1\left(\frac{v(m,k)}{2}\right) \right]$$

where:
- $v(m,k) = \frac{\xi(m,k)}{1+\xi(m,k)} \gamma(m,k)$ is the **a priori SNR scaled by a posteriori SNR**.
- $I_0$ and $I_1$ are modified Bessel functions of order 0 and 1.

#### 2.2.3 MMSE Log-Spectral Amplitude (MMSE-LSA)

**Ephraim & Malah (1985)** extended this to minimize the **log-spectral distortion** (a perceptually motivated criterion), yielding a simpler gain function:

$$\hat{G}_{\text{LSA}}(m,k) = \frac{\xi(m,k)}{1+\xi(m,k)} \exp\left(\frac{1}{2} \int_{v(m,k)}^{\infty} \frac{e^{-t}}{t} dt\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why MMSE-LSA > MMSE-STSA > Wiener:** The LSA estimator is computationally simpler than STSA and produces *less musical noise* because of the exponential term that softly suppresses low-SNR bins rather than hard-clipping them.

---

### 2.3 Harmonic Filtering

**Harmonic filtering** exploits the **harmonic structure of voiced speech**. Voiced speech has a comb-like spectrum with peaks at integer multiples of the fundamental frequency $f_0$ (pitch). Tonal noise (e.g., engine hum, 50/60 Hz mains hum) also has harmonic structure but at different $f_0$.

#### 2.3.1 Pitch Estimation

First, the pitch $f_0(m)$ is estimated for each frame using:
- **Autocorrelation method:** $r(\tau) = \sum_{n} x(n) x(n+\tau)$, peak at $\tau = 1/f_0$.
- **Cepstrum method:** Quefrency peak at $1/f_0$.
- **YIN algorithm:** Cumulative normalized difference function (state-of-the-art).

#### 2.3.2 Comb Filter Enhancement

A **pitch-synchronous comb filter** enhances energy at harmonics $k f_0$ and attenuates inter-harmonic regions:

$$H_{\text{comb}}(f) = 1 + \sum_{l=1}^{L} \rho^l \cos\left(2\pi l f_0 f / f_s\right) \quad \text{(in normalized frequency)}$$

In practice, the **spectral gain** at each bin $k$ is:

$$G_H(m,k) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } k \text{ is within } \Delta f \text{ of } l f_0 \text{ for some integer } l \\ \beta & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

with $L$ being the number of harmonics considered and $\rho \in [0, 1)$ a decay factor. Typical values: $\Delta f = 50$ Hz, $L = 20$, $\beta = 0.1$ to $0.3$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Harmonic filtering is most effective when** the noise is **non-harmonic** (e.g., white noise, babble) but speech is harmonic ŌĆö the contrast is enhanced. It is less effective when noise is also harmonic (e.g., competing speakers).

---

### 2.4 Parametric Resynthesis

**Parametric resynthesis** takes a completely different approach: instead of subtracting noise, it *rebuilds* the speech using a parametric model. This is the foundation of **vocoders** and modern **neural codec** approaches.

#### 2.4.1 Source-Filter Model

Speech is modeled as the output of a time-varying filter (vocal tract) excited by a source signal:

$$s(n) = h(n) * e(n)$$

where:
- $e(n)$ is the **excitation**: periodic pulse train (voiced) or white noise (unvoiced).
- $h(n)$ is the **vocal tract impulse response**, often modeled by an all-pole filter of order $p$:

$$H(z) = \frac{1}{1 - \sum_{i=1}^{p} a_i z^{-i}}$$

#### 2.4.2 Linear Predictive Coding (LPC) Analysis

The LPC coefficients $\{a_i\}$ are obtained by solving the **Yule-Walker equations**:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{p} a_i \, r(\vert k-i \vert) = -r(k), \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, p$$

where $r(\tau)$ is the autocorrelation of the windowed frame. Solved efficiently via the **Levinson-Durbin algorithm**.

For enhancement, the noisy LPC is replaced by an **enhanced LPC estimate** computed from a denoised autocorrelation (e.g., via Wiener-filtered speech).

#### 2.4.3 Resynthesis Procedure

1. **Analyze**: Compute LPC $\{a_i\}$, pitch $f_0$, voiced/unvoiced (V/UV) flag, gain $G$ for each frame.
2. **Modify**: 
   - Replace noisy pitch with median-filtered, jitter-controlled pitch track.
   - Replace noisy gain with normalized gain.
   - Optionally enhance LPCs to sharpen formants.
3. **Synthesize**: Generate excitation $e(n)$:
   - Voiced: $e(n) = \sum_{k} \delta(n - kT_0)$ with $T_0 = f_s / f_0$.
   - Unvoiced: $e(n) = $ Gaussian white noise.
4. **Filter**: $s(n) = G \cdot e(n) * \frac{1}{A(z)}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Parametric Resynthesis is Powerful:** It discards ALL phase and fine spectral information, replacing them with a clean synthetic version. As a result, it produces *zero musical noise* ŌĆö but it can sound "buzzy" or "robotic" if the model order is too low or the pitch is mis-tracked.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Noisy model | $x(n)=s(n)+w(n)$ | Additive, uncorrelated |
| 2 | Magnitude subtraction | $\vert \hat{S} \vert = \vert X \vert - \alpha \vert \hat{N} \vert$ | Boll 1979 |
| 3 | Power subtraction | $\vert \hat{S} \vert^2 = \vert X \vert^2 - \vert \hat{N} \vert^2$ | Less musical noise |
| 4 | Wiener filter | $H = \frac{\xi}{\xi+1} = 1 - \frac{1}{\gamma}$ | MMSE-optimal linear |
| 5 | A posteriori SNR | $\gamma = \frac{\vert X \vert^2}{\vert \hat{N} \vert^2}$ | Per-frame, per-bin |
| 6 | A priori SNR | $\xi = \frac{E[\vert S \vert^2]}{\vert \hat{N} \vert^2}$ | Smoothed via DD |
| 7 | Decision-Directed | $\hat{\xi}_m = \alpha \frac{\vert \hat{S}_{m-1} \vert^2}{\vert \hat{N} \vert^2} + (1-\alpha)\max(\gamma-1,0)$ | $\alpha \approx 0.98$ |
| 8 | MMSE-STSA gain | Involves Bessel $I_0, I_1$ | Gaussian assumption |
| 9 | MMSE-LSA gain | $\frac{\xi}{1+\xi}\exp(\cdot)$ | Perceptually better |
| 10 | Comb filter | $H(f) = 1 + \sum \rho^l \cos(2\pi l f_0 f / f_s)$ | Harmonic emphasis |
| 11 | LPC all-pole | $H(z) = 1/A(z)$ | Order $p = 8$ŌĆō$14$ |
| 12 | Yule-Walker | $\sum a_i r(\vert k-i\vert) = -r(k)$ | Solved via Levinson-Durbin |
| 13 | Spectral floor | $\max(\cdot, \beta \vert \hat{N} \vert)$ | $\beta \approx 0.1$ to mask artifacts |
| 14 | Over-subtraction | $\alpha \in [1, 5]$ | Trade-off parameter |

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Method Used |

| **Mobile phone noise suppression** | MMSE-LSA + Decision-Directed |
| **Hearing aids** | Wiener + multiband compression |
| **Air traffic control radio** | Spectral subtraction (low latency) |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Wiener Filter

We seek $H(m,k)$ minimizing:
$$J(H) = E\left[ \vert S(m,k) - H(m,k) X(m,k) \vert^2 \right]$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Expand the squared magnitude:**

$$J(H) = E\left[ (S - HX)(S^* - H^* X^*) \right]$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Distribute the product:**

$$J(H) = E[\vert S \vert^2] - H E[S X^*] - H^* E[S^* X] + \vert H \vert^2 E[\vert X \vert^2]$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Assume $S$ and $W$ are zero-mean and uncorrelated.** Then $E[S X^*] = E[S(S+W)^*] = E[\vert S \vert^2] \equiv P_{ss}$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Differentiate with respect to $H^*$ and set to zero:**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial H^*} = -E[S X^*] + H E[\vert X \vert^2] = 0$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Solve for $H$:**

$$H(m,k) = \frac{E[\vert S(m,k) \vert^2]}{E[\vert X(m,k) \vert^2]} = \frac{P_{ss}(m,k)}{P_{ss}(m,k) + P_{nn}(k)}$$

This is the **Wiener filter transfer function** ŌĆö the ratio of clean speech PSD to noisy speech PSD.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Express in terms of SNR.** Since $P_{ss}/P_{nn} = \xi$:

$$H(m,k) = \frac{\xi(m,k)}{\xi(m,k) + 1}$$

$\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Decision-Directed A Priori SNR Estimator

**Problem:** $P_{ss}(m,k) = E[\vert S(m,k) \vert^2]$ is unknown. We need an estimate $\hat{\xi}(m,k)$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Direct (Maximum-Likelihood) estimate from current frame:**

$$\hat{P}_{ss}^{\text{ML}}(m,k) = \max(\vert X(m,k) \vert^2 - \vert \hat{N}(k) \vert^2,\ 0) \;\Rightarrow\; \hat{\xi}_{\text{ML}} = \max(\gamma - 1,\ 0)$$

This is biased: it fluctuates wildly frame-to-frame, causing musical noise.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Smoothed estimate (Ephraim & Malah, 1984):** Combine ML estimate with previous frame's estimate:

$$\hat{\xi}(m,k) = \alpha \cdot \frac{\vert \hat{S}(m-1,k) \vert^2}{\vert \hat{N}(k) \vert^2} + (1-\alpha) \cdot \max(\gamma(m,k) - 1,\ 0)$$

with $\alpha \in [0.95, 0.99]$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Interpretation:**
- The first term is the **a priori** (predicted) part, using the previous frame's enhanced speech.
- The second term is the **a posteriori** (current data) part, using the ML estimate.
- For $\alpha = 0.98$, the prediction dominates ŌåÆ smooth, less musical noise.
- For $\alpha = 0.5$, the ML dominates ŌåÆ noisier but more responsive.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Why this works:** When the true speech is present, both terms contribute positively. When the current frame is noise-only, the ML term is near zero (so $\gamma - 1 \approx 0$) and the predicted term is also small (because previous frame was also noise). When speech appears, the predicted term is non-zero from the prior speech frame, providing a "warm start".

$\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the MMSE-STSA Gain (Sketch)

**Setup:** Assume $X(m,k) = S(m,k) + N(m,k)$ with $S$ and $N$ being **independent complex Gaussian** random variables. Then $X \sim \mathcal{CN}(0, P_{ss} + P_{nn})$ and the conditional distribution of $S$ given $X$ is also complex Gaussian:

$$p(S \vert X) = \frac{1}{\pi P_{ss}} \exp\left(-\frac{\vert S - \mu_S \vert^2}{P_{ss}}\right)$$

where $\mu_S = \frac{P_{ss}}{P_{ss} + P_{nn}} X = \frac{\xi}{1+\xi} X$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Express in polar form:** Let $X = R_x e^{j\theta_x}$, $S = R_s e^{j\theta_s}$, $N = R_n e^{j\theta_n}$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Use Bayes' rule to find the joint density of $(R_s, \theta_s)$ given $X = R_x e^{j\theta_x}$:**

$$p(R_s, \theta_s \vert X) = p(S \vert X) \cdot R_s$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Marginalize over $\theta_s$** (since we only care about amplitude) and compute $E[R_s \vert X]$:

$$\hat{R}_s = E[R_s \vert X = R_x] = \int_0^{\infty} R_s \, p(R_s \vert X) \, dR_s$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö After integration** (Ephraim & Malah 1984, Appendix A), the result is:

$$\hat{R}_s = \frac{\sqrt{\pi v}}{2\gamma} \exp(-v/2) \left[ (1+v) I_0(v/2) + v I_1(v/2) \right] \cdot R_x$$

where $v = \frac{\xi}{1+\xi} \gamma$.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Identify the gain** $G = \hat{R}_s / R_x$:

$$G_{\text{STSA}}(m,k) = \frac{\sqrt{\pi v}}{2\gamma} \exp(-v/2) \left[ (1+v) I_0(v/2) + v I_1(v/2) \right]$$

$\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU exam purposes**, you need to know:
> - The Gaussian assumption.
> - The form of the gain function with Bessel functions.
> - That LSA is a different (and computationally simpler) derivation minimizing log-spectral error.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Example ŌĆö Magnitude Spectral Subtraction

**Given:**
- A single STFT bin: $X = 4.5 \, e^{j\pi/4}$
- Noise estimate: $\vert \hat{N} \vert = 1.2$
- Over-subtraction factor: $\alpha = 2.0$
- Spectral floor: $\beta = 0.1$

**Find:** The enhanced spectrum $\hat{S}$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply the subtraction:**

$$\alpha \vert \hat{N} \vert = 2.0 \times 1.2 = 2.4$$

$$\vert X \vert - \alpha \vert \hat{N} \vert = 4.5 - 2.4 = 2.1$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply spectral floor:**

$$\beta \vert \hat{N} \vert = 0.1 \times 1.2 = 0.12$$

Since $2.1 > 0.12$, the floor is not active. The enhanced magnitude is $\vert \hat{S} \vert = 2.1$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Retain noisy phase** $\angle X = \pi/4$:

$$\hat{S} = 2.1 \, e^{j\pi/4} = 2.1(\cos 45┬░ + j\sin 45┬░) = 1.485 + j\,1.485$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Verify.** If true $\vert S \vert = 3.0$ and $\vert N \vert = 1.5$ (random), then $\vert X \vert$ ranges over $\sim$ [1.5, 4.5]. With $\alpha = 2$, we estimate $\vert S \vert \in [0.3, 2.7]$. The estimate is **under-estimating** the true magnitude ŌĆö a sign of mild over-subtraction.

$\blacksquare$

---

| Criterion | Spectral Subtract | Wiener | MMSE-LSA | Harmonic | Parametric Resynth |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Noise suppression** | Medium | Medium-High | High | Medium | Very High |
| **Musical noise** | High | Medium | Low | Low | None |
| **Speech distortion** | High | Medium | Low | Low (voiced) | Medium-High |
| **Computational cost** | Very Low | Low | Medium | Medium | Medium-High |
| **Latency** | Low | Low | Low | Medium (pitch lag) | Medium |
| **Handles unvoiced?** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Poorly | Yes |
| **Robust to pitch error** | Yes | Yes | Yes | No | Yes |
| **Output naturalness** | Fair | Good | Very Good | Good (voiced) | Fair-Buzzy |

### 4.1 Generic Speech Enhancement Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Noisy Speech xn] --> B[Framing + Windowing]
    B --> C[STFT]
    C --> D[Noise PSD Estimator]
    D --> E[SNR Computation gamma xi]
    C --> E
    E --> F[Gain Function Gm,k]
    F --> G[Apply Gain to STFT]
    G --> H[ISTFT + Overlap-Add]
    H --> I[Enhanced Speech shat n]
    D -.feedback.-> F
```

### 4.2 Spectral Subtraction Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Frame Xm,k] --> B[Compute Magnitude]
    A --> C[Retain Phase]
    D[Noise Profile Nhat k] --> E[Multiply by alpha]
    E --> F[Subtract from Magnitude]
    B --> F
    F --> G[Half-Wave Rectification]
    G --> H[Add Spectral Floor]
    H --> I[Recombine with Phase]
    I --> J[Output S hat m,k]
    C --> I
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Noisy STFT X] --> B[Posteriori SNR gamma]

    C --> D[Apriori SNR xi]

    E --> F[Multiply X by Gain]
    F --> G[ISTFT]
    G --> H[S hat]
    H --> Prev
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Noisy Speech] --> B[LPC Analysis]
    A --> C[Pitch Detection f0]
    A --> D[V/UV Decision]
    A --> E[Gain G]
    B --> F[Denoise / Smooth]
    F --> G[Enhanced LPC coeffs]
    C --> H[Pitch Smoothing / Jitter Control]

    E --> K
    K -->|Voiced| L[Pulse Train at T0]
    K -->|Unvoiced| M[Gaussian Noise]
    L --> J
    M --> J
    J --> N[Enhanced Speech shat n]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024]**
*Define speech enhancement. What is meant by "musical noise" and why does it occur in spectral subtraction?*

**Model Answer:**

**Speech Enhancement** is the process of suppressing background noise from a noisy speech signal to improve its **perceptual quality** and **intelligibility**, while controlling the distortion introduced in the speech.

**Musical Noise** is a perceptual artifact characterized by short-duration tonal bursts ("tweets" or "pops") that emerge from residual noise after spectral subtraction.

**Cause:**
- Noise magnitude $\vert N(k) \vert$ is estimated as an **average** over silence frames, but individual noise samples fluctuate bin-by-bin and frame-by-frame.
- Half-wave rectification (`max(┬Ę, 0)`) clips negative residual estimates, leaving **isolated non-zero bins** that appear as random tones.
- Overlap-add reconstruction of these isolated tones produces the characteristic "musical" perception.

**Preventive Measures:** (a) Over-subtraction factor $\alpha > 1$, (b) spectral floor $\beta > 0$, (c) smoothing across time and frequency, (d) MMSE-LSA gain function which softly suppresses rather than hard-clips. **[3 marks]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between spectral subtraction and Wiener filtering. State the Wiener filter transfer function.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Spectral Subtraction | Wiener Filter |
|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Subtract noise magnitude from noisy magnitude | Apply linear MMSE-optimal filter |
| **Domain** | Magnitude domain | Complex spectrum |
| **Optimality** | Heuristic | MMSE-optimal (under stationarity) |
| **Musical noise** | High | Medium |
| **Math form** | $\vert \hat{S} \vert = \vert X \vert - \alpha \vert \hat{N} \vert$ | $\hat{S} = H \cdot X$ |

**Wiener Filter Transfer Function:**

$$H(m,k) = \frac{P_{ss}(m,k)}{P_{ss}(m,k) + P_{nn}(k)} = \frac{\xi(m,k)}{\xi(m,k) + 1}$$

where $P_{ss}$ and $P_{nn}$ are the speech and noise power spectral densities, and $\xi$ is the a priori SNR. **[3 marks]**

---

### PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **Internal Choice Note:** KTU 2024 Scheme requires Module-level internal choice. The two alternatives below are independent selections within the same module topic.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the Wiener filter for speech enhancement. Explain the role of a posteriori SNR $\gamma$ and a priori SNR $\xi$ in the formulation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With reference to the **Decision-Directed approach** of Ephraim & Malah (1984), derive the recursive estimator for the a priori SNR. Show how it reduces musical noise compared to the maximum-likelihood estimate. **(7 marks)**

---

**(a) Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Problem Statement.** Given $X(m,k) = S(m,k) + N(m,k)$ with $S$ and $N$ uncorrelated, zero-mean, find the linear filter $H(m,k)$ such that $\hat{S}(m,k) = H(m,k) X(m,k)$ minimizes:

$$J = E\left[ \vert S(m,k) - H(m,k) X(m,k) \vert^2 \right]$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Expand J:**

$$J = E[\vert S \vert^2] - H E[S X^*] - H^* E[S^* X] + \vert H \vert^2 E[\vert X \vert^2]$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Use uncorrelated assumption.** $E[S X^*] = E[S(S+N)^*] = E[\vert S \vert^2] = P_{ss}$. Also $E[\vert X \vert^2] = P_{ss} + P_{nn}$.

$$J = P_{ss} - H P_{ss} - H^* P_{ss} + \vert H \vert^2 (P_{ss} + P_{nn})$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Set derivative w.r.t. $H^*$ to zero:**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial H^*} = -P_{ss} + H(P_{ss} + P_{nn}) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; H = \frac{P_{ss}}{P_{ss} + P_{nn}}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Express in terms of SNRs.** Define a priori SNR $\xi = P_{ss}/P_{nn}$ and a posteriori SNR $\gamma = \vert X \vert^2 / P_{nn}$:

$$H = \frac{\xi}{\xi + 1} = 1 - \frac{1}{\gamma}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Role of SNRs:**
- $\gamma$ is computed **instantaneously** from the current frame.
- $\xi$ is a **smoothed** estimate (see part b).
- When $\gamma \gg 1$ (high SNR), $H \to 1$ ŌåÆ pass speech unchanged.
- When $\gamma \approx 1$ (noise only), $H \to 0.5$ ŌåÆ suppress by half.
- The Wiener filter **never fully suppresses** speech; it gracefully attenuates based on local SNR.

**[7 marks]**

---

**(b) Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö ML a priori SNR estimate.** From the current frame, a non-negative estimate is:

$$\hat{\xi}_{\text{ML}}(m,k) = \max(\gamma(m,k) - 1,\ 0)$$

This is **unbiased in expectation** but has **very high variance**, varying wildly from frame to frame, which directly causes musical noise.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Decision-Directed estimator.** Ephraim & Malah (1984) proposed temporal smoothing:

$$\hat{\xi}(m,k) = \alpha \cdot \frac{\vert \hat{S}(m-1,k) \vert^2}{\vert \hat{N}(k) \vert^2} + (1-\alpha) \cdot \max(\gamma(m,k) - 1,\ 0)$$

**Derivation reasoning:** Write the a priori SNR at frame $m$ as the conditional expectation of $\vert S(m,k) \vert^2 / P_{nn}$ given all past data, and approximate the conditional expectation by a weighted average of (a) the previous frame's estimate and (b) the current ML estimate.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Choose $\alpha$.** Typically $\alpha = 0.98$.
- $\alpha$ high ŌåÆ strong smoothing ŌåÆ less musical noise, but slower to track SNR changes.
- $\alpha$ low ŌåÆ responsive, but reintroduces musical noise.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Why musical noise is reduced.** The first term $\vert \hat{S}(m-1,k) \vert^2$ is **already enhanced** (i.e., it has been Wiener-filtered in the previous frame). Using it as a predictor means that transient noise spikes in the current frame are ignored ŌĆö the "memory" of past enhancement smooths the estimate. The ML term acts as a residual corrector.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Worked example:** Suppose $\gamma(m) = 3$, $\gamma(m-1) = 4$, $\vert \hat{S}(m-1) \vert^2 / P_{nn} = 3.5$, $\alpha = 0.98$:

$$\hat{\xi}(m) = 0.98 \times 3.5 + 0.02 \times \max(3-1, 0) = 3.43 + 0.04 = 3.47$$

vs. ML: $\hat{\xi}_{\text{ML}}(m) = 2$. The DD estimate stays close to the previous, smooth value rather than dropping sharply.

**[7 marks]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Choice 2**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain **harmonic filtering** for speech enhancement. Describe the role of pitch estimation and comb filter design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **parametric resynthesis**? With the help of a block diagram, explain the source-filter model for LPC-based speech synthesis. Show how this method can be used for speech enhancement. **(7 marks)**

---

**(a) Model Solution:**

**Harmonic Filtering ŌĆö Concept.** Voiced speech exhibits a **harmonic structure**: its spectrum contains peaks at integer multiples of the fundamental frequency $f_0$. Tonal noise (engine hum, mains hum, fans) can also be harmonic but usually at a different $f_0$. The objective of harmonic filtering is to **enhance the harmonics of the desired speech** while suppressing inter-harmonic energy that is dominated by noise.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Pitch Estimation.** Per frame, $f_0$ is estimated using:
- **Autocorrelation:** $r(\tau) = \sum_n x(n) x(n+\tau)$, choose $\tau^* = \arg\max_\tau r(\tau)$ over a valid range, then $f_0 = f_s / \tau^*$.
- **Cepstrum:** Compute $c(n) = \text{IFFT}\{\log \vert X(k) \vert^2\}$; the peak at quefrency $1/f_0$ indicates pitch.
- **YIN algorithm:** Uses the cumulative normalized difference function for robust estimation.

The pitch track $\hat{f}_0(m)$ is then median-filtered across frames to remove outliers ("pitch doubling" or "halving").

**Step 2 ŌĆö Comb Filter Design.** A comb filter has gain peaks at harmonics of $f_0$:

$$H_{\text{comb}}(f) = 1 + 2\sum_{l=1}^{L} \rho^l \cos(2\pi l f_0 f / f_s)$$

with $0 \le \rho < 1$ controlling the harmonic bandwidth and $L$ controlling the highest harmonic retained.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply as Spectral Gain.** For each STFT bin $k$:

$$G_H(m,k) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \vert f_k - l f_0(m) \vert \le \Delta f \text{ for some } l \in \{1,\ldots,L\} \\ \beta & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

with $\Delta f \approx 50$ Hz and $\beta \in [0.1, 0.3]$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Limitation.** Harmonic filtering assumes the speech is **voiced** and the noise is **non-harmonic** (or at a different $f_0$). For unvoiced consonants (stops, fricatives) or competing speakers with similar pitch, the method may fail or introduce distortion.

**[7 marks]**

---

**(b) Model Solution:**

**Parametric Resynthesis ŌĆö Concept.** Unlike spectral subtraction or Wiener filtering, which operate on the noisy STFT coefficients directly, parametric resynthesis **discards the noisy waveform** and **rebuilds** the speech from a compact set of parameters: pitch $f_0$, voicing flag, gain $G$, and LPC coefficients $\{a_i\}$.

**Source-Filter Model.** Speech production is modeled as:

$$s(n) = G \cdot e(n) * h(n) \quad \text{or} \quad S(z) = G \cdot E(z) \cdot H(z)$$

- $e(n)$ is the **excitation signal**: a periodic impulse train (voiced) or white noise (unvoiced).
- $h(n)$ is the **vocal tract impulse response**, modeled by an all-pole filter of order $p$:

$$H(z) = \frac{1}{A(z)} = \frac{1}{1 - \sum_{i=1}^{p} a_i z^{-i}}$$

**LPC Analysis.** Per frame, the coefficients $a_i$ are computed by minimizing the prediction error $E = \sum e^2(n)$ where $e(n) = s(n) - \sum_{i=1}^p a_i s(n-i)$. This leads to the **Yule-Walker normal equations**:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{p} a_i \, r(\vert k - i \vert) = -r(k), \quad k = 1, \ldots, p$$

solved by the **Levinson-Durbin algorithm** in $O(p^2)$ operations.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Frame xn] --> B[Autocorrelation r tau]
    B --> C[Levinson-Durbin]
    C --> D[LPC Coeffs a_i]
    A --> E[Pitch Detector]
    A --> F[Energy G]
    A --> G[V/UV Classifier]
    D --> H[Synthesis Filter 1/Az]
    E --> I{Frame Type}
    F --> I
    G --> I
    I -->|Voiced| J[Pulse Train at T0=fs/f0]
    I -->|Unvoiced| K[Gaussian Noise]
    J --> H
    K --> H
    H --> L[Enhanced Speech shat n]
```

**Application to Speech Enhancement:**
1. **Analyze** the noisy frame to obtain $f_0$, V/UV, $G$, and $\{a_i\}$.
2. **Clean** the parameters:
   - Median-filter $f_0$ across frames.
   - Normalize $G$ to remove gain fluctuations.
   - Optionally de-noise the autocorrelation $r(\tau)$ via spectral subtraction, then re-solve Yule-Walker for cleaner $\{a_i\}$.
3. **Synthesize** the speech: generate the appropriate excitation, filter through $1/A(z)$, and overlap-add.

**Advantages:** Eliminates **all residual noise** and **all musical noise** by design (since noise is not in the model). **Disadvantages:** Sounds "buzzy" or unnatural if the model order $p$ is too low, and fails at low SNRs if pitch/V-UV cannot be reliably estimated from the noisy signal.

**[7 marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ŌĆö Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. **Forgetting the spectral floor** $\beta$ in spectral subtraction: students often write only the subtraction equation, missing the `max(┬Ę, ╬▓|N|)` clause. **-1 mark.**
> 2. **Confusing a posteriori and a priori SNR**: $\gamma$ is from the **current** frame; $\xi$ is the **smoothed/predicted** value. They are NOT the same. **-1 mark.**
> 3. **Omitting the Gaussian assumption** in the MMSE-STSA derivation: the Bessel function form arises *specifically* from this assumption. **-2 marks.**
> 4. **Writing the Wiener filter as $H = P_{nn}/(P_{ss}+P_{nn})$**: this is the **inverse** ŌĆö a noise-emphasis filter. Correct is $H = P_{ss}/(P_{ss}+P_{nn})$. **-2 marks.**
> 5. **Forgetting the phase retention** in spectral subtraction: must explicitly state that $\angle X$ is used for $\angle \hat{S}$. **-1 mark.**
> 6. **In LPC synthesis, generating the wrong excitation** for V/UV: pulses for voiced, random noise for unvoiced. Students sometimes mix these up. **-2 marks.**

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Noisy model** $x(n) = s(n) + w(n)$ is the universal starting point.
- [x] **All methods operate in the STFT domain** on short frames (20ŌĆō30 ms, 50% overlap).
- [x] **Spectral Subtraction** subtracts the noise magnitude (scaled by $\alpha$) from noisy magnitude; **half-wave rectify** to keep non-negative; **add a spectral floor** $\beta \vert \hat{N} \vert$ to mask musical noise.
- [x] **Wiener filter** $H = \xi/(\xi+1)$ is the **MMSE-optimal linear filter**; never fully suppresses, gracefully attenuates.
- [x] **A posteriori SNR** $\gamma = \vert X \vert^2 / \vert \hat{N} \vert^2$ is instantaneous.
- [x] **A priori SNR** $\xi$ is estimated via **Decision-Directed** smoothing with $\alpha \approx 0.98$.
- [x] **MMSE-STSA** assumes Gaussian spectral coefficients and uses Bessel functions $I_0, I_1$.
- [x] **MMSE-LSA** minimizes log-spectral distortion; computationally simpler and produces less musical noise.
- [x] **Musical noise** = residual random tonal artifacts from frame-by-frame, bin-by-bin subtraction. Mitigated by smoothing, over-subtraction, spectral floor, and soft-gain MMSE estimators.
- [x] **Harmonic filtering** uses pitch-synchronous comb filter; enhances harmonics $\ell f_0$, attenuates inter-harmonic regions.
- [x] **Parametric resynthesis** = analyze $f_0$, V/UV, $G$, LPC; **discard** noisy phase/waveform; **rebuild** from clean parameters. Zero musical noise but can sound buzzy.
- [x] **LPC analysis** solves Yule-Walker via Levinson-Durbin; order $p$ typically 10ŌĆō14 for speech at 8ŌĆō16 kHz.
- [x] **Source-filter model**: $s(n) = G \cdot e(n) * h(n)$ with $e(n)$ as pulse train (voiced) or noise (unvoiced) and $h(n) = \text{IFT}\{1/A(z)\}$.
- [x] **Noise PSD** is estimated during **speech-absent** segments (VAD) and held fixed or slowly updated.
- [x] **Trade-off**: Noise suppression Ōåö Speech distortion Ōåö Musical noise (the eternal triangle).
- [x] **Real-world applications**: mobile phones (MMSE-LSA), hearing aids (Wiener), codecs (parametric resynthesis), ATC radio (spectral subtraction).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Speech Coding - Fundamentals, Class of Coders: Time Domain/Spectral Domain/Vocoders, Sub-Band Coding, Adaptive Transform Coding, Phase Vocoder
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST866 - SPEECH AND AUDIO PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Speech Coding - Fundamentals, Class of Coders: Time Domain/Spectral Domain/Vocoders, Sub-Band Coding, Adaptive Transform Coding, Phase Vocoder

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Speech Coding - Formal Definition

**Speech Coding** is the process of compressing and representing digital speech signals using a reduced number of bits per second (bit-rate) while preserving acceptable perceptual quality for storage or transmission over bandwidth-limited channels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Speech coding is fundamentally different from *general audio coding* (like MP3) because it exploits the **source-filter model of speech production**, allowing much lower bit-rates (down to **2.4 kbps**) compared to transparent audio coders (typically > 64 kbps).

The basic functional equation governing any speech coder is the **Rate-Distortion trade-off**:

$$
R \geq H(S) - I(S; \hat{S})
$$

Where $R$ is the bit-rate, $H(S)$ is the source entropy, and $I(S;\hat{S})$ is the mutual information between original $S$ and reconstructed $\hat{S}$.

**Key Performance Metrics:**
- **Bit-rate (R):** Measured in **kbps** (kilobits per second).
- **Quality:** Measured via **MOS (Mean Opinion Score)** on a scale of **1 (Bad) to 5 (Excellent)**.
- **Algorithmic Delay:** End-to-end encoding + decoding delay in **ms**.
- **Complexity:** Computed in **MIPS** (Million Instructions Per Second) or **WMOPS**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Telephony Reference:** Standard PCM speech uses **64 kbps** (8 kHz sampling ├Ś 8 bits). Modern mobile telephony (4G/5G) uses AMR-WB at around **6.6 to 23.85 kbps**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy - The "Language Translator" Analogy

Imagine you have a 1000-page novel written in long, verbose English sentences. To save printing cost, you hire an expert who:

1. **Reads the entire book** (Analysis stage).
2. **Extracts only the unique vocabulary and grammatical structure** (Parameter extraction).
3. **Re-writes the novel in shorthand/telegraphic style** (Coding stage).

When the reader receives the shorthand, they use the same grammatical rules to **reconstruct the meaning** (Synthesis stage).

In speech coding, the "novel" is your speech waveform. The "expert" is the **coder**, and the "shorthand" is the **bitstream**. The cleverer the coder, the shorter the shorthand (lower bit-rate), while still preserving the original meaning (speech intelligibility).

### 1.3 Classification of Speech Coders (KTU Module 3 Core Concept)

Speech coders are broadly classified into **three classes** based on the domain in which redundancy is exploited:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Classes of Speech Coders:**
> 1. **Time Domain Coders** - Exploit sample-to-sample correlation in the **time domain** (e.g., PCM, DPCM, ADPCM, APC).
> 2. **Spectral/Frequency Domain Coders** - Exploit energy distribution in the **frequency domain** (e.g., Sub-Band Coding, Adaptive Transform Coding).
> 3. **Vocoders (Voice Coders)** - Exploit the **mathematical model of speech production** using a source-filter representation (e.g., LPC, Channel Vocoder, Phase Vocoder).

![Speech Coder Classification Overview](placeholder)

| Coder Class | Domain | Typical Bit-Rate | Key Technique |
|---|---|---|---|
| Time Domain | Temporal | **16ŌĆō64 kbps** | Prediction, Differential |
| Spectral Domain | Frequency | **16ŌĆō64 kbps** | Filter banks, DCT |
| Vocoders | Model-based | **1.2ŌĆō4.8 kbps** | Source-Filter Model |
| Hybrid | Mixed | **4.8ŌĆō13 kbps** | CELP (Code-Excited LP) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Time Domain Coders - The Predictive Approach

Time domain coders exploit the high **inter-sample redundancy** in speech signals. Since adjacent speech samples are correlated, instead of transmitting each sample, we transmit the **prediction error** (residual).

**A. Pulse Code Modulation (PCM):**
- The simplest time-domain coder.
- Samples are quantized uniformly (linear PCM) or logarithmically ($\mu$-law, A-law).
- Bit rate: $R = f_s \times n$, where $f_s$ is sampling rate, $n$ is bits/sample.

**B. Differential PCM (DPCM):**
- Transmits the difference: $d(n) = s(n) - \hat{s}(n)$.
- Uses a **fixed predictor** (e.g., first-order predictor).
- Reduction in bit-rate because $d(n)$ has lower variance than $s(n)$.

**C. Adaptive Differential PCM (ADPCM):**
- Predictor coefficients are **adapted** based on signal statistics.
- Used in ITU-T **G.726** standard at **32 kbps**.

**D. Adaptive Predictive Coding (APC):**
- Higher-order adaptive predictor.
- Uses **pitch period** extraction for better prediction.
- Bit-rate typically **16 kbps**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** APC by Atal & Schroeder (1970) was a landmark in speech coding, achieving 16 kbps quality close to 64 kbps PCM.

### 2.2 Spectral Domain Coders - The Frequency Perspective

These coders transform the speech signal into the frequency domain using **filter banks** (Sub-Band Coding) or **block transforms** (Adaptive Transform Coding). The frequency components are then quantized according to perceptual importance.

**Key Principle:** Human ear is more sensitive to lower frequencies (0ŌĆō4 kHz) than higher frequencies. So, **allocate more bits to perceptually important bands**.

### 2.3 Vocoders - Model-Based Coding

Vocoders (Voice Coders) use the **Source-Filter Model** of speech production:

$$
s(n) = e(n) * h(n)
$$

Where:
- $e(n)$ = excitation signal (periodic for voiced, random for unvoiced).
- $h(n)$ = vocal tract filter impulse response.
- $*$ denotes convolution.

The coder transmits only:
1. **Pitch period** (for voiced sounds).
2. **Voiced/Unvoiced decision**.
3. **Vocal tract parameters** (e.g., LPC coefficients, formant frequencies).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vocoder Limitation:** Naturalness is poor (MOS Ōēł 2.5ŌĆō3.0) because the excitation model is simplified. Modern coders like **CELP** improve this by using **vector quantization of the excitation**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Parameter** | **Expression** | **Unit / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Bit Rate (PCM) | $R = f_s \times n$ | **kbps** |
| Bit Rate (Sub-Band) | $R = \sum_{k=1}^{M} f_{s,k} \times R_k$ | $f_{s,k}$ = sub-band sample rate |
| Bit Rate (ATC) | $R = \frac{N \cdot B}{T}$ | $B$ = bits/coefficient, $N$ = block size |
| DPCM Error | $e(n) = s(n) - \hat{s}(n)$ | Scalar residual |
| Source-Filter Model | $s(n) = e(n) * v(n)$ | Convolution |
| LPC Predictor | $\hat{s}(n) = \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k s(n-k)$ | Order $p$ (typically 10ŌĆō16) |
| LPC Residual | $e(n) = s(n) - \sum a_k s(n-k)$ | Excitation signal |
| LPC Spectrum | $H(z) = \frac{1}{1 - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}}$ | All-pole model |
| STFT | $X(m,k) = \sum_{n} x(n) w(n-m) e^{-j\omega_k n}$ | Time-frequency representation |
| Phase Derivative | $\Delta\phi_k(m) = \phi_k(m) - \phi_k(m-1)$ | Instantaneous frequency |
| SQNR (Uniform) | $\text{SQNR} \approx 6.02n + 1.76$ | dB, $n$ = bits/sample |
| MOS Scale | 1 (Bad) to 5 (Excellent) | Subjective quality |
| Perceptual Weighting | $W(z) = \frac{A(z/\gamma_1)}{A(z/\gamma_2)}$, $\gamma_1 > \gamma_2$ | Spectral shaping |
| Spectral Flatness | $\text{SFM} = \frac{\exp\left(\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}\ln P(\omega)d\omega\right)}{\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} P(\omega)d\omega}$ | Measure of tonality |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Applications of Speech Coding:**
> - **Mobile Telephony (4G/5G):** AMR-WB (Adaptive Multi-Rate Wideband) at 6.6ŌĆō23.85 kbps.
> - **VoIP (Skype, WhatsApp):** Opus codec (6ŌĆō510 kbps).
> - **Satellite Communication:** MELPe at 2.4 kbps (military-grade).
> - **Voice Mail / Storage:** GSM-FR at 13 kbps.
> - **Music Streaming (Hybrid):** Sub-band coding in MPEG Audio (MP3).
> - **Audio Time-Stretching (DAWs):** Phase Vocoder in Adobe Audition, Ableton Live.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Sub-Band Coding (SBC) - Detailed Working

**Conceptual Flow:**

1. Input speech $x(n)$ is filtered into $M$ sub-bands using a **filter bank**.
2. Each sub-band is **downsampled** (decimated) by factor $M$.
3. Each sub-band is **quantized** with bits allocated based on perceptual importance.
4. At receiver, sub-bands are **upsampled** (interpolated) and **recombined**.

**Quadrature Mirror Filter (QMF) Bank Derivation:**

A two-band QMF splits signal $x(n)$ into low-pass $x_L(n)$ and high-pass $x_H(n)$ components. To allow perfect reconstruction, the analysis filters must satisfy:

$$
H_1(z) = H_0(-z), \quad G_0(z) = H_0(z), \quad G_1(z) = -H_1(z)
$$

For a two-band system, the **aliasing cancellation condition** is:

$$
H_0(-z) H_1(z) - H_0(z) H_1(-z) = 2z^{-d}
$$

The reconstruction output is:

$$
\hat{X}(z) = \frac{1}{2}\left[H_0(z)G_0(z) + H_1(z)G_1(z)\right]X(z) + \frac{1}{2}\left[H_0(-z)G_0(z) + H_1(-z)G_1(z)\right]X(-z)
$$

**Step-by-Step Algebraic Simplification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{X}(z) &= \frac{1}{2}\left[H_0(z)^2 - H_1(z)H_0(-z)\right]X(z) \\
&\quad + \frac{1}{2}\left[H_0(-z)H_0(z) - H_1(-z)H_0(-z)\right]X(-z) \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\left[H_0^2(z) - H_0(-z)H_1(z)\right]X(z) \\
&\quad + \frac{1}{2}H_0(-z)\left[H_0(z) - H_1(-z)\right]X(-z)
\end{aligned}
$$

For **perfect reconstruction**, the aliasing term must vanish:

$$
H_0(-z)\left[H_0(z) - H_1(-z)\right] = 0
$$

This requires $H_1(-z) = H_0(z)$, i.e., $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$ ŌĆö the **mirror filter** condition.

**Final reconstructed signal:**

$$
\hat{X}(z) = \frac{1}{2}\left[H_0^2(z) - H_0^2(-z)\right]X(z)
$$

**Bit Allocation in Sub-Band Coding:**

If $B$ total bits per frame are to be distributed across $M$ sub-bands:

$$
B = \sum_{k=1}^{M} B_k
$$

Allocation based on **energy** in each band:

$$
B_k = B_{\text{total}} \cdot \frac{\sigma_k^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{M} \sigma_i^2}
$$

Where $\sigma_k^2$ is the variance of the $k$-th sub-band signal.

### 3.2 Adaptive Transform Coding (ATC) - Detailed Working

ATC uses **block transforms** (typically **DCT** or **DFT**) to decorrelate the speech signal.

**Operational Steps:**

1. Block the input speech into frames of size $N$ (typically $N=128$ or $256$).
2. Apply **DCT** to each frame: $X(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \cos\left[\frac{\pi}{N}(n+\frac{1}{2})k\right]$.
3. Compute **adaptive bit allocation** based on variance of each coefficient.
4. Quantize each coefficient with the allocated bits.
5. At receiver, inverse DCT reconstructs the time-domain signal.

**Bit Allocation in ATC:**

For $N$ coefficients with variances $\sigma_1^2, \sigma_2^2, \ldots, \sigma_N^2$, the bits per coefficient $b_k$ are assigned to minimize total quantization error:

$$
b_k = \bar{b} + \frac{1}{2}\log_2\left(\frac{\sigma_k^2}{\left[\prod_{i=1}^{N}\sigma_i^2\right]^{1/N}}\right)
$$

Where $\bar{b}$ is the average bits per coefficient.

**Detailed Derivation of Bit Allocation:**

The total quantization distortion is approximated by:

$$
D = \sum_{k=1}^{N} E\left[(X_k - \hat{X}_k)^2\right] = \sum_{k=1}^{N} c \cdot 2^{-2b_k} \sigma_k^2
$$

Where $c$ is a constant depending on quantizer type. Minimize $D$ subject to $\sum b_k = B$ using Lagrange multipliers:

$$
\mathcal{L} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} c \cdot 2^{-2b_k} \sigma_k^2 + \lambda\left(\sum_{k=1}^{N} b_k - B\right)
$$

Taking partial derivative with respect to $b_k$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b_k} = -2\ln(2) \cdot c \cdot 2^{-2b_k} \sigma_k^2 + \lambda = 0
$$

Solving:

$$
2^{-2b_k} \sigma_k^2 = \frac{\lambda}{2c \ln(2)} = \text{constant} = \sigma^2_{\text{eff}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
2^{2b_k} = \frac{\sigma_k^2}{\sigma^2_{\text{eff}}} \implies b_k = \frac{1}{2}\log_2\left(\frac{\sigma_k^2}{\sigma^2_{\text{eff}}}\right)
$$

The constant $\sigma^2_{\text{eff}}$ is determined by the constraint $\sum b_k = B$:

$$
\sigma^2_{\text{eff}} = \left[\prod_{k=1}^{N} \sigma_k^2\right]^{1/N} \cdot 2^{2(\bar{b}-B/N)}
$$

Substituting back yields the final bit allocation formula above.

### 3.3 Phase Vocoder - Detailed Mathematical Framework

**Analysis Stage:**

Apply **Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT)** to the input signal $x(n)$:

$$
X(m,k) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n) w(n - mH) e^{-j\omega_k n}
$$

Where:
- $m$ = frame index (time).
- $k$ = frequency bin index.
- $w(n)$ = window function (typically Hann).
- $H$ = hop size.

**Compute Magnitude and Phase:**

$$
|X(m,k)| = \sqrt{\text{Re}\{X(m,k)\}^2 + \text{Im}\{X(m,k)\}^2}
$$

$$
\phi(m,k) = \angle X(m,k) = \text{atan2}(\text{Im}\{X(m,k)\}, \text{Re}\{X(m,k)\})
$$

**Instantaneous Frequency Derivation:**

The phase difference between successive frames gives the **instantaneous frequency**:

$$
\Delta\phi(m,k) = \phi(m,k) - \phi(m,k-1)
$$

**Phase Unwrapping:**

Raw phase differences are wrapped to $[-\pi, \pi]$. To unwrap, add multiples of $2\pi$:

$$
\phi_u(m,k) = \phi(m,k) + 2\pi \cdot \text{round}\left(\frac{\phi(m-1,k) + \Delta\phi_{\text{expected}}(m,k) - \phi(m,k)}{2\pi}\right)
$$

Where $\Delta\phi_{\text{expected}}(m,k)$ is the expected phase advance based on the bin frequency.

**Synthesis Stage:**

For **time-stretching by factor $\alpha$**, the synthesis hop is $\alpha H$. The synthesis phase is accumulated:

$$
\Phi_s(m,k) = \Phi_s(m-1,k) + \alpha \cdot \Delta\phi_{\text{analysis}}(m,k)
$$

Inverse STFT with overlap-add reconstructs the time-stretched signal:

$$
y(n) = \sum_{m} \left(\sum_{k} |X_s(m,k)| e^{j\Phi_s(m,k)} e^{j\omega_k n}\right) \cdot w_s(n - mH\alpha)
$$

**Pitch Shifting (Frequency Scaling):**

For pitch shifting by factor $\beta$, modify only the magnitude spectrum using frequency scaling, while preserving the phase progression. The relationship between time-scale $\alpha$ and pitch-scale $\beta$ is $\beta = 1/\alpha$.

### 3.4 LPC Vocoder - Source-Filter Parameter Estimation

**Linear Predictive Coding (LPC)** models speech as the output of an all-pole filter driven by an excitation signal:

$$
s(n) = \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k s(n-k) + G \cdot e(n)
$$

The predictor coefficients $\{a_k\}$ are computed using the **Levinson-Durbin algorithm** on the autocorrelation sequence $R(k)$ of the speech frame:

**Step 1:** Initialize:

$$
E^{(0)} = R(0), \quad a_0^{(0)} = 1
$$

**Step 2:** For $i = 1, 2, \ldots, p$:

$$
k_i = -\frac{1}{E^{(i-1)}}\left[R(i) + \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} a_j^{(i-1)} R(i-j)\right]
$$

$$
a_i^{(i)} = k_i
$$

For $j = 1, 2, \ldots, i-1$:

$$
a_j^{(i)} = a_j^{(i-1)} + k_i \cdot a_{i-j}^{(i-1)}
$$

**Step 3:** Update error:

$$
E^{(i)} = E^{(i-1)}(1 - k_i^2)
$$

The final LPC coefficients are $\{a_1^{(p)}, a_2^{(p)}, \ldots, a_p^{(p)}\}$. The reflection coefficients $k_i$ must satisfy $\vert k_i \vert < 1$ for stability.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Block Diagram - Speech Coding Ecosystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inputMic["Microphone Input"] --> preproc["Pre processing Anti aliasing filter and Sampling at 8 kHz or 16 kHz"]
    preproc --> timeDomain{"Time Domain Coders"}
    preproc --> spectralDomain{"Spectral Domain Coders"}
    preproc --> vocoders{"Vocoders Model based"}

    timeDomain --> pcm["PCM 64 kbps"]
    timeDomain --> dpcm["DPCM"]
    timeDomain --> adpcm["ADPCM 32 kbps G 726"]
    timeDomain --> apc["APC 16 kbps"]

    spectralDomain --> sbc["Sub Band Coding QMF filter bank"]
    spectralDomain --> atc["Adaptive Transform Coding DCT based"]

    vocoders --> channelVocoder["Channel Vocoder"]
    vocoders --> lpcVocoder["LPC Vocoder 2.4 kbps"]
    vocoders --> phaseVocoder["Phase Vocoder Time Pitch Manipulation"]

    pcm --> txChannel["Transmission Channel or Storage"]
    dpcm --> txChannel
    adpcm --> txChannel
    apc --> txChannel
    sbc --> txChannel
    atc --> txChannel
    channelVocoder --> txChannel
    lpcVocoder --> txChannel
    phaseVocoder --> txChannel

    txChannel --> receiver["Receiver"]
    receiver --> decode["Decoding and Synthesis"]
    decode --> outputSpeaker["Loudspeaker Output"]
```

### 4.2 Sub-Band Coding Architecture (Two-Band Analysis-Synthesis)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inNode["Input Speech x of n"] --> h0["H0 z Low pass Analysis"]
    inNode --> h1["H1 z High pass Analysis"]

    h0 --> down2["Downsample by M equals 2"]
    h1 --> down21["Downsample by M equals 2"]

    down2 --> qL["Quantizer Low band B0 bits"]
    down21 --> qH["Quantizer High band B1 bits"]

    qL --> encL["Encoder Bit allocation"]
    qH --> encH["Encoder Bit allocation"]

    encL --> ch["Channel or Storage"]
    encH --> ch

    ch --> decL["Decoder Low band"]
    ch --> decH["Decoder High band"]

    decL --> upL["Upsample by 2"]
    decH --> upH["Upsample by 2"]

    upL --> g0["G0 z Low pass Synthesis"]
    upH --> g1["G1 z High pass Synthesis"]

    g0 --> sumNode["Summation Node"]
    g1 --> sumNode

    sumNode --> outNode["Reconstructed Speech x hat of n"]
```

### 4.3 Adaptive Transform Coding Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    speechInput["Speech Frame of Size N"] --> window["Windowing Hann Window"]
    window --> dctBlock["DCT Transform X k"]
    dctBlock --> varCalc["Compute Variance of Each Coefficient sigma k squared"]
    varCalc --> bitAlloc["Adaptive Bit Allocation Algorithm"]
    bitAlloc --> quantBank["Quantization Bank Q1 Q2 ... QN"]
    quantBank --> entropyEnc["Entropy Encoding Huffman"]
    entropyEnc --> bitstream["Compressed Bitstream"]

    bitstream --> entropyDec["Entropy Decoding"]
    entropyDec --> dequant["Dequantization Bank"]
    dequant --> idct["Inverse DCT"]
    idct --> overlapAdd["Overlap Add Synthesis"]
    overlapAdd --> reconstructedOut["Reconstructed Speech"]
```

### 4.4 Phase Vocoder - Analysis-Modification-Synthesis

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    audioIn["Input Audio x of n"] --> stftAnalysis["STFT Analysis Windowing and FFT"]
    stftAnalysis --> polarDecomp["Polar Decomposition Magnitude and Phase"]

    polarDecomp --> magPath["Magnitude Spectrum X mag of m k"]
    polarDecomp --> phasePath["Phase Spectrum phi of m k"]

    phasePath --> unwrap["Phase Unwrapping Remove 2 pi Discontinuities"]
    unwrap --> instFreq["Instantaneous Frequency Computation delta phi"]

    instFreq --> phaseAccum["Phase Accumulation Time Scale alpha"]
    magPath --> synthFrame["Synthesis Frame Magnitude Retention"]

    phaseAccum --> synthFrame
    synthFrame --> istft["Inverse STFT Overlap Add Synthesis"]
    istft --> audioOut["Modified Audio Time Stretched by alpha"]

    synthFrame -.->|"Pitch Shift beta equals 1 over alpha"| pitchShift["Pitch Manipulation Module"]
    pitchShift --> audioOut2["Pitch Shifted Audio"]
```

### 4.5 Speech Coder Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root["Speech Coders"] --> timeClass["Time Domain Coders Waveform Followers"]
    root --> spectralClass["Spectral Domain Coders Frequency Domain"]
    root --> vocoderClass["Vocoders Parametric Model Based"]

    timeClass --> t1["PCM 64 kbps"]
    timeClass --> t2["DPCM 48 kbps"]
    timeClass --> t3["ADPCM 16 to 32 kbps"]
    timeClass --> t4["APC 16 kbps"]

    spectralClass --> s1["Sub Band Coding 16 to 32 kbps"]
    spectralClass --> s2["Adaptive Transform Coding 16 kbps"]
    spectralClass --> s3["Perceptual Coders 32 to 64 kbps"]

    vocoderClass --> v1["Channel Vocoder 2.4 kbps"]
    vocoderClass --> v2["Formant Vocoder 1.2 kbps"]
    vocoderClass --> v3["LPC Vocoder 2.4 to 4.8 kbps"]
    vocoderClass --> v4["Phase Vocoder Time Pitch Modification"]
    vocoderClass --> v5["Hybrid Vocoders CELP 4.8 to 16 kbps"]
```

### 4.6 LPC Vocoder Source-Filter Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pitchDetect["Pitch Detector F0 in Hz"] --> voicedFlag["Voiced or Unvoiced Decision"]
    voicedFlag --> switchSelector{"Voiced Decision Switch"}

    switchSelector -->|Voiced| pulseGen["Periodic Pulse Train at F0"]
    switchSelector -->|Unvoiced| noiseGen["Random Noise Generator"]

    pulseGen --> gainAmp["Gain G Energy Parameter"]
    noiseGen --> gainAmp

    gainAmp --> lpcFilter["LPC Synthesis Filter 1 divided by A of z"]
    lpcFilter --> synthSpeech["Synthesized Speech Output"]

    pitchDetect --> tx["Transmit Pitch F0 Voiced Flag Gain G LPC Coeffs"]
    voicedFlag --> tx
    gainAmp --> tx
    lpcFilter --> tx
```
