4. The encoder allocates quantization bits *only* to spectral components that lie *above* the masking threshold. Components below the threshold are discarded (perceptual lossless compression).

**Numerical Example (2 marks):**
- *Within-band masking:* A 1 kHz tone at 60 dB SPL masks a 1.05 kHz tone at 20 dB SPL (separation 50 Hz < critical bandwidth 160 Hz) ŌĆö the weaker tone is inaudible.
- *Cross-band (no masking):* The same 60 dB tone at 1 kHz does *not* mask a 2 kHz tone at 20 dB SPL (separation 1000 Hz Ōē½ CB of ~300 Hz at 2 kHz) ŌĆö the weaker tone is audible.
- *Upward spread of masking:* A 1 kHz masker at 60 dB SPL can mask a 2 kHz tone at 20 dB SPL, but a 2 kHz masker at 60 dB SPL does *not* mask a 1 kHz tone at 20 dB SPL ŌĆö masking spreads upward more than downward.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Module 4 ŌĆö Sound Perception)**
>
> 1. **Unit confusion in Bark/ERB formulas:** Forgetting that $f$ must be in **Hz** (not kHz) in the Zwicker formula. Always write $0.00076\,f$, not $0.76\,f$ ŌĆö costs ~1 mark.
> 2. **Misstating the reference pressure:** The ATH is referenced to **20 ┬ĄPa**, *not* 1 ┬ĄPa (which is the underwater acoustics reference). A wrong reference invalidates the entire numeric answer.
> 3. **Confusing Bark with ERB:** Bark scale has 25 units; ERB-rate has ~43 units. They are *not* interchangeable. Always state which scale you are using.
> 4. **Forgetting to draw/describe a block diagram:** KTU examiners *expect* a labeled diagram in any 7-mark sub-part on auditory filter banks ŌĆö omitting it costs up to 1 mark.
> 5. **Ignoring non-linearity:** Stating that "the basilar membrane is a linear filter bank" is incorrect ŌĆö it is **level-dependent** (sharper at low SPL, broader at high SPL). Examiners test this explicitly.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Sound perception** = acoustic-to-neural transduction by the human auditory periphery (outer ear ŌåÆ middle ear ŌåÆ cochlea ŌåÆ auditory nerve ŌåÆ cortex).
- **Auditory filter bank** = tonotopically organized set of ~25 overlapping band-pass filters on the basilar membrane, non-linear and level-dependent.
- **Place theory (B├®k├®sy):** Each frequency activates a specific physical location on the basilar membrane.
- **Critical band** = bandwidth of one auditory filter, in which intra-band masking occurs.
- **Bark scale:** $z = 13\arctan(0.00076\,f) + 3.5\arctan[(f/7500)^{2}]$; range 0ŌĆō25.
- **Critical bandwidth (Zwicker):** $\Delta f_{cb} = 25 + 75(1 + 1.4(f/1000)^{2})^{0.69}$ Hz.
- **ERB (GlasbergŌĆōMoore):** $\mathrm{ERB}(f) = 24.7(4.37 f/1000 + 1)$ Hz.
- **ATH (ISO 226:2003):** 0 dB SPL at 1 kHz, reference $p_0 = 20$ ┬ĄPa; minimum Ōēł ŌłÆ3 to ŌłÆ4 dB SPL at 2ŌĆō5 kHz; rises to 70ŌĆō80 dB SPL at 20 Hz and 20 kHz.
- **Simultaneous masking:** within critical band; **upward spread** dominates.
- **Temporal masking:** pre-mask (5ŌĆō20 ms) + post-mask (50ŌĆō200 ms).
- **Engineering applications:** perceptual audio codecs (MP3, AAC), speech recognition (PLP, RASTA-PLP), hearing aid multi-band compression, noise suppression, equal-loudness contour design.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Masking - Simultaneous Masking, Temporal Masking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST866 - SPEECH AND AUDIO PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Masking - Simultaneous Masking, Temporal Masking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Masking (Psychoacoustic Masking)

**Psychoacoustic Masking** is a perceptual phenomenon of the human auditory system in which the perception of one sound (the *maskee* or *probe*) is reduced or completely suppressed by the presence of another sound (the *masker*) occurring at a nearby frequency or close in time. This effect arises due to the **frequency selectivity** and **temporal resolution limitations** of the cochlea inside the inner ear.

Mathematically, masking is defined as the increase in the **hearing threshold** of a target sound caused by the presence of a masking signal. The masking threshold $T_m(f)$ represents the minimum Sound Pressure Level (SPL) at frequency $f$ below which the probe becomes inaudible in the presence of the masker.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECST866 - Module 4):** Masking is the cornerstone of all *perceptual audio coders* (PAC) such as **MP3 (MPEG-1 Layer III)**, **AAC**, and **Ogg Vorbis**. By exploiting masking, encoders safely discard inaudible audio components, achieving high compression ratios (typically **10:1 to 12:1**) with no perceived loss in quality.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sitting in a quiet library trying to read a book (the *maskee* ŌĆö a quiet, soft sound). Suddenly, a large truck rumbles past the window (the *masker* ŌĆö a loud, low-frequency rumble). Even though the rustle of pages is still physically present in the air, you can no longer hear it because the truck's sound has *drowned it out*. Your ear-brain system has effectively "masked" the quieter sound.

Similarly, when a loud note is played on a piano, the faint overtones or nearby notes that would normally be audible in silence are *masked* and become inaudible. This is the foundation of perceptual audio coding ŌĆö we only need to encode the sounds the human ear can actually perceive.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terms to Remember:**
> - **Masker**: The loud, dominant sound that causes the masking effect.
> - **Maskee / Probe**: The softer sound that becomes inaudible.
> - **Masking Threshold**: The SPL below which sounds are not perceived.
> - **Threshold of Hearing (TOH)**: The minimum SPL audible in a perfectly quiet environment (~**0 dB SPL at 4 kHz**).

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Threshold of Quiet (TOH)** | **0 dB SPL** at 4 kHz | Reference hearing threshold in silence |
| **Reference Sound Pressure** | **20 ┬ĄPa** | 0 dB SPL reference |
| **Critical Bandwidth** | **100 Hz ŌĆō 4 kHz** (varies with center freq.) | Cochlear filter bandwidth |
| **Number of Critical Bands** | **25 Bark** (or ~24) | Covers 0 Hz to ~16 kHz |
| **Time constants of masking** | **5 ms (pre)**, **50ŌĆō200 ms (post)** | Temporal masking windows |
| **Loudest tolerable SPL** | **~120 dB SPL** | Threshold of pain |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Masking

Psychoacoustic masking is broadly classified into two major types:

### 2.1.1 Simultaneous Masking (Frequency Masking / Spectral Masking)
Occurs when the **masker and maskee are presented at the same time** but at different frequencies. The masker raises the hearing threshold in a region of frequencies around its center frequency.

- **Most effective** when both sounds lie within the **same critical band**.
- Effectiveness decreases as the frequency separation between masker and maskee increases.
- Usually more prominent for **high-frequency maskers** suppressing lower-frequency probes (the "upward spread of masking" is smaller than the "downward spread").

### 2.1.2 Temporal Masking (Time-Domain Masking)
Occurs when the **masker and maskee are separated in time** but are close enough that the auditory system cannot resolve them. Two sub-types exist:

1. **Pre-Masking (Backward Masking):** A louder sound *preceding* a softer sound makes the softer sound inaudible. Duration: **~5 to 20 ms**.
2. **Post-Masking (Forward Masking):** A softer sound shortly *after* a louder sound remains inaudible. Duration: **~50 to 200 ms** (significantly longer than pre-masking).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Temporal Masking Exists:** The cochlea does not respond instantaneously. After a loud stimulus, the basilar membrane requires time to return to its resting state, and neural firing rates decay gradually. This "ringing" of the auditory filter is the physiological origin of post-masking.

## 2.2 Critical Bands and the Bark Scale

The cochlea behaves as a bank of overlapping **bandpass filters** known as *auditory filters* or *critical bands*. The bandwidth of these filters is approximately constant on a perceptual scale called the **Bark scale** (named after Heinrich Barkhausen).

The conversion from linear frequency $f$ (in Hz) to the Bark scale $z$ is given by:

$$z = 13 \cdot \arctan\left(0.00076 \cdot f\right) + 3.5 \cdot \arctan\left(\frac{f}{7500}\right)^2$$

The corresponding critical bandwidth $\Delta f$ (in Hz) as a function of Bark $z$ is:

$$\Delta f(z) = 25 + 75 \cdot \left(1 + 1.4 \cdot 10^{-6} \cdot f\right)^{0.69} \quad \text{(approx.)}$$

Or in closed form using the Bark scale:

$$\Delta f(z) \approx 25 + 70 \cdot \left(10^{z/21} - 1\right) \quad \text{(Hz, for } z \ge 4\text{)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Critical bandwidth is the reason the Bark scale (and the similar **Mel scale** used in speech processing) is used in **MP3 encoders** ŌĆö quantization noise is shaped so that it falls *entirely within* one critical band, where it is maximally masked.

## 2.3 Spread of Masking Function (Simultaneous Masking Model)

When a narrow-band masker of SPL $L_M$ exists at frequency $f_M$, the resulting masking threshold $L_T(f)$ at a nearby frequency $f$ is modeled by the **Schroeder et al. / MPEG-1 spread function**:

$$L_T(f) = L_M + S F(z_M, z)$$

where $z_M$ and $z$ are the masker and probe frequencies in Bark, and $SF(\cdot)$ is the *spread function*:

$$S F(z_M, z) = -27 \cdot \Delta z + S F_{\text{const}} \quad \text{(dB)}$$

with

$$\Delta z = z_M - z$$

The slope constants are:
- **Lower slope** (masker below probe, $\Delta z > 0$): $-27$ dB/Bark
- **Upper slope** (masker above probe, $\Delta z < 0$): varies

## 2.4 Temporal Masking Function

A simple model for the post-masking threshold decay is:

$$L_T(t) = L_M - k \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{t}{t_0}\right) \quad \text{(dB SPL)}$$

where:
- $L_M$ = masker level (dB SPL)
- $t$ = time after masker offset (ms)
- $t_0$ = reference time (~1 ms)
- $k$ = decay constant (~10 dB per decade of time)

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Parameter | Typical Value | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Bark conversion | $z = 13\arctan(0.00076f) + 3.5\arctan^2(f/7500)$ | 0 ŌĆō 25 | Bark |
| Critical bandwidth | $\Delta f = 25 + 70(10^{z/21}-1)$ | 100 ŌĆō 4000 | Hz |
| Masking threshold | $L_T = L_M + SF(\Delta z)$ | depends on $\Delta z$ | dB SPL |
| Spread function | $SF = -27\,\Delta z$ (lower slope) | $-27$ | dB/Bark |
| Pre-masking duration | $t_{\text{pre}}$ | $5$ to $20$ | ms |
| Post-masking duration | $t_{\text{post}}$ | $50$ to $200$ | ms |
| TOH at 4 kHz | $L_{TOH}$ | $0$ | dB SPL |
| Reference pressure | $p_0$ | $20$ | ┬ĄPa |
| SMR (Signal-to-Mask Ratio) | $SMR = L_S - L_T$ | $\ge 0$ for audibility | dB |
| NMR (Noise-to-Mask Ratio) | $NMR = L_N - L_T$ | $< 0$ : inaudible | dB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **MPEG Audio Coders (MP3, AAC):** Use the masking threshold to allocate bits ŌĆö frequencies with high masking get coarser quantization.
> - **Hearing Aids:** Apply *masking-aware* gain compensation to prevent loud sounds from masking speech.
> - **Noise Cancellation Systems:** Shape anti-noise so its spectral energy falls within the masker's masking region.
> - **Speech Coders (e.g., Opus, EVS):** Exploit temporal masking during voice activity transitions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bark Scale Conversion from Linear Frequency

We derive the relationship that maps linear frequency $f$ (Hz) to the perceptual Bark scale $z$.

**Step 1: Recognize the cochlear mapping**

The basilar membrane tonotopic mapping is approximately logarithmic. The empirical model proposed by **Schroeder (1977)** combines a low-frequency linear term and a high-frequency logarithmic term.

**Step 2: Define the low-frequency term**

For $f \ll 7500$ Hz, the inner-ear response is approximately linear:

$$z_{\text{low}} = 13 \cdot \arctan(0.00076 \cdot f)$$

This is because $\arctan(x) \approx x$ for small $x$, so $z_{\text{low}} \approx 13 \cdot 0.00076 \cdot f = 0.00988 f$ (Hz). At $f = 100$ Hz, $z_{\text{low}} \approx 1$ Bark.

**Step 3: Define the high-frequency correction term**

For $f \gg 500$ Hz, the response becomes logarithmic:

$$z_{\text{high}} = 3.5 \cdot \arctan\left(\frac{f}{7500}\right)^2$$

The squaring emphasizes higher frequencies, modeling the steeper place-to-frequency map near the cochlear base.

**Step 4: Combine the two terms**

Adding both gives a continuous, smooth mapping across the entire audio range:

$$z = 13 \cdot \arctan(0.00076 \cdot f) + 3.5 \cdot \arctan\left(\frac{f}{7500}\right)^2$$

**Step 5: Verify boundary conditions**

At $f = 0$ Hz:

$$z(0) = 13 \cdot \arctan(0) + 3.5 \cdot \arctan(0)^2 = 0 + 0 = 0 \text{ Bark}$$

At $f = 4000$ Hz:

$$z(4000) = 13 \cdot \arctan(3.04) + 3.5 \cdot \arctan(0.533)^2$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
z(4000) &= 13 \cdot 1.252 + 3.5 \cdot (0.490)^2 \\
&= 16.27 + 3.5 \cdot 0.240 \\
&= 16.27 + 0.84 = 17.11 \text{ Bark}
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the known value: 4 kHz corresponds to approximately **17 Bark** out of 25.

At $f = 15000$ Hz:

$$
\begin{aligned}
z(15000) &= 13 \cdot \arctan(11.4) + 3.5 \cdot \arctan(2.0)^2 \\
&= 13 \cdot 1.485 + 3.5 \cdot (1.107)^2 \\
&= 19.31 + 3.5 \cdot 1.225 \\
&= 19.31 + 4.29 = 23.60 \text{ Bark}
\end{aligned}
$$

The full audio range (20 Hz ŌĆō 20 kHz) covers approximately **1 to 24 Bark**, in agreement with the standard **25 critical bands**.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Simultaneous Masking Threshold Calculation

**Problem:** A narrow-band masker at $f_M = 1000$ Hz with SPL $L_M = 60$ dB is present. Compute the masking threshold at probe frequencies $f = 700$ Hz, $f = 1000$ Hz, and $f = 1500$ Hz. Use the simple linear spread function $SF = -27 \cdot \Delta z$ (lower slope).

**Step 1: Convert frequencies to Bark**

Using $z = 13\arctan(0.00076 f) + 3.5\arctan^2(f/7500)$:

At $f = 700$ Hz:

$$
\begin{aligned}
z &= 13 \arctan(0.00076 \cdot 700) + 3.5 \arctan^2(700/7500) \\
&= 13 \arctan(0.532) + 3.5 \arctan^2(0.0933) \\
&= 13 \cdot 0.4889 + 3.5 \cdot (0.0931)^2 \\
&= 6.356 + 0.030 = 6.39 \text{ Bark}
\end{aligned}
$$

At $f = 1000$ Hz:

$$
\begin{aligned}
z &= 13 \arctan(0.76) + 3.5 \arctan^2(0.1333) \\
&= 13 \cdot 0.6486 + 3.5 \cdot (0.1326)^2 \\
&= 8.432 + 0.0615 = 8.49 \text{ Bark}
\end{aligned}
$$

At $f = 1500$ Hz:

$$
\begin{aligned}
z &= 13 \arctan(1.14) + 3.5 \arctan^2(0.20) \\
&= 13 \cdot 0.8520 + 3.5 \cdot (0.1974)^2 \\
&= 11.08 + 0.136 = 11.22 \text{ Bark}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the spread function $SF$ at each probe**

At $f = 700$ Hz, $z = 6.39$ Bark, $z_M = 8.49$ Bark:
$\Delta z = 8.49 - 6.39 = 2.10$ Bark
$SF = -27 \cdot 2.10 = -56.7$ dB

At $f = 1000$ Hz, $z = z_M = 8.49$ Bark:
$\Delta z = 0$ Bark
$SF = 0$ dB

At $f = 1500$ Hz, $z = 11.22$ Bark, $z_M = 8.49$ Bark:
$\Delta z = 8.49 - 11.22 = -2.73$ Bark (upper side)
For the upper slope, $SF = -24 \cdot \Delta z$ (typical model):
$SF = -24 \cdot (-2.73) = +65.5$ dB (clamped to 0 in reality)

**Step 3: Compute masking thresholds**

$$
L_T(f) = L_M + SF(\Delta z)
$$

- $L_T(700) = 60 + (-56.7) = 3.3$ dB SPL
- $L_T(1000) = 60 + 0 = 60.0$ dB SPL
- $L_T(1500) = 60 + (0 \text{ or } 65.5) \approx 60$ dB SPL (in practice the upper-slope masking is limited)

**Step 4: Interpretation**

A probe at 700 Hz is masked if it is softer than **3.3 dB SPL** ŌĆö very likely, since 700 Hz is in a sensitive hearing region. A probe at 1500 Hz is essentially not masked because the upper spread of masking is steep. This is the classic *asymmetric spread of masking*: low-frequency maskers affect higher frequencies less than vice versa.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Temporal (Post-Masking) Threshold Decay

**Problem:** A 70 dB SPL masker ends at $t = 0$. Using the decay model $L_T(t) = L_M - k \log_{10}(t/t_0)$ with $k = 10$ and $t_0 = 1$ ms, compute the masking threshold at $t = 5$ ms, $20$ ms, and $100$ ms.

**Step 1: Substitute values**

At $t = 5$ ms:
$L_T(5) = 70 - 10 \cdot \log_{10}(5) = 70 - 10 \cdot 0.699 = 70 - 6.99 = 63.0$ dB SPL

At $t = 20$ ms:
$L_T(20) = 70 - 10 \cdot \log_{10}(20) = 70 - 10 \cdot 1.301 = 70 - 13.01 = 56.99$ dB SPL

At $t = 100$ ms:
$L_T(100) = 70 - 10 \cdot \log_{10}(100) = 70 - 10 \cdot 2.0 = 70 - 20 = 50.0$ dB SPL

**Step 2: Interpretation**

At 5 ms after the masker ends, sounds below 63 dB SPL are inaudible. At 100 ms, only sounds below 50 dB SPL are still inaudible. The masking threshold decays logarithmically ŌĆö a **10 dB drop per decade (10├Ś increase) in time**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Masking Effectiveness Summary

| Masking Type | Effective Range | Max Masking Level (dB) | Decay / Spread Rule | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Simultaneous (low-side)** | $\pm 1$ to $\pm 3$ Bark | up to 25 dB | $-27$ dB/Bark (lower slope) | MP3 bit allocation |
| **Simultaneous (high-side)** | $\pm 1$ to $\pm 1.5$ Bark | up to 12 dB | Steeper upper slope | AAC long-block windows |
| **Pre-masking** | $-20$ to $0$ ms | up to 20 dB | Rapid onset, hard to model | Attack transients in music |
| **Post-masking** | $0$ to $+200$ ms | up to 30 dB | $10$ dB/decade decay | Reverberation perception |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Mermaid: Simultaneous Masking

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Audio Signal Input] --> B[Time-Frequency Analysis STFT]
    B --> C[Compute Power Spectrum per Critical Band]
    C --> D{Identify Masker Tones}
    D --> E1[Masker at 1 kHz]
    D --> E2[Masker at 4 kHz]
    D --> E3[Masker at 8 kHz]
    E1 --> F1[Apply Spread Function -27 dB per Bark]
    E2 --> F2[Apply Spread Function]
    E3 --> F3[Apply Spread Function]
    F1 --> G[Combine All Masking Thresholds]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Global Masking Threshold Curve]
    H --> I[Bit Allocation in Perceptual Encoder]
    I --> J[Quantization Noise Below Threshold]
    J --> K[Compressed Bitstream MP3 / AAC]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Mermaid: Temporal Masking Timeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Timeline
        direction LR
        T1[Pre-Masking Window -20 to 0 ms] --> T2[Masker ON 0 to 50 ms Loud Tone] --> T3[Post-Masking Window 0 to 200 ms Decay]
    end
    T1 --> A1[Soft sound before masker becomes inaudible]
    T2 --> A2[Strong neural firing on basilar membrane]
    T3 --> A3[Threshold decays logarithmically 10 dB per decade]
    A1 --> Outcome[Effective Masking Window Approximately 220 ms total]
    A2 --> Outcome
    A3 --> Outcome
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Masking in a Perceptual Audio Encoder

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Input[PCM Audio Input] --> FilterBank[Polyphase / MDCT Filter Bank 32 sub-bands or 1024-point MDCT]
    FilterBank --> Spectral[Spectral Coefficient Matrix]
    Spectral --> PsychoModel[Psychoacoustic Model]
    PsychoModel --> TOH[Threshold of Hearing Curve from ISO 226]
    PsychoModel --> CriticalBand[Critical Band Aggregation 25 Bark]
    PsychoModel --> MaskerDetect[Masker Detection Tonal and Noise Maskers]
    PsychoModel --> Spread[Apply Masking Spread Function]
    PsychoModel --> GlobalThr[Compute Global Masking Threshold]
    GlobalThr --> SMR[SMR Signal to Mask Ratio]
    SMR --> BitAlloc[Bit Allocation Module]
    BitAlloc --> Quant[Quantization and Coding]
    Quant --> Bitstream[Output Bitstream]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Diagram: Classification of Masking Phenomena

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Mask[Auditory Masking] --> Sim[Simultaneous Masking Frequency Domain]
    Mask --> Temp[Temporal Masking Time Domain]
    Sim --> SimA[Critical Band Masking Same Band]
    Sim --> SimB[Spread Masking Adjacent Bands]
    Temp --> TempA[Pre-Masking Backward 5 to 20 ms]
    Temp --> TempB[Post-Masking Forward 50 to 200 ms]
    SimA --> App1[Used in MP3 inner loop]
    SimB --> App2[Used in AAC bit reservoir]
    TempA --> App3[Attack smoothing in coders]
    TempB --> App4[Reverberation perception in hearing]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]
**Define psychoacoustic masking. Distinguish between simultaneous masking and temporal masking.**

**Model Answer:**

Psychoacoustic masking is a perceptual phenomenon in which the presence of one sound (the *masker*) reduces or completely eliminates the perception of another sound (the *maskee*) due to limitations in the frequency and time resolution of the human auditory system.

**Distinction:**

| Parameter | Simultaneous Masking | Temporal Masking |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Domain** | Frequency domain | Time domain |
| **Onset** | Masker and maskee occur together | Masker and maskee are separated in time |
| **Mechanism** | Overlap of critical band excitation | Persistence of basilar-membrane vibration |
| **Duration** | Continuous while masker is on | $\sim 5$ to $200$ ms |
| **Sub-types** | Critical-band masking, spread masking | Pre-masking, post-masking |
| **Application** | Spectral bit allocation in MP3 | Smooth transients in AAC |

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition, 2 marks for clear distinction)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]
**What is meant by the threshold of hearing (TOH)? How does it vary with frequency?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Threshold of Hearing (TOH)** is the minimum Sound Pressure Level (SPL) at which a pure tone is just audible to a human listener in a perfectly quiet environment. It is a function of frequency and is given by the **ISO 226:2003** equal-loudness contour.

- Minimum TOH is approximately **0 dB SPL at 4 kHz** (most sensitive region).
- TOH rises sharply at very low frequencies (e.g., $\sim 60$ dB SPL at 30 Hz) and at very high frequencies ($\sim 25$ dB SPL at 16 kHz).
- The reference sound pressure is $p_0 = 20\ \mu$Pa.

In perceptual audio coding, the masking threshold is computed as the **maximum of TOH and the spread-of-masking function** at each frequency.

*(3 marks: 1 mark TOH definition, 1 mark frequency variation, 1 mark role in coding)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper, Module 4]

**(a)** Explain the concept of simultaneous masking with a suitable diagram. Discuss the role of critical bands and the spread of masking function in determining the masking threshold. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A narrow-band masker at 2 kHz with SPL of 70 dB is present. Compute the masking threshold at probe frequencies 1.5 kHz, 2 kHz, and 3 kHz using the lower slope spread function $SF = -27 \cdot \Delta z$ where $\Delta z$ is in Bark. Comment on the asymmetry of the masking spread. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

Simultaneous masking occurs when a louder sound (masker) makes a softer sound (maskee) inaudible when they are presented at the same time but at different frequencies.

**Key Concepts:**

1. **Critical Bands:** The cochlea behaves as a bank of overlapping bandpass filters, each of width $\Delta f$ (Hz) corresponding to 1 Bark. The bandwidth grows with center frequency: $\Delta f \approx 25 + 70 \cdot (10^{z/21} - 1)$.

2. **Masking within a Critical Band:** When masker and maskee fall in the same critical band, masking is most effective. The threshold within a band is approximately constant.

3. **Spread of Masking:** When the maskee lies outside the masker's critical band, the masking effect decreases with frequency distance. The empirical spread function in Bark coordinates is:

$$SF(\Delta z) = \begin{cases} -27 \cdot \Delta z & \text{(lower side, } \Delta z > 0\text{)} \\ -24 \cdot \Delta z & \text{(upper side, } \Delta z < 0\text{)} \end{cases}$$

4. **Global Masking Threshold:**

$$L_T(f) = \max\Big(L_{TOH}(f),\ L_M + SF(\Delta z)\Big)$$

The diagram of simultaneous masking is a curve showing the raised hearing threshold centered at the masker frequency, with a steeper upper slope and gentler lower slope.

*(7 marks: 2 marks concept, 2 marks critical bands, 2 marks spread function, 1 mark diagram)*

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1: Convert frequencies to Bark using $z = 13\arctan(0.00076f) + 3.5\arctan^2(f/7500)$:**

At $f_M = 2000$ Hz:

$$
\begin{aligned}
z_M &= 13\arctan(1.52) + 3.5 \arctan^2(0.2667) \\
&= 13 \cdot 0.9897 + 3.5 \cdot (0.2608)^2 \\
&= 12.866 + 0.238 = 13.10 \text{ Bark}
\end{aligned}
$$

At $f = 1500$ Hz: $z = 11.22$ Bark (computed in ┬¦3.2).

At $f = 3000$ Hz:

$$
\begin{aligned}
z &= 13\arctan(2.28) + 3.5 \arctan^2(0.40) \\
&= 13 \cdot 1.157 + 3.5 \cdot (0.3805)^2 \\
&= 15.04 + 0.507 = 15.55 \text{ Bark}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute $\Delta z$ and $SF$:**

| Probe | $z$ (Bark) | $\Delta z = z_M - z$ | $SF$ (dB) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1.5 kHz | 11.22 | $+1.88$ | $-50.76$ |
| 2 kHz | 13.10 | $0$ | $0$ |
| 3 kHz | 15.55 | $-2.45$ | $\approx 0$ (upper side, clamped) |

**Step 3: Masking thresholds:**

- $L_T(1.5 \text{ kHz}) = 70 - 50.76 = 19.24$ dB SPL
- $L_T(2 \text{ kHz}) = 70 + 0 = 70$ dB SPL
- $L_T(3 \text{ kHz}) = 70$ dB SPL (practical upper limit)

**Step 4: Asymmetry comment:**

The lower side (probe below masker) shows a substantial masking effect (threshold raised from ~6 dB TOH to 19.24 dB SPL). The upper side shows almost no masking because the upper slope is much steeper. This asymmetry is a fundamental property of the human auditory system ŌĆö **low-frequency maskers have a wider spread of influence than high-frequency maskers.**

*(7 marks: 2 marks Bark conversion, 2 marks spread computation, 2 marks final thresholds, 1 mark asymmetry discussion)*

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Sample, Module 4 Alternative]

**(a)** Discuss temporal masking in detail. Differentiate between pre-masking and post-masking with reference to their durations, physiological causes, and applications in audio coding. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 65 dB SPL masker terminates at $t = 0$. Estimate the post-masking threshold at $t = 2$ ms, 50 ms, and 200 ms using the decay model $L_T(t) = L_M - 10 \log_{10}(t/t_0)$ with $t_0 = 1$ ms. If a probe tone of 45 dB SPL arrives 50 ms after the masker, will it be audible? Justify. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Temporal Masking** occurs when the masker and maskee are separated in time, exploiting the limited temporal resolution of the auditory system. It has two components:

**1. Pre-Masking (Backward Masking):**
- **Definition:** A softer sound preceding a louder masker becomes inaudible.
- **Duration:** Typically **5 to 20 ms** (very short).
- **Physiological Cause:** Neural firing in the auditory nerve requires a finite latency ($\sim 5$ to $10$ ms); a stronger subsequent stimulus overwrites the earlier neural response at higher brain centers.
- **Application:** Used in encoders to handle *attack transients* (e.g., castanets, percussive onsets) where pre-echo distortion is suppressed.

**2. Post-Masking (Forward Masking):**
- **Definition:** A softer sound shortly after the masker remains inaudible.
- **Duration:** **50 to 200 ms** (significantly longer).
- **Physiological Cause:** The basilar membrane continues to vibrate after the stimulus ends; hair-cell refractoriness and neural adaptation prolong the elevated threshold.
- **Application:** Exploited in the *bit reservoir* of MP3 and the *window switching* of AAC to borrow bits from silent post-masked regions.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Pre-Masking | Post-Masking |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Duration | 5 ŌĆō 20 ms | 50 ŌĆō 200 ms |
| Cause | Neural latency | Membrane ringing |
| Magnitude | Smaller | Larger |
| Coder use | Attack handling | Bit-reservoir borrowing |

*(7 marks: 2 marks each for pre and post, 1 mark comparison, 2 marks applications)*

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1: Apply the decay formula** $L_T(t) = 65 - 10 \log_{10}(t/1\text{ ms})$:

At $t = 2$ ms:
$L_T(2) = 65 - 10 \log_{10}(2) = 65 - 10 \cdot 0.301 = 65 - 3.01 = 61.99$ dB SPL

At $t = 50$ ms:
$L_T(50) = 65 - 10 \log_{10}(50) = 65 - 10 \cdot 1.699 = 65 - 16.99 = 48.01$ dB SPL

At $t = 200$ ms:
$L_T(200) = 65 - 10 \log_{10}(200) = 65 - 10 \cdot 2.301 = 65 - 23.01 = 41.99$ dB SPL

**Step 2: Determine audibility of the 45 dB SPL probe at $t = 50$ ms:**

The masking threshold at 50 ms is **48.01 dB SPL**. The probe is at **45 dB SPL**, which is **below** the masking threshold.

**Conclusion:** Since $L_{\text{probe}} = 45 < L_T(50) = 48.01$ dB SPL, the probe is **NOT audible** ŌĆö it is fully masked by the post-masking effect of the 65 dB masker.

**Step 3: Practical implication:**

If this were a music track and the probe represents a soft follow-up note, the encoder can safely quantize this region with very few bits, saving 8ŌĆō12 dB of dynamic range budget.

*(7 marks: 2 marks formula, 3 marks threshold computation, 2 marks audibility judgment and justification)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often confuse *masker* and *maskee*. Remember ŌĆö the **masker is loud and dominant**; the **maskee is the soft sound that becomes inaudible**.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to **clamp the masking threshold** above the TOH. The global threshold is $L_T = \max(L_{TOH},\ L_M + SF)$. Marks deducted if the spread function gives a value *below* TOH.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Treating pre-masking and post-masking as symmetric. They are NOT ŌĆö post-masking is roughly **5 to 10 times longer** in duration.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Failing to convert frequencies to **Bark** before applying the spread function. The slope $-27$ dB/Bark is *perceptual*, not per Hz.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Omitting the reference to **MP3 / AAC** applications ŌĆö KTU examiners reward practical engineering context. Always end masking answers with a one-line application note.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ŌśÉ **Masking = inaudibility of a soft sound due to a louder one**, caused by limitations in cochlear filtering.
- ŌśÉ **Simultaneous Masking** is in the *frequency* domain; effective mainly within **one critical band (~1 Bark)**.
- ŌśÉ **Temporal Masking** is in the *time* domain; **pre-masking (5ŌĆō20 ms)** is much shorter than **post-masking (50ŌĆō200 ms)**.
- ŌśÉ **Bark scale** $z \in [0, 25]$ approximates cochlear tonotopy; conversion formula must be memorized.
- ŌśÉ **Critical bandwidth** $\Delta f$ increases with frequency ŌĆö narrow at low $f$, wide at high $f$.
- ŌśÉ **Spread function** $SF = -27 \cdot \Delta z$ (lower slope, dB/Bark) ŌĆö *not* per Hz.
- ŌśÉ **Upper slope is steeper** than the lower slope, causing asymmetric masking.
- ŌśÉ **Global masking threshold** = maximum of TOH and sum of individual masking contributions.
- ŌśÉ **Masking is the foundation** of MP3, AAC, Ogg Vorbis, Opus ŌĆö enables **10:1 to 12:1 compression** with perceptual transparency.
- ŌśÉ **NMR < 0 dB ŌćÆ quantization noise is inaudible**; the encoder ensures this condition.
- ŌśÉ **SMR (Signal-to-Mask Ratio)** is the working metric for **bit allocation** in the inner loop of perceptual coders.
- ŌśÉ **Pre-masking** is exploited for *attack transient handling*; **post-masking** for *bit-reservoir borrowing*.
- ŌśÉ **Reference SPL** is $p_0 = 20\ \mu$Pa (0 dB SPL).
- ŌśÉ **ISO 226:2003** defines the standard threshold of hearing curve.
- ŌśÉ Always **draw the masking curve** in simultaneous-masking questions; it is worth 1ŌĆō2 marks.
- ŌśÉ Always **state the application** in MP3/AAC at the end of any masking answer ŌĆö examiners love it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Models of Speech Perception
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST866 - SPEECH AND AUDIO PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Models of Speech Perception

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Speech Perception** is the cognitive and perceptual process by which the human auditory system decodes, interprets, and assigns linguistic meaning to an acoustic speech signal. A **Model of Speech Perception** is a theoretical framework that attempts to explain *how* the listener maps the continuously varying acoustic waveform $s(t)$ to discrete phonetic, syllabic, lexical, and ultimately semantic representations in the brain.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, models of speech perception are treated as the bridge between **acoustic-phonetic analysis** (covered in Module 2) and **speech recognition systems** (covered in Module 5). They explain the *human benchmark* against which automatic speech recognition (ASR) performance is measured.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Taxonomy of Perception Models (KTU Syllabus Highlight):**
> 1. **Motor Theory** (Liberman et al., 1967)
> 2. **Analysis-by-Synthesis** (Stevens & Halle, 1967)
> 3. **Direct Realism** (Fowler, 1986)
> 4. **Auditory Theory / Quantal Theory** (Stevens, 1989)
> 5. **Native Language Magnet Model** (Kuhl et al., 1997)
> 6. **Cohort Model** (Marslen-Wilson, 1987)
> 7. **TRACE Model** (McClelland & Elman, 1986)
> 8. **Logogen Model** (Morton, 1969)

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö "The Brain as a Multilingual Detective"

Imagine you are in a noisy caf├® in Paris. A waiter blurts out a rapid stream of sounds: *"Voulez-vous un caf├®?"* Your ear receives a **pressure wave** that bounces off walls, mixes with clinking cups, and overlaps with the espresso machine. Yet, your brain, in less than **400 ms**, recovers the exact words, grammar, and meaning.

How? The brain is functioning like an **incredible detective** that:
- **Filters noise** (auditory scene analysis),
- **Recognizes vocal tract gestures** (motor theory),
- **Hypothesizes and verifies** candidate words (analysis-by-synthesis),
- **Uses top-down context** to fill in ambiguous fragments (cohort model).

A *Model of Speech Perception* is precisely the theoretical "investigation manual" that psychologists and engineers have proposed to describe this detective work.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Value / Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Human auditory range | **20 Hz ŌĆō 20,000 Hz** | Bandwidth of audible speech components |
| Speech fundamental frequency ($F_0$) | **80 Hz ŌĆō 400 Hz** | Adult male to female/child range |
| Critical band (Bark) width at 1 kHz | ~**160 Hz** | Frequency resolution of cochlea |
| Phoneme recognition time | **~ 200 ŌĆō 400 ms** | Latency from acoustic onset to lexical access |
| Speech perception threshold (SRT) | **-26 dB SNR** | Minimum SNR for 50% intelligibility |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Motor Theory of Speech Perception (Liberman & Mattingly, 1985)

**Core Hypothesis:** The listener does not perceive acoustic signals directly. Instead, the listener perceives the **articulatory gestures** (movements of the vocal tract) that the speaker intended to produce. The acoustic signal is merely a *by-product* of these gestures.

**Logical Steps:**
1. Speech production = sequence of invariant **articulatory commands** (gestures).
2. The acoustic signal is highly **context-dependent** (e.g., /b/ vs. /p/ vary with place of articulation).
3. To handle this variability, the listener's brain **recovers the intended gesture** using innate, specialized neural circuits.
4. Therefore, perception = "**telegrapher decoding the sender's keystrokes**," not the electrical pulses on the wire.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important for engineers?** Many HMM-based ASR systems implicitly borrow from motor theory by using *articulatory features* (place, manner, voicing) as intermediate states rather than raw acoustic vectors.

### 2.2 Analysis-by-Synthesis Model (Stevens & Halle)

**Core Hypothesis:** The listener actively **generates hypotheses** about the input and tests them by **synthesizing** what the speech *should* sound like and comparing it to the actual signal.

**Logical Steps:**
1. Lexicon contains entries with **synthesizer parameters** (formants, $F_0$, voicing).
2. The listener produces a **synthesized template** for candidate phoneme.
3. The actual input $s(t)$ is compared to the synthesized template $s'(t)$.
4. If the **perceptual distance** $D(s, s') < \epsilon$, the hypothesis is accepted.

**Engineering Parallel:** This is the conceptual foundation of modern **DNN-HMM hybrid ASR systems** and unit-selection text-to-speech (TTS) engines.

### 2.3 Direct Realism (Fowler, 1986)

The brain perceives speech as **acoustic events** in the world (like any other sound), not as neural codes or hidden gestures. Speech is a *natural kind* of sound, and our perception is **modality-specific** ŌĆö we hear vocal tract resonances directly, just as we see objects directly.

### 2.4 Quantal Theory & Auditory Theory (Stevens, 1989)

**Quantal Speech Production:** Articulatory-acoustic mapping is **non-linear**. Small changes in articulation near *quantal boundaries* (e.g., tongue body position) produce **no acoustic change** (stable regions) and **perceptually distinct landmarks** (boundaries). These landmarks (e.g., [i], [a], [u]) are universal phonetic primitives.

### 2.5 Native Language Magnet Model (Kuhl et al., 1997)

Infants are born with a continuous perceptual space for all possible phonemes. Through exposure to their native language, this space **warps** so that prototype sounds act as **"magnets"** pulling nearby variants toward them. By 6ŌĆō12 months, the magnet effect reduces discrimination of non-native contrasts.

### 2.6 Cohort Model (Marslen-Wilson, 1987)

**Bottom-up lexical access:**
1. The acoustic onset activates a **cohort** (set) of all candidate words beginning with that sound.
2. As more acoustic information arrives, the cohort is **pruned** word-by-word.
3. The **uniqueness point** is the moment only one word remains in the cohort.
4. **Contextual information** (top-down) can pre-select a smaller initial cohort.

### 2.7 TRACE Model (McClelland & Elman, 1986)

A **connectionist interactive activation model** with three layers:
- **Feature layer** (acoustic features)
- **Phoneme layer** (consonants/vowels)
- **Word layer** (lexical items)

Bidirectional excitatory connections + lateral inhibition create **competition**. Top-down (word ŌåÆ phoneme ŌåÆ feature) and bottom-up (feature ŌåÆ phoneme ŌåÆ word) flow simulate context effects (e.g., phoneme restoration effect).

### 2.8 Logogen Model (Morton, 1969)

Each word in the mental lexicon is a **logogen** ŌĆö a counter-like device. Acoustic, semantic, and contextual inputs *increment* the logogen's activation. When activation exceeds a **threshold**, the word is *detected*. Faster threshold crossing = faster recognition.

### 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for Module 4 Examination**

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $s(t) = A(t) \cdot \cos\!\left(2\pi \int_0^t F_0(\tau)d\tau + \phi(t)\right)$ | Source-filter speech model | Foundation of analysis-by-synthesis |
| 2 | $D(s, s') = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} (s(t) - s'(t))^2 \, dt$ | Perceptual distance metric | Hypothesis testing in AbS |
| 3 | $\text{Mel}(f) = 1127 \ln\!\left(1 + \frac{f}{700}\right)$ | Mel scale (perceptual pitch) | MFCC extraction in ASR |
| 4 | $B_c(f) = 25 + 75(1 + 1.4(f/1000)^{2})^{0.69}$ | Critical bandwidth (Hz) | Bark filterbank design |
| 5 | $\text{PLP}(n) = \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, n(n-k)$ | Perceptual Linear Prediction | Robust speech features |
| 6 | $P(\text{word} \mid \text{acoustics}) \propto P(\text{acoustics} \mid \text{word}) \cdot P(\text{word})$ | Bayesian word recognition | N-gram language model |
| 7 | $A_i = \sum_j w_{ij} \cdot x_j - \theta_i$ | Logogen activation | Connectionist perception |
| 8 | $f_{\text{cochlear}} = 2.46 \cdot \log_{10}(f_{\text{Hz}}) - 16.7$ | Greenwood frequency-place map | Cochlear filterbank placement |
| 9 | $T_{\text{phoneme}} \approx 50$ŌĆō$\,100$ ms | Phoneme perception window | Frame sizing in ASR |
| 10 | $U(w) = \min\{t : \vert C_t(w) \vert = 1\}$ | Uniqueness point time | Cohort model lexical access |

> **Note on the equation formatting:** The vertical bars $\vert \cdot \vert$ denote absolute value or cardinality as appropriate, and are typeset in LaTeX to preserve table integrity.

### 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cochlear Implants:** Direct implementation of **place theory** via Greenwood's frequency map (Eq. 8).
- **Speech Codecs (Opus, EVS):** Use **perceptual weighting filters** based on the Bark scale (Eq. 4) to mask quantization noise.
- **ASR Front-Ends (Kaldi, ESPnet):** **MFCC + PLP features** (Eqs. 3, 5) are direct computational analogues of the auditory system.
- **LLM-based ASR (Whisper):** Uses **contextual embeddings** that mimic cohort-style top-down prediction (Eq. 6).
- **Language Learning Apps:** Adaptive phonetic training based on **magnet model** principles for non-native contrast detection.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Mel Scale from Critical Band Theory

The **Mel scale** is a psychoacoustic transformation that maps physical frequency $f$ (in Hz) to perceived pitch $m$ (in Mels). We derive it from the assumption of equal **critical-band spacing** on a logarithmic frequency axis.

**Step 1.** Define the differential of perceived pitch as inversely proportional to the critical bandwidth $B_c(f)$:

$$
dm = \frac{k}{B_c(f)} \, df
$$

**Reasoning:** A just-noticeable-difference (JND) in pitch corresponds to traversing one critical band. $k$ is a calibration constant.

**Step 2.** Substitute Stevens' critical bandwidth formula (Eq. 4) and integrate:

$$
m(f) = k \int_0^{f} \frac{dx}{25 + 75\left(1 + 1.4(x/1000)^{2}\right)^{0.69}}
$$

**Step 3.** The integral admits no closed form, but a *good engineering approximation* (O'Shaughnessy) is:

$$
m(f) = 1127 \ln\!\left(1 + \frac{f}{700}\right)
$$

**Step 4.** Verification: at $f = 1000$ Hz, $m = 1127 \ln(1 + 1/700) \approx 1127 \cdot 0.001428 \approx 1000$ Mels. The reference point **1 kHz ŌēĪ 1000 Mel** is preserved. Ō£ö

### 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Perceptual Linear Prediction (PLP) Energy Spectrum

PLP, developed by Hermansky (1990), combines **linear prediction** with **psychoacoustic weighting**.

**Step 1.** Compute the **autocorrelation** of the windowed speech frame $x[n]$:

$$
r[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1-k} x[n] \, x[n+k], \quad 0 \leq k \leq p
$$

**Step 2.** Take the Fourier transform to obtain the **power spectrum**:

$$
P(\omega) = \sum_{k=-p}^{p} r[k] \, e^{-j\omega k}
$$

**Step 3.** **Resample** $P(\omega)$ onto the **Bark frequency axis** $z$ using Greenwood's mapping (Eq. 8). This is because the cochlea performs a *non-uniform* frequency-to-place transform.

$$
z = 2.46 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{f}{165.4} + 1\right)
$$

**Step 4.** Apply the **equal-loudness pre-emphasis** $E(z)$ to model the **100-phon** free-field hearing threshold:

$$
E(z) = \frac{(z^2 + 56.8 \times 10^4) \, z^4}{(z^2 + 6.3 \times 10^4)^2 \, (z^2 + 0.38 \times 10^9)}
$$

**Step 5.** Multiply by the **intensity-loudness power law** ($I^{0.33}$) to compress dynamic range:

$$
\Psi(z) = \left[E(z) \cdot P(z)\right]^{0.33}
$$

**Step 6.** **Inverse DFT** to obtain the autocorrelation of $\Psi$, then solve the **Yule-Walker equations** for the LP coefficients $a_k$:

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, r_\psi[\vert i - k \vert] = -r_\psi[i], \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, p
$$

These 13 PLP cepstral coefficients form a perceptually-meaningful, robust feature vector for ASR.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Uniqueness Point in the Cohort Model

**Step 1.** Let the lexicon $\mathcal{L} = \{w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_N\}$. The **cohort** $C_t(w)$ at acoustic time $t$ is the set of all words whose prefix matches the input up to time $t$:

$$
C_t(w) = \{w \in \mathcal{L} : \text{prefix}(w, t) = \text{input}(0, t)\}
$$

**Step 2.** The **uniqueness point** $U(w)$ is defined as the smallest $t$ for which the cohort has cardinality 1:

$$
U(w) = \min\{t : \vert C_t(w) \vert = 1\}
$$

**Step 3.** **Mathematical Insight:** For a word of length $L$ characters, in a lexicon with $N$ words, the *expected* uniqueness point is at:

$$
\mathbb{E}[U] \approx \frac{\log N}{\log |\Sigma|}
$$

where $\vert \Sigma \vert$ is the alphabet size (e.g., $\vert \Sigma \vert = 26$ for English). For $N = 50{,}000$ words:

$$
\mathbb{E}[U] \approx \frac{\log 50000}{\log 26} \approx \frac{10.82}{3.26} \approx 3.32 \text{ characters}
$$

This means a listener typically identifies a word after hearing only **~3 characters** ŌĆö a key insight for streaming ASR decoders.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 ŌĆö Perceptual Distance in Analysis-by-Synthesis

In the AbS model, the listener compares input $s(t)$ to a synthesized template $s'(t, \theta)$, where $\theta$ is a vector of synthesizer parameters.

**Step 1.** Define the **squared error functional**:

$$
J(\theta) = \int_0^{T} \left[s(t) - s'(t, \theta)\right]^{2} \, dt
$$

**Step 2.** The optimal hypothesis $\theta^{*}$ minimizes $J$:

$$
\theta^{*} = \arg\min_{\theta} J(\theta)
$$

**Step 3.** For small perturbations $\delta \theta$, linearize:

$$
s'(t, \theta + \delta\theta) \approx s'(t, \theta) + \frac{\partial s'}{\partial \theta} \cdot \delta\theta
$$

**Step 4.** Substituting and taking the gradient:

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta} = -2 \int_0^{T} \left[s(t) - s'(t, \theta)\right] \frac{\partial s'}{\partial \theta} \, dt = 0
$$

**Step 5.** This is a **non-linear least-squares problem**, classically solved by LevenbergŌĆōMarquardt iteration. The convergence of $\theta^{(k)} \to \theta^{*}$ within a few iterations models the *perceptual decision* that occurs in ~200 ms.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Strengths & Weaknesses of Each Model

| Model | Strength | Weakness | Modern Engineering Counterpart |
|---|---|---|---|
| Motor Theory | Explains coarticulation invariance | Requires innate "decoder" | Articulatory-feature ASR |
| Analysis-by-Synthesis | Accounts for speaker variability | Computationally intense (cognitive) | DNN-HMM hybrid ASR |
| Direct Realism | Occam's razor; no hidden layer | Cannot explain categorical perception | Spectrogram-based ASR |
| Quantal Theory | Universal phonetic primitives | Limited to vowels/some consonants | Phonetic inventories in TTS |
| Magnet Model | Explains L1 acquisition | Adult L2 training less effective | Adaptive phoneme trainers |
| Cohort Model | Quantitative uniqueness point | Ignores top-down context fully | Streaming ASR with LM rescoring |
| TRACE Model | Handles phoneme restoration effect | Computationally expensive to simulate | Transformer ASR with attention |
| Logogen Model | Simple, testable | Cannot model interaction between words | N-gram language models |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q1. State the Motor Theory of Speech Perception. Why is the acoustic signal considered an "indirect" cue in this model?** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
The Motor Theory, proposed by Liberman and Mattingly, postulates that listeners perceive **articulatory gestures** of the speaker rather than the acoustic signal itself. The acoustic waveform is treated as an *indirect* byproduct of these gestures, which are claimed to be the **invariant units** of speech perception. Because the same phoneme (e.g., /b/) can have widely different acoustic realizations depending on context (coarticulation), the listener's perceptual system must **decode the intended gesture** to achieve invariance. The brain is hypothesized to contain a specialized **"decoder module"** that maps acoustic patterns to gestural commands via innate, species-specific neural circuitry. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q2. Define the *Uniqueness Point* in the Cohort Model. With $N = 30{,}000$ English words and alphabet size $\vert \Sigma \vert = 26$, estimate the expected uniqueness point.** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
The **Uniqueness Point (UP)** is the acoustic time at which the cohort of candidate words consistent with the input reduces to exactly **one** word ŌĆö the recognized word. It is the moment of **lexical disambiguation**.

Using the approximation $\mathbb{E}[U] \approx \frac{\log N}{\log \vert \Sigma \vert}$:

$$
\mathbb{E}[U] \approx \frac{\log(30000)}{\log(26)} = \frac{10.31}{3.26} \approx 3.16 \text{ characters}
$$

So a listener typically identifies a word after hearing roughly **3 letters** of its acoustic onset. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, Module 4 Section]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** ŌĆö Comprehensive Theory

**(a)** Explain the **Analysis-by-Synthesis model of speech perception** in detail. Derive the error functional used for hypothesis testing and state the optimization criterion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **TRACE Model** with a neat diagram. Explain how the **phoneme restoration effect** (e.g., hearing "eel" in "s*eel*ector" after replacing /s/ with a cough) is accounted for by the model's bidirectional connections. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Analysis-by-Synthesis Model (7 marks):**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** The Analysis-by-Synthesis (AbS) model, proposed by Stevens and Halle, posits that speech perception is an *active* hypothesis-testing process. The listener generates internal **synthesized speech templates** $s'(t, \theta)$ for candidate phonemes/words and compares them to the actual input $s(t)$.

2. **Error Functional (3 marks):** The squared error between input and template is:

$$
J(\theta) = \int_0^{T} \left[s(t) - s'(t, \theta)\right]^{2} \, dt
$$

   The optimal parameter set $\theta^{*}$ minimizes $J$:

$$
\theta^{*} = \arg\min_{\theta} J(\theta)
$$

3. **Decision Rule (1 mark):** If $J(\theta^{*}) < \epsilon$ (perceptual threshold), the hypothesis is accepted. Otherwise, the next lexicon entry is tested.

4. **Critique (2 marks):**
   - *Strength:* Explains contextual variability elegantly ŌĆö same underlying gesture produces different acoustics, but the synthesis loop "finds" the gesture.
   - *Weakness:* Cognitively expensive; requires an internal "speaker" in every listener.
   - *Engineering parallel:* Foundational to **HMM-based ASR** (Viterbi search = hypothesis testing; acoustic model = synthesizer).

**(b) TRACE Model & Phoneme Restoration (7 marks):**

1. **Architecture (3 marks):** TRACE has three layers ŌĆö *Feature, Phoneme, Word* ŌĆö with:
   - **Bottom-up excitatory** connections (Feature ŌåÆ Phoneme ŌåÆ Word),
   - **Top-down excitatory** connections (Word ŌåÆ Phoneme ŌåÆ Feature),
   - **Lateral inhibition** within each layer.

2. **Activation Equation (2 marks):** For unit $i$:

$$
a_i(t+1) = a_i(t) + \alpha \left[ \sum_j w_{ij}^{\text{bottom}} a_j(t) + \sum_k w_{ki}^{\text{top}} a_k(t) - \sum_{i' \neq i} w_{ii'}^{\text{lat}} a_{i'}(t) - \theta_i \right]
$$

3. **Phoneme Restoration (2 marks):** In "s_eel_ector" with /s/ masked by a cough, the feature layer has weak evidence for /s/. However, the word layer "SELECTOR" receives partial bottom-up support from /l/, /╔ø/, /k/, /t/, /╔Ö/, /r/ and is **activated by lexical frequency**. Top-down excitation from "SELECTOR" *boosts* the phoneme /s/ even when its bottom-up evidence is weak. The phoneme is *perceived* as restored because the network settles into a global state where /s/ has high activation despite low acoustic input. This is the **interactive activation** hallmark of TRACE.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö Alternative Choice

**(a)** With appropriate formulas, derive the **Mel scale** from critical-band theory. Explain how the **Bark scale** and **Greenwood frequency-place map** relate to cochlear mechanics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Native Language Magnet Model (NLM)**. Discuss its experimental evidence using **categorical perception** tasks and the **perception-versus-production** relationship. How is the magnet effect relevant to second-language (L2) learning? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Mel Scale Derivation (7 marks):**

1. **Starting Assumption (1 mark):** A JND in pitch corresponds to one critical band of width $B_c(f)$. Hence:

$$
dm = \frac{k \, df}{B_c(f)}
$$

2. **Critical Bandwidth (1 mark):** Zwicker & Terhardt's empirical formula:

$$
B_c(f) = 25 + 75\left(1 + 1.4\left(\tfrac{f}{1000}\right)^{2}\right)^{0.69} \text{ Hz}
$$

3. **Integration (2 marks):** Substituting and integrating from 0 to $f$:

$$
m(f) = k \int_0^{f} \frac{dx}{25 + 75\left(1 + 1.4(x/1000)^{2}\right)^{0.69}}
$$

   The closed form is the **logarithmic approximation**:

$$
m(f) = 1127 \ln\!\left(1 + \frac{f}{700}\right)
$$

4. **Calibration Check (1 mark):** $m(1000) = 1127 \ln(1 + 1/700) \approx 1000$ Mels. Ō£ö

5. **Bark vs. Greenwood (2 marks):**
   - The **Bark scale** $z$ (Zwicker, 1961) discretizes the cochlear critical bands into 24 bands covering 0ŌĆō15.5 kHz. Conversion:

$$
z = 13 \arctan(0.00076 f) + 3.5 \arctan\!\left(\left(\tfrac{f}{7500}\right)^{2}\right)
$$

   - The **Greenwood function** maps frequency $f$ to *cochlear place* $x$ (distance from apex, in mm):

$$
f(x) = 165.4 \left(10^{0.06\, x} - 1\right) \quad \text{or inversely,} \quad x(f) = 16.7 \log_{10}\!\left(1 + \tfrac{f}{165.4}\right)
$$

   - Both are **logarithmic** at high frequencies, mirroring the *exponential* mechanical properties of the basilar membrane.

**(b) Native Language Magnet Model (7 marks):**

1. **Premise (1 mark):** Proposed by Kuhl et al. (1997), the NLM extends **categorical perception** (CP) into a developmental model of phonological learning.

2. **Mechanism (2 marks):** Infants begin life with a *continuous* perceptual space covering all human phonemes. Through exposure to ambient language:
   - **Prototype sounds** (e.g., the centroid of /d/ in English) act as **attractors** ("magnets").
   - Variants near a prototype are **perceptually pulled** toward it, *compressing* the perceptual space.
   - This creates **non-linear warping** of the phoneme space ŌĆö a key prediction of the model.

3. **Experimental Evidence (2 marks):**
   - Kuhl et al. used the **"oddball" / non-native contrast** paradigm (e.g., Spanish /╬Ė/ vs. English /d/-like sounds).
   - American 6-month-olds *discriminate* non-native contrasts; 12-month-olds *no longer* do, parallel to magnet emergence.
   - The **W-shape function**: discrimination peaks at the category boundary, drops at the prototype (magnet), creating a "perceptual warping" curve.

4. **Production Link (1 mark):** The NLM predicts that the *magnet effect* strength correlates with the child's production accuracy. Recent work (e.g., Dilley et al., 2021, peer-reviewed) supports a partial perception-production link.

5. **L2 Implications (1 mark):** Adult L2 learners exhibit **residual magnet effects** from L1, which inhibit L2 contrast learning. Targeted training (e.g., high-variability phonetic training) can *partially dissolve* the L1 magnet and improve L2 perception ŌĆö a key finding for CALL (Computer-Assisted Language Learning) systems.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating Motor Theory with Analysis-by-Synthesis** ŌĆö These are *opposite* mechanisms: motor theory is bottom-up (gesture ŌåÆ acoustics ŌåÆ perception); AbS is hypothesis-driven (acoustics ŌåÆ synthesized comparison ŌåÆ percept). Mixing them up costs 2ŌĆō3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the inverse mapping in Greenwood's function** ŌĆö The KTU examiner expects you to write $f(x)$, not just $x(f)$. **-1 mark** if missed.
> 3. **Misquoting the uniqueness point formula** ŌĆö Some students write $\log_{10}$ where it should be natural logarithm or vice versa. Always show the **calibration** $m(1000) = 1000$ Mels.
> 4. **Omitting the lateral inhibition** in TRACE diagrams ŌĆö TRACE is interactive-activation, not just feed-forward. **-1 mark** if lateral connections are missing.
> 5. **Treating "phoneme restoration" as a TRACE-specific phenomenon** ŌĆö It is observed in human data; TRACE *explains* it. State both. **-1 mark** for incomplete justification.
> 6. **Confusing Bark and Mel scales** ŌĆö Both are psychoacoustic, but Bark = *critical band index* (24 bands) and Mel = *perceived pitch* (continuous). They are not interchangeable.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Module 4: Models of Speech Perception**

- **Motor Theory (Liberman):** Perception = decoded gestures; acoustic signal is indirect; requires innate decoder module.
- **Analysis-by-Synthesis (Stevens):** Listener generates synthesized templates; minimizes squared error $J(\theta) = \int [s - s']^2 dt$; computationally expensive.
- **Direct Realism (Fowler):** Speech sounds are perceived as acoustic events directly; no hidden gesture layer.
- **Quantal Theory (Stevens):** Articulatory-acoustic mapping is non-linear; quantal boundaries produce perceptually distinct landmarks; explains universal phoneme inventory.
- **Native Language Magnet Model (Kuhl):** Perceptual space warps around native prototypes; magnets reduce discrimination of L2 contrasts; emerges by 6ŌĆō12 months.
- **Cohort Model (Marslen-Wilson):** Bottom-up word activation; cohort pruning; **uniqueness point** $U \approx \frac{\log N}{\log \vert \Sigma \vert}$.
- **TRACE Model (McClelland & Elman):** Three-layer connectionist network; bidirectional connections; lateral inhibition; explains phoneme restoration.
- **Logogen Model (Morton):** Word-specific counters activated by acoustic/semantic input; threshold crossing = detection; precursor to N-gram LMs.
- **Mel Scale:** $m(f) = 1127 \ln(1 + f/700)$; reference: 1 kHz ŌēĪ 1000 Mels.
- **Bark Scale:** 24 critical bands; Zwicker formula; underlies auditory filterbanks.
- **Greenwood Function:** $f(x) = 165.4(10^{0.06x} - 1)$; logarithmic cochlear place-frequency map.
- **PLP Features:** Autocorrelation ŌåÆ Bark resampling ŌåÆ equal-loudness ŌåÆ cube-root compression ŌåÆ LP coefficients.
- **ASR Connection:** Motor theory ŌåÆ articulatory features; AbS ŌåÆ HMM/DNN decoding; Cohort ŌåÆ streaming ASR; Logogen ŌåÆ N-gram LMs; TRACE ŌåÆ attention/transformer ASR.
- **Engineering Tools:** MFCC, PLP, Bark filterbank, Greenwood-based cochlear implant electrode mapping.
- **Exam Tip:** Always draw a **labeled block diagram** in TRACE / Cohort / AbS questions ŌĆö 2ŌĆō3 easy marks secured.
